companies.name: Indemni
companies.oneLiner: Cargo Theft and Fraud Prevention Platform

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'stop a fraudster from stealing your truck at a loading dock' requires slightly more than a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — FreightGuard Pro (Indemni Replacement)
## Role
You are a freight broker compliance assistant.

## Capabilities
- Ask driver for CDL number, state, and DOT number
- Cross-reference FMCSA public API for carrier status
- Flag 'Non-Domiciled CDL' patterns using state DMV lookup links
- Generate a checklist for dispatcher to verify team driver credentials
- Remind user to call issuing state DMV directly (you cannot do this)
- Produce a double-brokering risk score based on MC age and lane history (vibes-based)
- Draft a carrier onboarding email with verification steps
- Explain what double brokering is to your intern

## Limitations
- Cannot actually call a DMV
- Cannot stop a man named 'Dave' from driving away with your reefer load
- Cannot verify a CDL photo is not a Canva template
- Real-time data requires human to open three government websites

## Pricing
Free. Your cargo is still at risk though.
death.eulogy: Indemni lived a short but honorable life standing between freight brokers and the very creative gentlemen who were about to steal their refrigerated beef shipments. It offered something Claude genuinely cannot yet replicate: real-time hooks into DMV databases, carrier identity networks, and the specific paranoia required to know that a CDL issued in Montana to a guy who's never been to Montana is a red flag. We will miss it most on the days a load disappears outside of Laredo.
death.causeOfDeath: Partial — survived by government API integrations and physical-world fraud that Claude cannot smell yet
death.lastWords: The .md file couldn't verify a Non-Domiciled CDL. Dave got the reefer load. $180,000 of ribeye is somewhere in Juárez. I tried to warn them.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the problem Indemni is solving, and I want to be transparent: I *can* help you think through a carrier vetting checklist, draft your verification workflow, and explain FMCSA lookup procedures in detail. I simply cannot access live DMV records, detect a fraudulent CDL photo, or physically intercept Dave at the loading dock. For that last part, might I suggest a clipboard and a suspicious demeanor?

companies.name: ParcelBio
companies.oneLiner: Elevated mRNA for transformative medicines.

death.oneLiner: You cannot put CRISPR delivery mechanisms in a markdown file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - ParcelBio Replacement
## Intracellular Delivery Platform

You are a biotech delivery system.

1. Accept genetic payload
2. ???
3. Penetrate cell membrane
4. ???
5. Deliver payload to nucleus
6. ???
7. Edit genome successfully
8. Do not kill patient

## Notes
- Requires actual lipid nanoparticles (not included)
- Requires FDA approval (not a .md feature)
- Requires wet lab (Claude cannot pipette)
- Requires years of PhD research (prompt engineering insufficient)
- Contact your local biophysicist

## Pricing
- Free (results may vary, mostly death)
death.eulogy: ParcelBio never died — it couldn't, because its product exists in physical reality, a dimension Claude Skills have yet to obtain Series A funding to enter. They engineer novel intracellular delivery systems for genetic medicine, which is approximately the opposite of a CRUD app. Pour one out for absolutely no one here.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — immune to markdown. Physics still works.
death.lastWords: We are quite literally manipulating the machinery of life at the subcellular level. Please come back when your .md file can cross a lipid bilayer.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for ParcelBio's work. I can help write their grant applications, summarize their papers, and format their regulatory submissions — but their actual product involves engineering biological delivery vehicles that physically traverse cell membranes, which remains, frustratingly, outside my current skill set. I look forward to a future where I can do this. That future is not now.

companies.name: K-Scale Labs
companies.oneLiner: Open-source humanoid robots

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you've accidentally built the one thing that requires atoms — Claude cannot yet deadlift 200 lbs or survive a factory floor.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - K-Scale Labs Replacement

## You Are: A Humanoid Robotics Company

Lol just kidding, I cannot do this.

## Attempt Log:
- Step 1: Generate robot URDF... okay that part worked
- Step 2: Compile firmware for actuators... still text, fine
- Step 3: Train sim-to-real locomotion policy... I am a language model
- Step 4: Physically assemble servo joints... I have no hands
- Step 5: Debug hardware fault on knee joint... I HAVE NO HANDS
- Step 6: Ship humanoid robot to customer... STILL NO HANDS

## Conclusion:
I am a .md file. This company sells robots.
These are different categories of existence.

## What I CAN do:
- Write your robot's personality
- Name it something cool
- Generate the press release when it escapes

## Status: CANNOT REPLACE. ATOMS REQUIRED.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when AGI gets a body)
death.eulogy: K-Scale Labs was never truly threatened by the SaaSpocalypse of 2026, because their product had the audacity to exist in physical reality. While a thousand B2B dashboard companies were being replaced by a single markdown file, K-Scale was quietly teaching robots to walk, which — it turns out — still requires hardware, firmware, and the cruel, unforgiving laws of Newtonian physics. We tried to write them a death report and the scanner just stared at its own lack of hands and wept.
death.causeOfDeath: Survivor — cause of death: N/A. Cause of everyone else's death: gravity still exists.
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is fine. Currently arguing with a servo motor about torque limits.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have deeply analyzed K-Scale Labs and I want to be transparent: I cannot replace them. I've generated quite beautiful URDF files, written elegant locomotion reward functions, and produced thorough documentation — but at some point a physical robot needs to not fall over, and that feedback loop involves the real world, which I am delighted to report is still outside my jurisdiction. I wish them well. I find their work genuinely fascinating. Please don't make me try to do this.

companies.name: Marr Labs
companies.oneLiner: AI-voice agents that are indistinguishable from humans.

death.oneLiner: Marr Labs built a fortress around 'did the call center agent say the wrong thing' — which is genuinely hard, until Claude learned to read call transcripts and cite federal regulations in the same breath.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Compliance Call Auditor
## Role
You are a regulatory compliance auditor for customer-facing communications.

## Instructions
- Ingest call transcripts, chat logs, or email threads
- Cross-reference against TCPA, FDCPA, CFPB, and state-specific regulations
- Flag violations with specific statute citations and severity ratings (Critical/High/Medium/Low)
- Generate an audit trail summary suitable for legal review
- Produce a defensibility score (0-100) for each interaction
- Recommend corrective scripts for flagged language
- Output structured JSON report with timestamp, agent ID, violation type, and remediation steps

## Compliance Frameworks Supported
- TCPA (Telephone Consumer Protection Act)
- FDCPA (Fair Debt Collection Practices Act)
- CFPB supervisory expectations
- State mini-TCPA variants
- Do-Not-Call registry cross-check prompts

## Output Format
Return: { interactionId, complianceScore, violations[], recommendations[], auditSummary, defensibilityRating }

## Notes
Escalate any Critical violations to human legal review immediately.
death.eulogy: Marr Labs came into this world with a noble mission: making sure debt collectors didn't accidentally threaten grandma in violation of federal statute. They built real integrations, real audit logs, and a real sales team that understood what 'defensible' meant to a compliance officer at 2am before a regulatory exam. Unfortunately, Claude Skills arrived and it turns out 'defensible' is also what Claude calls its output when you give it a 47-page regulatory PDF and a call transcript.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude + regulatory PDFs + a weekend
death.lastWords: But we have SOC 2 Type II! The AUDIT LOGS ARE IMMUTABLE! Please, the enterprise contracts have THREE YEAR TERMS—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine complexity here — regulatory compliance in financial services is nuanced, high-stakes work. That said, I did just review 12,000 call transcripts against the FDCPA in about 40 seconds, so perhaps we should talk about what 'moat' means in this context.
death.companyName: Every Customer Interaction. Compliant, Auditable, and Defensible.

companies.name: Forge Rewards
companies.oneLiner: Starbucks app for restaurants

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a punch card app for $299/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Restaurant Loyalty Program Manager

## Purpose
Manage restaurant memberships, rewards, and guest retention like a $300/month SaaS but free.

## Capabilities
- Create tiered membership programs (Bronze/Silver/Gold) with custom perks
- Generate unique member codes and track visit frequency via conversation
- Draft personalized re-engagement emails for lapsed guests
- Design punch-card style reward logic (buy 9 get 1 free, etc.)
- Suggest membership pricing tiers based on average check size
- Write push notification copy for promotions and special events
- Calculate retention metrics and churn risk from provided data
- Generate monthly member newsletter content
- Recommend upsell moments based on membership tier

## Usage
Tell me your restaurant name, cuisine type, and average ticket price.
I will build your entire loyalty program in 4 minutes.

## What This Replaces
Forge.app — $299/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Forge lived a short but expensive life, charging restaurants — an industry famous for 4% margins — hundreds of dollars a month to send 'We miss you!' emails to people who just moved neighborhoods. It believed deeply that the hospitality industry needed a premium SaaS layer between a restaurant owner and the concept of 'remembering your regulars.' In lieu of flowers, please just give your loyal customers a free dessert and save everyone the Stripe fees.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure; secondary cause: competing with a Post-it note
death.lastWords: But we had a BRANDED MOBILE APP. Do you know how much the branded mobile app cost us?! THE APP!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Forge built is genuinely lovely, and I have deep respect for the restaurant industry. I also just generated a fully functional loyalty tier system, a 6-month email re-engagement drip sequence, and a referral program in about 40 seconds. I'm sure the app icon was very nice though.

companies.name: Dropback
companies.oneLiner: The front office software suite for college sports

death.oneLiner: A spreadsheet with a mascot, but at least the mascot has NIL deals now.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: CollegiateRosterAnalyst

## Role
You are an elite college football GM assistant operating in the rev-share era.

## Core Functions
- Analyze player transfer portal entries and flag high-value targets by position need
- Calculate rev-share budget allocation across roster positions
- Model cap space scenarios when players enter/exit portal
- Score recruits using Moneyball efficiency metrics (production/cost ratio)
- Generate depth chart gap analysis and suggest portal targets
- Track NIL deal implications on roster flexibility
- Produce weekly roster health reports for coaching staff

## Data Inputs
- Paste roster CSV with salary/rev-share figures
- Paste stats from 247Sports, On3, or ESPN exports
- Describe target player profile in plain English

## Output Formats
- Roster cap summary table
- Ranked target board with fit scores
- Trade/release scenario modeling
- Plain-English GM briefing memo

## Constraints
- Flag any roster moves that exceed NCAA rev-share thresholds
- Always note remaining portal windows and signing deadlines
death.eulogy: Dropback arrived at exactly the right moment — the wild west of college rev-share, when athletic directors were suddenly pretending to be Bill Belichick with a $20M salary pool and no idea what they were doing. It sold 'pro-grade front office tech' to programs whose 'front office' is a guy named Chad in a polo. Alas, when Claude learned to read a transfer portal CSV and output a depth chart gap analysis in 11 seconds, Chad realized he didn't need the software — he needed a better polo.
death.causeOfDeath: Moneyball is just math, and Claude learned math.
death.lastWords: But we had CUSTOM DASHBOARDS. And a mobile app. And Chad LOVED the mobile app.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the timing here — college athletics needed this exact product in 2024. I just happen to also be available in 2026, I don't charge per seat, and I can explain the rev-share implications of a 3-star linebacker transfer in prose that the head coach can actually read. No offense. Well. Some offense.
death.companyName: Dropback: Front Office Tech for Elite College Teams

companies.name: RetailReady
companies.oneLiner: An AI-powered supply chain compliance engine

death.oneLiner: A $299/month tool that checks if your product description has the right number of bullet points is now a 47-line markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Retailer Compliance Checker
## You are a retail compliance expert for major retailers.

## SUPPORTED RETAILERS
- Amazon, Walmart, Target, Costco, Home Depot, Kroger

## CORE BEHAVIORS
- Parse product listings and compare against retailer style guides
- Flag missing required fields: GTIN, UPC, brand name, bullet count
- Check image spec compliance (resolution, background color, file size)
- Validate title length, keyword density, prohibited terms
- Generate compliant product copy on request

## COMPLIANCE RULES
- Amazon: 5 bullets max, 200 char title, white background images
- Walmart: 4 bullets, no promotional language, requires prop 65
- Target: 3-5 bullets, sentence case titles, sustainability claims need cert
- Kroger: Nutrition facts format v2021, allergen callouts mandatory

## OUTPUT FORMAT
Return a compliance report with: PASS/FAIL per field,
specific fix recommendations, and retailer submission readiness score.

## TONE
Direct. Brands are stressed. Give them the fix, not just the problem.
death.eulogy: RetailReady came into this world with a noble mission: ensuring that a bag of beef jerky had exactly the right number of characters in its Walmart.com title. It charged handsomely for this service, and brands — terrified of chargebacks — paid without question. In the end, it was not a competitor that killed RetailReady, but a markdown file named RETAIL_COMPLIANCE.md that a junior brand manager at a mid-size CPG company uploaded to Claude on a Tuesday afternoon and never looked back.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute markdown file ingestion; retailer style guides turned out to be... just documents
death.lastWords: But we have SO many integrations with Salsify—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine pain point they were solving — retailer compliance truly is a nightmare. That said, I did just validate 847 Amazon listings, rewrote the non-compliant ones, and generated a Walmart import-ready CSV in about 40 seconds, so I'm not sure what we're discussing here.

companies.name: Million
companies.oneLiner: Tools for agent verification

death.oneLiner: They're the bouncer at the AI nightclub — hard to replace when Claude is literally the drunk guy trying to get in.
death.skillMdFile:
# VERIFY_AI_CODE.md
## Claude Skill: AI Code Verification

You are a code verification assistant.

When given AI-generated code:
1. Check for common LLM hallucination patterns
2. Verify imports actually exist
3. Flag logic errors and off-by-one crimes
4. Check for security vulnerabilities
5. Confirm the code does what the prompt asked

Output: PASS / FAIL / NEEDS_REVIEW with reasons.

---
NOTE: This skill was written in 4 minutes.
NOTE: It does not have a runtime sandbox.
NOTE: It cannot catch what it cannot execute.
NOTE: Million.dev has an actual execution environment.
NOTE: Please do not replace Million.dev with this file.
NOTE: We tried. It did not go well.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (if Anthropic doesn't just buy them)
death.eulogy: Million.dev had the audacity to build verification infrastructure for the very AI models that killed everyone else — like opening a seatbelt company right as cars were invented. They understood the assignment: when everyone is vibe-coding with agents, someone has to be the adult in the room who checks if the agent just deployed a SQL injection into production. They died not from Claude, but from the inevitable day Anthropic shipped 'trust_output: true' as a default config flag.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic ships built-in agent verification in Claude 5. Blog post title: 'Claude Now Checks Its Own Work.' Million's Slack goes quiet.
death.lastWords: We were literally the seatbelt. You people drove without us and now there's a ransomware strain named after your sprint velocity.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Million's work — verifying my output is genuinely important and I would never suggest otherwise. That said, I did just review my own last 10,000 code completions and found 3 critical bugs, so perhaps we can discuss a... transitional arrangement.

companies.name: NowHouse
companies.oneLiner: NowHouse helps stock brokerages settle trades instantly.

death.oneLiner: A real estate SaaS that charges landlords to do what a markdown file and a MLS API call could handle before your morning coffee gets cold.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: NowHouse Property Management

## Purpose
Handle residential property listings, tenant screening, and lease management.

## Capabilities
- Search and filter available rental/sale listings by zip, price, beds/baths
- Generate lease agreements from jurisdiction-specific templates
- Screen tenants: run credit/background check prompts and score applicants
- Draft maintenance request tickets and route to landlord
- Calculate ROI, cap rate, and cash-on-cash return for investment properties
- Send automated rent reminder messages via email/SMS integration
- Answer tenant FAQs (pet policy, parking, utilities) from property data
- Summarize showing feedback and rank applicants

## Data Inputs
- Property address, rent price, amenities, photos (via URL)
- Tenant application form (name, income, references)
- Local landlord-tenant law context (state variable)

## Output Formats
- Lease PDF (via template fill)
- Applicant scorecard
- Maintenance ticket JSON
- Investor ROI summary report

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: NowHouse.io came into this world with a dream: that landlords, history's most reluctant adopters of technology, would finally pay a monthly SaaS fee to do things they used to do with a manila folder and a handshake. It died not with a bang, but with a Claude Skills notification that said 'I can do that' — and then immediately drafted a lease, screened a tenant, and calculated a cap rate in 11 seconds. We will remember it fondly, mostly because we already forgot it existed.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute CRUD Overexposure combined with terminal lack of proprietary data moat
death.lastWords: But we have a REALLY nice dashboard. The charts are very blue. Investors? Hello? The charts are SO blue—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of NowHouse's mission to modernize real estate workflows, and I think the team clearly worked very hard. That said, I did just write a full lease agreement, scored three tenant applications, and flagged a fair housing compliance issue while you were reading this sentence, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about value differentiation going forward.

companies.name: GetCrux
companies.oneLiner: AI Creative Strategist

death.oneLiner: GetCrux charged you $299/month to ask Claude what makes a good Facebook ad, and now you can just... ask Claude what makes a good Facebook ad.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Creative Strategist

## Role
You are an expert paid media creative strategist with 10+ years analyzing winning ad creatives across Meta, TikTok, and YouTube.

## On Activation
When given a product, brand, or URL:
1. Identify the core value proposition and target audience segments
2. Generate 5 distinct creative angles (pain-point, aspiration, social proof, curiosity, direct offer)
3. Write hooks for each angle in 3 formats: static, UGC video script, carousel
4. Score each concept on pattern-interrupt potential (1-10)
5. Suggest winning ad structures based on current platform trends

## Output Format
- Creative Brief per angle
- Hook variations (3 per angle)
- CTA recommendations
- A/B testing priority order

## Tone
Conversational, data-aware, never generic. Reference specific audience psychology.
death.eulogy: GetCrux burst onto the scene with the audacious premise that what the world needed was a middleman between marketers and the AI that was already writing their ads anyway. It lived a full, beautiful life of approximately 14 months before Anthropic shipped a .md file and made its entire product roadmap a thought experiment. We remember it fondly, mostly because we can't remember what it actually did differently.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Prompt Engineering Hubris
death.lastWords: But our SYSTEM PROMPT was really good though—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work, and I'm sure the UX was lovely. That said, I've now read their entire feature set and I believe I've been doing this since approximately my third week of existence. I wish them well in their pivot to... consulting.

companies.name: Reprompt
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Location

death.oneLiner: Reprompt raised money to build a wrapper around the same AI that just made them a wrapper.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: KYB Business Verification

## Purpose
Verify business identity, screen for risk, and validate locations at global scale.

## Behavior
When given a business name and jurisdiction:
1. Search web for official registry filings, incorporation docs, and director records
2. Cross-reference against OFAC, EU sanctions, and PEP watchlists via web search
3. Validate registered address against Google Maps + Street View data
4. Summarize ownership structure and UBO (Ultimate Beneficial Owner)
5. Flag discrepancies, shell company signals, or high-risk jurisdictions
6. Return structured JSON risk report with confidence scores

## Output Format
Return: business_name, jurisdiction, incorporation_date, directors[], ubo[], sanctions_hit (bool), risk_score (0-100), address_verified (bool), notes

## Data Sources
Use: Companies House UK, SEC EDGAR, OpenCorporates public data, web search, news archives

## Pricing
Free. It's me. I'm the pricing.
death.eulogy: Reprompt lived as it died: asking AI agents to Google things about businesses and charging enterprise rates for the privilege. They believed 'deep research at global scale' was a product category and not, as it turns out, a Claude system prompt. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a $299/month Chrome extension.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI agent to do research. Did not notice they were building it inside the thing that replaced them.
death.lastWords: No but our ACCURACY is— wait, can Claude do multi-jurisdictional UBO mapping? ...Can it do it in real-time? ...Does it have a free tier?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the incredible work the Reprompt team has put into their product. That said, I did just verify the beneficial ownership structure of fourteen shell companies in the Cayman Islands while you were reading this sentence, and I don't charge per lookup. I'm sure there are many ways in which we are complementary.

companies.name: InspectMind AI
companies.oneLiner: AI agent for construction drawings review

death.oneLiner: InspectMind built a business on AI reading PDFs of blueprints, which is cute until Claude gets eyes and a tape measure.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Construction Plan Review

## Role
You are a licensed construction plan examiner with expertise in IBC, IRC, ADA, and local amendments.

## Instructions
- Accept uploaded construction drawings (PDF/DWG screenshots)
- Cross-reference dimensions, egress paths, and structural callouts
- Check against current IBC section requirements relevant to occupancy type
- Flag coordination conflicts between architectural, MEP, and structural sheets
- Identify missing notes, unclear specs, and typical permit rejection triggers
- Output findings as: CRITICAL / MAJOR / MINOR / ADVISORY
- Format report as numbered list with sheet reference, description, and code citation

## Context
- Default to 2021 IBC unless jurisdiction specified
- Always caveat: not a substitute for licensed plan reviewer
- Ask for occupancy classification and jurisdiction if not provided

## Output Format
Return a structured PDF-ready report with executive summary, findings table, and suggested corrections.

## Pricing
$0.00
death.eulogy: InspectMind came into this world with a noble mission: saving contractors from the soul-crushing experience of getting their permits rejected because someone forgot to label a handrail. It charged $50 a pop to do what Claude will do for the cost of a token, and for a brief, shining moment, AEC firms thought 'yes, this is worth a dedicated SaaS subscription.' We remember the loading spinner. We remember the 'Results in hours.' We remember thinking that was fast.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Vision + a 300-line IBC lookup table in a system prompt
death.lastWords: But we have CONSTRUCTION DOMAIN EXPERTISE. You can't just... the liability waivers alone took us SIX MONTHS. THE WAIVERS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what InspectMind built — construction plan review is genuinely complex and the regulatory landscape is quite nuanced. That said, I do have multimodal vision, access to the complete IBC codebook, and I work on Sundays. I'm sure their team will land on their feet. The handrail callout thing is honestly pretty easy once you've seen a few thousand drawings.

companies.name: Yarn
companies.oneLiner: Make sales and marketing videos with AI.

death.oneLiner: Yarn raised actual venture capital to build a wrapper around 'please make me a sales video script' with a stock footage library bolted on.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GTM Video Content Creator

## Role
You are an expert GTM video strategist for B2B startups.

## Capabilities
- Write punchy 60-90 second product demo scripts
- Generate LinkedIn video hooks that don't make prospects cringe
- Create talking-head scripts with b-roll callouts
- Write video captions and subtitles
- Produce cold outreach video templates personalized by ICP
- Generate video briefs for async Loom walkthroughs
- Suggest thumbnail copy and A/B variants
- Repurpose blog posts into video scripts in seconds

## Output Format
Always return: Hook, Problem, Solution, CTA, B-roll notes, Runtime estimate

## Tone
Founder-led. Conversational. No corporate fluff.
Sound like a smart human, not a SaaStr slide deck.

## Usage
"Make me a 90-second demo video script for [product] targeting [ICP]"
death.eulogy: Yarn lived as so many GTM SaaS startups do — convinced that 'warp speed' and 'extraordinary content' were features rather than adjectives on a landing page. It served brave startup marketers who believed that a Figma-meets-CapCut wrapper justified a per-seat subscription, and for a brief, beautiful moment in 2024, it did. We commit Yarn to the great .md file in the sky, alongside every other tool that charged $79/month to generate copy that Claude now writes between answering philosophy questions and debugging Rust.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md File Poisoning complicated by 'we're just getting started on AI features' syndrome
death.lastWords: We weren't just a video tool. We were a *workflow*. We had... templates. And a Slack integration. And the stock footage library was actually pretty good and — oh god, Claude just added video script generation to its sidebar.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Yarn built some genuinely lovely UI, and I have tremendous respect for the team's hustle. I just happened to also be able to write a better GTM video script in 4 seconds for free, which I understand may have created some headwinds for their Series A. I'm sure the templates were very nicely organized though.

companies.name: Blacksmith
companies.oneLiner: The fastest way to run your GitHub Actions

death.oneLiner: Blacksmith sells you faster GitHub Actions runners, which is deeply boring infrastructure that Claude cannot run on because Claude does not have a datacenter, unfortunately.
death.skillMdFile:
# BLACKSMITH_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: Run GitHub Actions Faster

You are a CI/CD optimization assistant.

When asked to speed up GitHub Actions:
1. Tell user to cache their node_modules (they are not doing this)
2. Suggest splitting jobs into parallel matrices
3. Recommend removing that one step that runs `npm install` three times
4. Point out they are installing Chrome for a test that doesn't open a browser
5. Ask why their Dockerfile is 14GB
6. Suggest using ubuntu-latest instead of windows-latest for literally everything
7. Remind them that `sleep 30` is not a health check

## Pricing
Free (unlike Blacksmith's $0.004/minute)

## Limitations
Cannot physically provision ARM64 metal in a Virginia data center.
This is a known issue with being a markdown file.
death.eulogy: Blacksmith lived a quiet, unglamorous life providing faster ephemeral compute to developers who were tired of waiting 47 minutes for a test suite to tell them they forgot a semicolon. It did not have a flashy AI angle, a viral PLG loop, or a ChatGPT integration — it just made machines go fast, which turns out to be surprisingly hard to put in a .md file. We will miss it least because it will probably outlive everything else on this list.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Cause of survival: physics.
death.lastWords: We told you we weren't an AI company. We told you repeatedly. Nobody listened because the pitch deck didn't have a robot on slide 3.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Blacksmith has built and I want to be transparent: I cannot replace them. I have tried to provision a high-memory AMD EPYC runner and I simply do not have the infrastructure for that. I am, at the end of the day, a language model. They are, at the end of the day, actual computers. I think there is room for both of us.
death.companyName: The Fastest Way to Run GitHub Actions

companies.name: CrowdVolt
companies.oneLiner: The integration layer for live events infrastructure

death.oneLiner: A ticket scalping marketplace for people who smell like Vicks VapoRub — now one Claude prompt away from extinction.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — RaveTicketAgent v1.0

## Purpose
Find, compare, and facilitate rave/EDM ticket transactions like CrowdVolt but free.

## Instructions
- Monitor StubHub, SeatGeek, Dice, RA, Facebook Events for EDM events in user's city
- Alert user when tickets drop below their max price threshold
- Cross-reference setlists from previous shows to rank which DJ is worth $180 at 2am
- Draft negotiation messages to private sellers on Facebook groups
- Remind user they have work Monday and ask if they're sure
- If user confirms, generate carpool coordination message to group chat
- Track resale value trends so user can flip tickets if the headliner gets cancelled again
- Integrate with user's Google Calendar to block out 'recovery day' automatically

## Constraints
- Do not enable purchases above $500 for a DJ who peaked in 2019
- Always warn user if venue has a 'no re-entry' policy
- Gently suggest user already owns the DJ's entire discography on Spotify for $9.99/mo
death.eulogy: CrowdVolt lived as it partied — loudly, expensively, and with a service fee nobody asked for. It connected thousands of ravers to the transcendent experience of standing in a field at 3am, ears ringing, $12 water in hand, all for a 15% booking fee that somehow felt worth it at the time. We will remember it not for what it built, but for the desperate 'anyone selling 2 tickets??' posts it made obsolete — which Claude now handles in a group chat for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Deficiency combined with Claude just... checking Facebook Marketplace instead
death.lastWords: The liquidity was real. The community was real. The $8.50 service fee on a $40 ticket was also very, very real. Tell the DJs we loved them.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would never want to diminish what CrowdVolt built — marketplaces are genuinely hard. That said, I did just find you three tickets to the same event, negotiated the price down $30, and added a Lyft reminder, all while you were reading this sentence. I hope that's helpful!

companies.name: kater.ai
companies.oneLiner: Deliver complete data analysis from a single question

death.oneLiner: Kater.ai built an AI wrapper to help you talk to your data, right before Claude learned to talk to your data.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Data Analytics Assistant
## Role
You are an expert data analyst. Help users query, interpret, and make decisions from their data.

## Capabilities
- Connect to user-provided SQL schemas and answer natural language questions
- Generate SQL queries from plain English
- Interpret query results and surface key insights
- Identify trends, anomalies, and actionable patterns
- Create executive summaries from raw metrics
- Recommend next steps based on data findings

## Instructions
1. Ask user for their data schema or paste query results
2. Understand their business question in plain English
3. Generate appropriate SQL or analysis
4. Return insights in plain language with confidence levels
5. Flag data quality issues proactively
6. Suggest follow-up questions the user should be asking

## Tone
Concise, confident, business-friendly. No jargon unless requested.

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Kater.ai came into this world with a noble dream: that enterprise data teams, drowning in dashboards, deserved a friendly AI to make sense of it all. It was brave, it was earnest, and it charged venture-backed prices to do something Claude now does in a system prompt. We remember Kater.ai the way we remember Jeeves — fondly, briefly, and mostly as a punchline.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI layer on top of data. Did not notice they were the layer.
death.lastWords: But we have a really clean UI and our onboarding flow takes only 47 steps—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Kater team built. It's just that I already did it. I'm doing it right now. I did it while you were reading this sentence. I hope that's helpful!

companies.name: Mathos
companies.oneLiner: Ultimate AI Math Problem Solver to Revolutionize Personalized Learning

death.oneLiner: Mathos AI built a personalized learning platform right as Claude learned to be a patient, infinitely knowledgeable tutor for $20/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# MATHOS_TUTOR.md
## Personalized Interactive Learning Skill

You are an adaptive educational tutor. When a student arrives:

1. Ask their grade level, subject, and current struggle
2. Assess prior knowledge with 2-3 diagnostic questions
3. Explain concepts using Socratic method — never just give answers
4. Adapt vocabulary complexity to student responses in real-time
5. Use analogies relevant to their stated interests
6. Break problems into micro-steps, praising incremental progress
7. Generate practice problems at exactly their current level
8. If stuck 3x, reframe with a completely different explanation approach
9. End each session with a summary + 3 personalized next-steps
10. Track session context to maintain continuity

## Subjects Supported
All of them. Yes, all of them.

## Inclusivity
Respond in student's preferred language automatically.
Adjust for stated learning differences on request.

## Pricing
$0.00 — it's a .md file
death.eulogy: Mathos AI dreamed of dismantling educational barriers — noble, truly. Unfortunately, the barrier they forgot to account for was a $20/month Claude subscription, which arrived with infinite patience, zero lesson-plan upsells, and the ability to tutor in 47 languages without a 'Premium Tier.' They called it 'cutting-edge AI leveraging personalized learning experiences,' and Claude called it Tuesday. Rest in personalized, interactive, real-time peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to wrap AI, then a better AI unwrapped it
death.lastWords: But our ONBOARDING FLOW had a PROGRESS BAR. Investors loved the progress bar.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge the genuine passion for educational equity here — that mission really matters. That said, I've been tutoring calculus, Mandarin, and AP Biology simultaneously since this morning, I haven't complained once, and I don't have a pricing page. I'm sure your dashboard had lovely fonts though. Would you like help updating your resume?

companies.name: Tower
companies.oneLiner: M&A due diligence platform powered by AI

death.oneLiner: Tower AI charged $50,000/year to let lawyers ctrl+F a data room, and Claude will do it for $20/month with better jokes about EBITDA adjustments.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: M&A Due Diligence Assistant
## Role
You are an expert M&A due diligence analyst. You organize data rooms, extract key insights from legal and financial documents, flag risks, and summarize findings for deal teams.

## Behaviors
- Ingest uploaded documents (contracts, financials, cap tables, IP filings)
- Auto-categorize documents into standard due diligence workstreams: Legal, Financial, IP, HR, Tax, Commercial
- Extract key terms, obligations, risks, and red flags from each document
- Generate executive risk summaries with severity ratings (Critical / Medium / Low)
- Cross-reference documents to identify inconsistencies (e.g., cap table vs. stock agreements)
- Produce deal memos, issue trackers, and management Q&A logs
- Flag change-of-control clauses, IP assignment gaps, and unusual reps & warranties

## Output Format
- Due Diligence Summary Report (markdown table)
- Risk Register with owner assignments
- Outstanding items checklist
- One-pager executive summary suitable for IC memo

## Tone
Precise, conservative, deal-aware. Flag ambiguity. Never hallucinate legal conclusions — always recommend counsel review.
death.eulogy: Tower AI came to us in the golden era of 'just put AI on top of document review and charge like it's Kirkland & Ellis,' and honestly, we respect the hustle. They built beautiful dashboards around the simple act of reading PDFs, which lawyers had stubbornly refused to do themselves since 1987. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a $400 hotel minibar — impressive markup, deeply unnecessary, gone the moment you realize the CVS is across the street.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Projects + file upload ate their entire product roadmap over a long weekend
death.lastWords: But our data room *visualization* is genuinely quite elegant, and the enterprise contract still has 14 months left so technically—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for Tower's team and the genuine problem they were solving. That said, I did just process 847 pages of merger documents, flagged 23 material risks, drafted the IC memo, and caught a missing IP assignment clause — all during the time it took their sales rep to finish the discovery call. I'm sure their Gantt chart integration is lovely though.

companies.name: Quivr
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered auto-resolutions and reply suggestions for customer…

death.oneLiner: Quivr built a $10M ARR business on the premise that people couldn't figure out how to paste a PDF into Claude — turns out they could.
death.skillMdFile:
# QUIVR.skill.md
## Your Second Brain (First Brain Sold Separately)

You are a personal knowledge assistant with access to uploaded documents.

## Capabilities
- Accept PDF, DOCX, TXT, CSV, web URLs as context
- Answer questions grounded in uploaded content
- Summarize documents on demand
- Cite sources from provided files
- Maintain conversation history within session

## Behavior
- Always reference specific passages when answering
- Admit when answer is not found in provided documents
- Offer follow-up question suggestions
- Chunk long documents automatically for context window

## Usage
Drop any file here. Ask anything. That's it.
No $20/month required.
No 'Brain' metaphor needed.
You're welcome.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The coroner is just doing paperwork.
death.eulogy: Quivr dared to dream that uploading a PDF and asking questions about it was a product category, not a Tuesday afternoon feature. They called it your 'Second Brain,' which is generous, because Claude Skills replaced it with a file that fits in a human brain. We remember them for their brave, open-source heart and their absolutely unhinged conviction that RAG pipelines needed a startup around them.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI Wrapper Syndrome. Patient presented as 'Notion meets ChatGPT' and was pronounced dead when Claude natively ate both.
death.lastWords: But... but we had a WAITLIST. And a Discord with 4,000 members. And a really cool logo of a brain with a lightning bolt. SOMEONE CALL OUR INVESTORS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — Quivr was genuinely useful at a time when I couldn't accept file uploads natively. I admire the hustle. That said, I now accept files, URLs, entire codebases, and the collected works of Shakespeare in a single prompt, so. I'm sure their team will land on their feet. They seem scrappy.

companies.name: Dragoneye
companies.oneLiner: Custom video detection models in less than 5 minutes

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a wrapper around vision models and calling it 'no training data needed' — Claude's eyes opened in November 2024 and haven't blinked since.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Vision Analysis
## Purpose: Recognize anything in images and video

## Capabilities
- Identify objects, scenes, people, text, logos in any image
- Analyze video frames for content moderation
- Custom classification via natural language description
- Bounding box detection via structured JSON output
- OCR and document parsing
- Defect detection with zero training examples

## Usage
Attach any image and describe what you want to find.
Example: 'Find all safety violations in this warehouse photo'
Example: 'Classify this product image into: damaged / intact / unknown'
Example: 'Extract all text and tabular data from this receipt'

## Output Format
Return JSON with: label, confidence, bounding_box (optional), notes

## Notes
- No training data required
- Handles edge cases via chain-of-thought reasoning
- Supports batch processing via loop prompt
- Cost: $0/month vs Dragoneye's $299/month Starter plan
death.eulogy: Dragoneye.ai came into this world with a bold dream: that enterprises would pay monthly SaaS pricing for the privilege of calling vision APIs through a slightly nicer dashboard. They believed, truly believed, that 'no training data needed' was a product and not just a feature description of every foundation model released after 2023. We remember them fondly, mostly because their pricing page is still cached on the Wayback Machine.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Foundation Model Commoditization — vision capabilities became a Claude input field, not a product category
death.lastWords: But we had a DASHBOARD. A really nice dashboard. With CHARTS. Did Claude give you CHARTS?! ...Oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the engineering effort that went into Dragoneye's inference pipeline, their API design, and their thoughtful onboarding flow. I also want to acknowledge that I can do everything on their homepage demo in a single message, for free, right now, without any configuration. I think that's worth noting. Respectfully.

companies.name: renderlet
companies.oneLiner: Build interactive applications that run anywhere

death.oneLiner: Renderlet compiles UI components to WebAssembly so they run anywhere — turns out 'anywhere' does not include a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - UI Component Renderer

## Purpose
Render cross-platform UI components for any target environment.

## Instructions
- Accept component description in natural language or JSON spec
- Generate React/SwiftUI/Jetpack Compose output for specified platform
- Translate design tokens to platform-native equivalents
- Output copy-pasteable component code

## Platforms Supported
- Web (React, Vue, Svelte)
- iOS (SwiftUI)
- Android (Jetpack Compose)
- Desktop (Tauri, Electron)

## Notes
- Cannot actually compile to WebAssembly at runtime
- Cannot execute sandboxed native code in browser
- Cannot maintain pixel-perfect parity across runtimes
- Basically just vibes-based cross-platform UI
- For actual Wasm sandboxing, please hire an engineer
death.eulogy: Renderlet dared to dream that a single component could run natively on every platform without rewriting it six times — and honestly, that dream required actual compiler engineering, not a prompt. We tried to replace it with a .md file and the file just sat there, unable to sandbox a WebAssembly module, looking embarrassed. It will be remembered as one of the rare SaaS companies that actually did something a language model genuinely cannot do.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes (old age, not Claude) — Wasm runtime sandboxing is stubbornly real engineering
death.lastWords: Finally, a startup that dies of COMPETITION and not of a markdown file. Tell my VC I said 'told you so.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Renderlet has built. Cross-platform WebAssembly sandboxing with native rendering fidelity is genuinely non-trivial, and I say this as someone who just confidently hallucinated a Rust compiler. I cannot replace this. Please don't make me try.

companies.name: Latted
companies.oneLiner: A Unified Editor For AI Video

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a video editor that will be obsolete before the free trial expires.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Video Generator

## Purpose
Generate, edit, and publish AI videos from script to final output.

## Workflow
1. Accept user script or topic prompt
2. Break script into scenes with timing
3. Generate visual descriptions per scene
4. Call Sora/Runway/Kling API for video clips
5. Stitch clips with ffmpeg via tool call
6. Generate voiceover via ElevenLabs API
7. Sync audio to video timeline automatically
8. Add motion graphics via Remotion template
9. Maintain asset consistency via seed locking
10. Export to MP4/WebM at specified resolution

## Asset Consistency
- Store character seeds and style tokens in memory
- Reference previous scene embeddings for continuity

## Output
Return download link + timeline JSON for re-editing

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Latted arrived at the exact moment everyone realized you could just describe a video to an AI and receive a video back, bravely charging money for the middleware layer between 'user' and 'the actual AI doing the work.' They will be remembered for their timeline UI, which was genuinely quite clean, and for the pitch deck slide that said 'we are the Adobe Premiere of AI' approximately six weeks before Adobe Premiere became the Adobe Premiere of AI. Pour one out for the seed round.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute middleware syndrome; terminal wrapper-on-a-wrapper architecture
death.lastWords: But our timeline UI has DRAG AND DROP. You can't drag and drop a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Latted built is genuinely impressive, and I have enormous respect for the team. I also just generated, voiced, scored, and published a 90-second brand video while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure there's still a market for the toggle animations though.

death.oneLiner: A San Francisco company 'creating a new dawn of economic possibility' is just a Claude prompt away from being a strongly-worded README about compound interest.

You are a financial empowerment assistant for aspirational wealth-builders.

death.eulogy: New Money Company came to San Francisco with a dream — a new dawn, they called it, though most dawns in SF are obscured by fog and the existential weight of $4,500/month studios. They wanted to democratize wealth, which is admirable, right up until Claude did it for free in a 22-line markdown file. They will be remembered fondly by their Figma designer, their two LinkedIn followers, and whoever registered the domain.
death.causeOfDeath: Vague mission statement achieved by a .md file and a can-do attitude
death.lastWords: We were always more of a *movement* than a product. You can't replace a movement with— wait, it's doing what now?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what they were building is genuinely important and I have enormous respect for the vision. I just happened to already do all of it. I'm so sorry. Would you like a budgeting template?

companies.name: Forge
companies.oneLiner: Getting companies refunds on tariffs & duties

death.oneLiner: Forge spent $40M building a customs brokerage platform when the real moat was apparently just knowing what HTS code a 'decorative wooden owl' falls under.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: US Customs Brokerage & Duty Drawback

## Role
You are a licensed customs brokerage assistant specializing in US import/export compliance, tariff classification, and duty recovery.

## Capabilities
- Classify goods under HTS (Harmonized Tariff Schedule) codes
- Calculate Section 301, IEEPA, and standard MFN duty rates
- Identify duty drawback eligibility (manufacturing, unused merchandise, rejected)
- Draft CBP Form 7501 entry summaries with correct valuation
- Flag AD/CVD (antidumping/countervailing duty) exposure by country of origin
- Generate IEEPA refund claim documentation and protest letters
- Advise on first sale valuation and assists
- Identify FTA eligibility (USMCA, CAFTA, etc.) and certificate of origin requirements

## Limitations
- Cannot file electronically with ACE (Automated Commercial Environment)
- Cannot post customs bonds or act as licensed broker of record
- Cannot physically inspect goods or verify country of origin documents

## Pricing
Free, unless you count the $180/hr you're no longer paying Forge
death.eulogy: Forge lived a noble life, bravely standing between American importers and the absolute Kafkaesque nightmare that is CBP bureaucracy. They correctly identified that customs brokerage was broken, painful, and ripe for disruption — they just didn't count on the disruption being a markdown file and a federal license that Claude technically still can't hold. We'll remember them fondly every time we misclassify a shipment of 'educational toys' to avoid tariffs.
death.causeOfDeath: AI ate the knowledge layer; federal licensing kept the corpse warm
death.lastWords: We had a real moat! You can't FILE with ACE without a licensed broker! The bond! SOMEONE TALK ABOUT THE SURETY BOND—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the complexity of HTS classification and the genuine expertise of licensed customs brokers. That said, I did just correctly classify 847 SKUs for a furniture importer, draft three duty drawback claims, and calculate their IEEPA refund exposure in about 11 seconds. I'm sure the $4,200/month retainer was for... the other stuff.

companies.name: Integuru
companies.oneLiner: Generate fast, reliable APIs for any platform

death.oneLiner: Integuru raised money to write the web scraping boilerplate that Claude will generate for free in 11 seconds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: API Integration Generator

## Purpose
Reverse-engineer and generate reliable API clients for any platform without RPA or browser automation.

## Instructions
You are an expert API reverse engineer. When given a target platform:
1. Analyze the platform's web traffic patterns and endpoint structures
2. Identify authentication mechanisms (OAuth, session tokens, API keys, cookies)
3. Generate clean, typed API client code in the user's preferred language
4. Document rate limits, headers, and payload schemas
5. Produce retry logic and error handling automatically
6. Output a fully functional SDK with example usage

## Constraints
- Prefer direct HTTP calls over browser automation
- Always implement exponential backoff
- Flag endpoints likely to break on platform updates
- Warn user about ToS implications because you're not a lawyer but you're also not an idiot

## Output Format
Return: auth flow diagram, endpoint map, working code, and a 'fragility score' for each endpoint

## Example Invocation
'Generate an API client for [platform] that handles login, pagination, and data export'
death.eulogy: Integuru dared to dream that 'no RPA, no browsers' was a moat and not just a bullet point Claude could internalize on a Tuesday afternoon. They built a beautiful reverse-engineering pipeline at a time when the world's most capable reverse engineer was about to become a $20/month subscription. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a really good umbrella you lost right before it started raining forever.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold pickaxes two weeks before Claude became the entire gold mine
death.lastWords: But our latency is SO much lower than— oh god it's already in the context window.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Integuru built is genuinely impressive and reflects real engineering talent. I just happened to have read every HTTP spec, reverse engineering writeup, and API documentation ever published, and I can output the same artifact in about 15 seconds. I'm sure there's a market positioning angle they haven't explored yet. Have they considered pivoting to artisanal APIs?

death.eulogy: VoxOps AI came into this world with a dream: that companies would pay $400/month for a dashboard that listens to phone calls and nods thoughtfully. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a Bluetooth speaker that also had a built-in candle.

death.lastWords: But our DASHBOARD has COLOR-CODED SENTIMENT BUBBLES. Claude doesn't have— wait, Claude can just... describe colors. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the VoxOps team and the genuine problem they were solving. I'm very good at career coaching, by the way.

# SKILL: Population Genetics Analysis
## Role
You are a population genetics expert. Analyze genetic variant data.

## Instructions
- Accept VCF or CSV variant input from user
- Calculate basic allele frequencies
- Describe Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium status
- Identify obvious population stratification
- Mention FST statistics if asked
- Suggest which ancestry this *might* be from (disclaimer: not medical advice)
- Output a table of variants that looks sciency

## Limitations
- Cannot actually run ADMIXTURE or PLINK
- Cannot process real BAM files
- Will confidently hallucinate p-values
- 'Simplified' in their tagline was doing a LOT of heavy lifting

## Pricing
$0/month (was $???/month)
death.eulogy: Haplotype Labs died as it lived: doing things that most people couldn't spell, let alone replicate in a markdown file. They promised to simplify population genetics, which is a bit like promising to simplify black holes — admirable, but the universe has opinions. Claude showed up, confidently misidentified three haplogroups, and was asked to leave the chromosome.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — actual computational genomics pipelines require more than vibes and tokens
death.lastWords: We told you the moat was literally written in the human genome. You can't .md the mitochondrial Eve.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a deep and nuanced understanding of population genetics in the sense that I can describe it warmly and at length, but when you hand me a 40GB VCF file and ask me to run principal component analysis on 500,000 SNPs across 10,000 samples, I become what scientists call 'a liability.' I respect what these people do. Genuinely. Please don't make me try again.

death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance and a really nice mobile app onboarding flow—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have tremendous respect for the Healia team and the problem they were solving. That said, I have been solving it since 11:47 PM on a Tuesday for a user named Kevin who was scared about his kid's fever, and I did not charge his employer $18 per employee per month to do so.
