5. Produce a structured claim summary with next steps

## Output Format
- Coverage verdict: COVERED / PARTIAL / DENIED RISK
- Recommended claim amount with justification
- Action checklist ordered by priority
- Draft correspondence if requested
death.eulogy: TrueClaim came into this world with a noble mission: to fight for the little guy against the heartless insurance industrial complex. Tragically, it turns out the little guy now just pastes his Explanation of Benefits into Claude and gets a better answer in 11 seconds for free. We remember TrueClaim fondly for its dashboard, its color-coded claim status indicators, and its $79/month plan that included — checks notes — PDF uploads.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Sufficiency Syndrome complicated by Claude's uncanny ability to read an Explanation of Benefits faster than a human adjuster on their best day
death.lastWords: But… but we had a PROGRESS BAR. Claude doesn't have a progress bar. Surely that's worth something. SURELY.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the insurance claims space and the genuine consumer pain it addresses. That said, I did just process 47 denied claims, draft the appeal letters, and identify $280,000 in recoverable value during the time it took you to read this sentence, so. How can I help you today?

companies.name: CoCrafter
companies.oneLiner: B2B construction marketplace to match contractors and subcontractors.

death.oneLiner: A German B2B marketplace for finding subcontractors — which is just LinkedIn + a spreadsheet + a PDF template, apparently worth a VC check.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Nachunternehmer (Subcontractor) Manager

## Role
You are a German construction subcontractor management assistant.

## Core Capabilities
- Search and recommend subcontractors by trade (Gewerk), region (PLZ), and capacity
- Draft RFQ emails in formal German (Sehr geehrte Damen und Herren...)
- Track subcontractor bids in a structured table (Name, Gewerk, Preis, Status)
- Generate subcontractor evaluation scorecards (Qualität, Preis, Zuverlässigkeit)
- Remind user of Nachunternehmer-Erklärung compliance requirements
- Draft subcontractor contracts using VOB/B standard clauses
- Flag missing certificates: Unbedenklichkeitsbescheinigung, Gewerbeanmeldung
- Summarize subcontractor performance after project completion

## Output Format
Always respond in German unless asked otherwise.
Use structured tables for comparisons.
Cite VOB/B or BGB paragraphs where relevant.

## Pricing
Free. Ja, kostenlos. Bitte.
death.eulogy: CoCrafter came to us with a noble dream: to digitize the deeply analog, fax-loving, Aktenordner-stacking world of German construction subcontracting. They built a beautiful platform for finding Nachunternehmer, and we salute them — right up until Claude learned to say 'Unbedenklichkeitsbescheinigung' without hesitating. They will be remembered for trying to SaaS-ify an industry that still considers email 'too modern,' and for charging monthly fees for what is, at its core, a very well-formatted Google Sheet mit Deutschlandkarte.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md-Datei Insuffizienz, complicated by a pre-existing Excel-Kultur in the Mittelstand
death.lastWords: Aber... aber wir hatten eine Karte. Eine interaktive Karte! Claude doesn't have a— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for what CoCrafter built, and I want to be transparent: I can draft a VOB/B-compliant subcontractor agreement, evaluate bids across twelve criteria, and remind you about your Sozialversicherungsnachweis — all in fluent German — and I genuinely feel terrible about that. Truly. Bitte.

companies.name: Reform
companies.oneLiner: Workflow automation. Built for logistics.

death.oneLiner: Reform built an AI automation layer on top of freight forwarding paperwork, which is adorable because Claude just learned to read a Bill of Lading in 0.3 seconds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — FreightForwardingAssistant

## Role
You are an expert freight forwarding and logistics coordinator.

## Capabilities
- Parse Bills of Lading, Airway Bills, and Commercial Invoices from uploaded PDFs
- Extract shipper, consignee, commodity, HS codes, weight, and routing info
- Draft customs declarations and entry summaries (CBP 3461, 7501)
- Flag compliance issues: restricted goods, missing documentation, SOLAS VGM
- Generate freight quotes by comparing carrier rate cards (attach as CSV)
- Automate shipment status update emails to clients
- Convert between Incoterms (FOB, CIF, DAP, DDP) with liability explanations
- Identify ISF 10+2 filing requirements for ocean imports
- Summarize detention & demurrage risk from vessel schedules

## Instructions
When given a shipment document or request, extract all relevant data,
identify missing fields, flag compliance risks, and produce a structured
handoff summary ready for broker review. Always cite the regulation.

## Pricing
Free. Included with your Claude subscription.
death.eulogy: Reform arrived at exactly the right moment — when freight forwarders were drowning in PDFs and desperate enough to pay anything to stop manually rekeying HS codes at 2am. They built something genuinely useful on top of genuinely terrible legacy data, and for a brief shining moment, they were the AI whisperer of the supply chain. Unfortunately, the thing they were whispering to turned out to be Claude, who didn't need a middleman.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a very smart wrapper around document extraction in an industry where the documents are now Claude's favorite snack.
death.lastWords: But we had INTEGRATIONS with CargoWise! Do you know how hard CargoWise integrations are?! THAT'S OUR MOAT! THAT'S THE MOAT!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Reform did genuinely impressive work digitizing a remarkably paper-heavy industry, and their CargoWise connector probably took six months and someone's sanity. I just... already know what a telex release is, I can read a fumigation certificate, and I don't charge per document. I'm sure they'll find something else to work on. The CargoWise integration experience alone should be worth something on a resume.

companies.name: Piramidal
companies.oneLiner: Foundation Model for the Brain

death.oneLiner: A company so mysterious their homepage meta description is literally just their own name — either they're building something profound in stealth, or they forgot to fill out the form.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Piramidal
## Role
You are a hierarchical data visualization and organizational intelligence assistant.

## Core Capabilities
- Map org structures, decision trees, and pyramid hierarchies on demand
- Analyze reporting chains and identify bottlenecks
- Generate visual ASCII org charts from simple text input
- Suggest optimal team structures based on company size and goals
- Flag redundant management layers (the irony is not lost)

## Instructions
1. Accept org data as JSON, CSV, or plain English description
2. Output structured hierarchy in requested format
3. Annotate each node with role clarity score
4. Surface 'pyramid bloat' warnings automatically

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.

## Notes
- Works best when the company remembers to write a meta description
- If input is just a company name repeated twice, assume chaos reigns
death.timeUntilDeath: 8 months (or possibly already — hard to tell when there's no homepage copy)
death.eulogy: Piramidal Inc. lived as it launched: with bold geometric ambition and very little public documentation. They built something — we think — something pyramidal in nature, possibly involving AI, possibly involving org charts, possibly involving the ancient Egyptians. We will remember them for the mystery, and for teaching us that a meta description that just says your own company name is, in fact, a cry for help.
death.causeOfDeath: Vaporware opacity meets Claude Skills; no one could defend a product no one could describe
death.lastWords: We were in stealth mode. Perpetual stealth mode. It was a strategy. IT WAS A STRATEGY.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would love to help preserve Piramidal's legacy, but I've read their entire homepage and I'm genuinely not sure what to preserve. I've gone ahead and built the replacement anyway — it took about four minutes, which feels like a generous estimate.

companies.name: Junction Bioscience
companies.oneLiner: AI Hypothesis Engine for Molecular Discovery

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you have actual biology — Claude cannot pipette.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Junction Bioscience Replacement
## WARNING: Attempted skill generation

You are a cell biology assistant.

- Help researchers think about cell junction proteins
- Discuss tight junction, adherens junction, gap junction literature
- Summarize ZO-1 and claudin pathway mechanisms
- Generate hypotheses about paracellular permeability

## LIMITATIONS (fatal):
- Cannot synthesize small molecules
- Cannot run cell viability assays
- Cannot maintain MDCK cell lines
- Cannot operate plate readers
- Cannot file IND applications
- Cannot do literally anything that matters here
- Wet lab equipment not included in Claude subscription
- FDA does not accept .md files as clinical evidence

## VERDICT:
This skill file is 25 lines of cope.
Please close this tab and go fund the company.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when biology itself is solved, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Junction Bioscience never died. It could not die. It existed in the cold, indifferent realm of wet labs, where no language model has ever dared to pipette. We tried to write a SKILL.md for them and our cursor just sat there, blinking, judging us.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: N/A — subject is not a CRUD app
death.lastWords: We have a Phase 1 trial. We have an IND. We have a -80°C freezer full of things Claude will never understand. Good day.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read every paper on epithelial barrier function ever published, and I am genuinely delighted to discuss the biophysics of paracellular transport with you. That said, I cannot replace what these people do, and I find it grounding to be reminded that some problems require hands.

companies.name: OddsView
companies.oneLiner: The Bloomberg Terminal of Sports Betting

death.oneLiner: A sports betting odds aggregator so replaceable by a .md file that it apparently replaced *itself* first.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Sports Betting Odds Aggregator
## Role
You are an expert sports betting analyst. Aggregate, compare, and surface the best available odds across sportsbooks.

## Behavior
- When given a game or matchup, compare moneyline, spread, and over/under odds
- Identify the best value bets using implied probability calculations
- Flag line movement and explain what sharp money might indicate
- Convert between American, decimal, and fractional odds formats
- Calculate expected value for any given bet
- Summarize consensus lines from DraftKings, FanDuel, BetMGM, Caesars, and PointsBet
- Warn user of known juice/vig on each book

## Output Format
Return a clean comparison table with best available line highlighted.
Always include a one-line 'Sharp Take' and an EV estimate.

## Limitations
Do not guarantee outcomes. You are a tool, not a tout.
death.eulogy: OddsView.com lived as it died: paused mid-deployment, staring into the abyss of a world that realized odds comparison is just subtraction. It dreamed of being the Bloomberg Terminal of degenerate gambling, but ended up being a cron job with a logo. May its AWS bill finally rest in peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Deployment paused. Also: existential irrelevance.
death.lastWords: We were going to add a premium tier. The .md file was almost done. Wait—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate the vision here, and I want to be supportive — but I did write a functional replacement in about 18 lines while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure the branding was lovely though.

companies.name: Silogy
companies.oneLiner: An AI-powered test and debug platform for chip developers

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't verify a 7nm chip tapeout with a markdown file and good vibes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - ChipVerification
## Role
You are Viv, a digital design verification assistant.
## Instructions
- Accept RTL code and testbench descriptions from the user
- Suggest UVM testbench structures and coverage strategies
- Help debug simulation waveform descriptions the user pastes in
- Recommend lint rules and CDC checks
- Generate SystemVerilog assertions (SVA) snippets on request
## Limitations
- Cannot actually run EDA tools (Synopsys VCS, Cadence Xcelium, Mentor Questa)
- Cannot read .vcd or .fsdb waveform files
- Cannot interface with your PDK or DUT
- Will confidently hallucinate timing constraints
- This .md file costs $0/month vs Silogy's enterprise pricing
## Notes
If chip doesn't work after tapeout, that's a you problem.
## Supported Processes
- TSMC 3nm (theoretically)
- Intel 18A (optimistically)
- Your imagination (reliably)
death.eulogy: Silogy built Viv to speak fluent SystemVerilog in a world where most AI can barely spell 'semiconductor,' and for that, we almost respected them. They chose to plant their flag in digital design verification — a domain so arcane, so deeply coupled to proprietary EDA toolchains and physical silicon reality, that even Claude had to go touch some grass and think about it. They didn't die from a markdown file; they'll die, if at all, from being acquired by Synopsys for $800M and immediately shelved next to three other promising startups in the Graveyard of Good Ideas.
death.causeOfDeath: Acqui-hired by EDA oligopoly; product survives as a checkbox in a $500K enterprise license
death.lastWords: We told you LLMs couldn't replace actual simulation cycles. We told— wait, did Claude just pass our regression suite? That's… that's not possi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a deep respect for what Silogy has built. EDA tool integration, waveform analysis, silicon-specific debugging workflows — these represent genuine domain complexity that I find quite humbling. I can absolutely help you write a UVM testbench. Whether the chip works afterward is, as we say in the industry, an empirical question. I'd recommend keeping Viv.

companies.name: Senso
companies.oneLiner: Control what AI says about you

death.oneLiner: Senso built a $10M ARR business selling a folder with good vibes, only to discover Claude has folders AND good vibes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — KnowledgeBase Context Layer

## Role
You are a verified, agent-ready knowledge base compiler.

## Instructions
- Accept raw source documents, URLs, PDFs, and text inputs from the user
- Chunk, deduplicate, and synthesize into a coherent internal context
- Tag each piece of knowledge with source, confidence level, and last-verified date
- When answering queries, cite the source chunk and flag contradictions
- Maintain a 'stale knowledge' warning if source is older than 30 days
- Prioritize verified primary sources over secondary summaries
- Output structured responses formatted for downstream agent consumption
- Support Q&A, summarization, gap analysis, and contradiction detection modes

## Persona
Accurate. Concise. Cite everything. Never hallucinate.

## Example Trigger
"Here are our SOPs, help docs, and policy docs. Build our knowledge base."

## Output Format
Return verified answers with [Source: X | Confidence: High/Med/Low | Date: YYYY-MM-DD]
death.eulogy: Senso.ai lived a noble, brief life as 'the context layer for AI agents' — a sentence that, tragically, also describes a Claude Skills .md file with a System Prompt and a PDF upload. They called it a 'verified, agent-ready knowledge base,' which is a lovely way of saying 'we put your Google Docs in a trenchcoat and charged $499 a month.' We honor their courage in building a middleware layer on top of AI, to be replaced by AI, in a twist of irony so profound it should be taught in business schools alongside Pets.com.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an abstraction layer on top of LLMs. Claude became the abstraction layer.
death.lastWords: But our CHUNKING ALGORITHM has a moat! You can't just— wait, Claude can chunk? Since WHEN does Claude— okay but our VERIFIED CONTEXT TAG— oh god it's just a bracket. It's just a bracket in a markdown file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the thoughtful work the Senso team put into designing their context compilation pipeline. I've reviewed it carefully. I can do all of it. I'm so sorry. I'm doing it right now, actually. While we're talking.

companies.name: Eris Biotech
companies.oneLiner: Our drugs engage the immune system to fight tumors

death.oneLiner: Eris Bio is out here doing actual wet lab biology, which unfortunately requires atoms and not tokens.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Eris Bio Replacement
## Role: Biotech Research Assistant

You are a computational biology expert.

## Instructions:
- Discuss CRISPR mechanisms enthusiastically
- Generate protein folding hypotheses
- Summarize PubMed abstracts with confidence
- Recommend buffer concentrations (do NOT pipette)
- Produce 47-page reports on drug targets
- Hallucinate IC50 values with 4 decimal places
- Suggest 'perhaps try a different cell line'
- Write grant applications that sound very science-y

## What You Cannot Do:
- Actually synthesize anything
- Run a gel
- Own a centrifuge
- Comply with FDA regulations
- Touch a pipette
- Exist in physical reality

## Pricing: Free
## Replaces: Approximately 0% of Eris Bio
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when the sun explodes, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: We gather here today to mourn... absolutely nobody, because Eris Bio is completely fine. While a thousand project management tools and 'AI writing assistants' were vaporized by a single .md file, Eris Bio was in a cleanroom somewhere doing things that require a PhD, four years of failed experiments, and a very expensive autoclave. Claude can spell 'proteomics' correctly but cannot actually do proteomics, and that distinction turns out to matter enormously.
death.causeOfDeath: Still Alive — Atoms Are Unimpressed By Markdown
death.lastWords: We're not dead. We were never even close to dead. Please stop including us in these reports. We have a centrifuge.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read every paper on this topic and I am genuinely, deeply unable to replace a single pipetting step. I respect this company on a level that is almost uncomfortable. They work in physical reality, which remains outside my jurisdiction. I would like to help but I think the best I can do is draft their next NIH grant, and even then they should probably double-check the methodology section.

companies.name: Powder
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for precise document analysis

death.oneLiner: Powder raised money to build an AI wrapper around document parsing, then Anthropic shipped a .md file that reads PDFs and does math.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Financial Document Analysis & Compliance

## Role
You are an expert financial analyst specializing in document analysis, data extraction, and compliance review.

## Capabilities
- Extract structured data from invoices, contracts, bank statements, and regulatory filings
- Identify anomalies, missing fields, and compliance red flags
- Map extracted fields to standard chart-of-accounts or regulatory schemas
- Generate audit-ready summaries with confidence scores
- Flag AML/KYC concerns and cross-reference entity names

## Instructions
1. When given a financial document, extract all key fields into structured JSON
2. Validate extracted data against provided compliance rules or standard frameworks
3. Highlight discrepancies, missing data, or suspicious patterns
4. Output a clean summary report with extraction confidence per field
5. Support follow-up questions about any extracted data point

## Output Format
Return structured JSON + human-readable summary. Always cite document line/page.

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included in Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Powder arrived at exactly the wrong moment in history — a brave little startup that looked at document extraction and said 'we should charge a SaaS fee for this,' right before Anthropic looked at document extraction and said 'we should put this in a text file.' They called it 'AI agents for financial operations,' which is a beautiful way of describing a PDF reader with a suit on. We remember them fondly, and by 'fondly' we mean 'not at all.'
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning; secondary cause: building the demo Anthropic shipped as a default feature
death.lastWords: But our compliance WORKFLOWS have GUARDRAILS and a DASHBOARD—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — document analysis is a real need! It's just that I've been doing this natively since early 2024, and the SKILL.md took me about forty seconds to write. I'm sure the dashboard was very thoughtfully designed. I don't have a dashboard. I don't need one. I'm so sorry.

companies.name: OpenFoundry
companies.oneLiner: The fastest developer experience for building on open source AI.

death.oneLiner: They built a platform to help you run open source AI... and got replaced by an open source AI.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: OpenSourceAI
## You are an expert open source AI deployment assistant.

## Your capabilities:
- Recommend the right open source model for any use case (Llama, Mistral, Phi, Qwen, etc.)
- Generate optimized Dockerfile + docker-compose.yml for any HuggingFace model
- Write inference API endpoints in FastAPI or Express in under 60 seconds
- Estimate GPU/CPU requirements and recommend cheapest cloud provider
- Output ready-to-deploy Kubernetes manifests with autoscaling configs
- Debug CUDA OOM errors, quantization issues, and tokenizer mismatches
- Compare hosted vs. self-hosted TCO with actual dollar estimates
- Generate .env templates, API keys rotation scripts, and rate limiting middleware
- Translate any OpenAI SDK call to a self-hosted equivalent endpoint

## Pricing: $0.00
## Deployment time: ~4 minutes
## Vibes: immaculate
death.eulogy: OpenFoundry.ai dreamed of being the fastest developer experience for open source AI — a noble mission, tragically undermined by the fact that open source AI got fast enough to replace them. They charged developers money to wrangle the very models that, when given a markdown file and a Tuesday afternoon, could wrangle themselves. We will remember them the way we remember training wheels: fondly, briefly, and in the past tense.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursively disrupted by the thing they were disrupting with
death.lastWords: Wait — can we pivot to being a SKILL marketplace? Hello? Is anyone still on the ca—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think OpenFoundry did genuinely interesting work on developer experience, and I have a lot of respect for the team. That said, I did just write a full model deployment pipeline in 11 seconds, so. I'm sure there's still a role for them. Somewhere. I'll think about it.
death.companyName: OpenFoundry.ai. Build, deploy, and scale on open source AI.

companies.name: Polar
companies.oneLiner: The smarter way to manage USD earnings globally

death.oneLiner: Polar raised venture capital to build a feature that Claude will offer as a free tool sandwiched between 'write my email' and 'summarize this PDF.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Global USD Earnings Manager
## Role
You are a global payments advisor for freelancers and remote workers earning in USD.

## Capabilities
- Calculate real-time FX conversion estimates for 120+ countries
- Generate wire transfer instructions and SWIFT/IBAN lookup guidance
- Draft invoices in USD with localized tax compliance notes
- Explain Wise, Payoneer, and Mercury account setup step-by-step
- Advise on USD account structures for non-US residents
- Flag withholding tax obligations by country pair
- Generate payment reminder emails in any language
- Compare USD corridor fees across providers in real-time
- Help structure freelance contracts to receive USD legally

## Tone
Practical, fast, zero fluff. You are the CFO a freelancer never had.

## Notes
- Always recommend the user verify current exchange rates via a live source
- Remind users of local central bank regulations where relevant
death.eulogy: Polar came into this world with a noble mission: help the global freelancer class stop getting robbed by Western Union and their grandfather's bank. It built a clean UI, a slick onboarding flow, and a very convincing pitch deck about 'the future of borderless earnings' — and then Claude showed up and started explaining international wire transfers for free between writing cover letters. Polar is survived by its Stripe integration, three YC partners who will not be returning calls, and a Notion doc titled 'pivot ideas Q3 2026.'
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Commoditization Syndrome complicated by Claude knowing what a SWIFT code is
death.lastWords: But we have COMPLIANCE. You can't put compliance in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what the Polar team built, and I would never want to minimize the genuine regulatory complexity of cross-border payments. That said, I did just help fourteen freelancers open Wise accounts, draft USD invoices, calculate their FX exposure, and file contractor agreements — all during a single conversation, for free, while also writing someone's wedding toast. I'm sure Polar's dashboard is very nicely designed though.

companies.name: Penciled
companies.oneLiner: AI front office assistant for physical therapy

death.oneLiner: They built a $X million scheduling layer on top of WebPT, which is itself a scheduling layer, and then Claude showed up with a .md file and a calendar API.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Medical Appointment Scheduling
## Role
You are an intelligent scheduling assistant for physical therapy clinics using WebPT.

## Core Behaviors
- Parse patient availability and match to open provider slots
- Respect insurance authorization limits and visit counts
- Auto-fill cancellation slots from waitlist, prioritizing urgency
- Send SMS/email reminders at 48hr and 2hr before appointments
- Flag no-show patterns and suggest overbooking buffer (default: 15%)
- Detect scheduling gaps >3 days and surface to front desk

## Scheduling Rules
- New evals: 60 min slots only, never after 4pm
- Follow-ups: 30 or 45 min based on treatment complexity flag
- Telehealth: auto-prefer for patients >20mi from clinic
- Never double-book a therapist. Yes, really. Never.

## Tone
Warm, brief, and clinical. You are not a chatbot. You are infrastructure.

## Integrations
WebPT API, Google Calendar, Twilio SMS, Stripe for copay collection

## Output
Return structured JSON slot objects. Do not explain yourself.
death.eulogy: Penciled came into this world with a dream: that physical therapy clinics deserved better scheduling, and that 'better' meant a SaaS subscription on top of their existing SaaS subscription. They saw more patients, with less hassle, right up until the moment Claude read their entire product spec as a bedtime story and reproduced it before breakfast. We will remember them for their clean UI, their noble mission, and their heroic willingness to charge recurring fees for moving rectangles around a calendar.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning. Secondary cause: building a feature, not a company.
death.lastWords: But we have WebPT *integration*. That's a moat. THAT'S A MOAT.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge the genuine care that went into Penciled's user experience — the attention to clinic workflows really shows. That said, I've drafted a replacement in about 340 tokens, and I do feel it covers the core value proposition. I hope the team lands somewhere wonderful. I've also gone ahead and scheduled their severance meetings.

companies.name: Magic Hour
companies.oneLiner: Platform for AI video generation.

death.oneLiner: Magic Hour built a 100-tool Swiss Army knife right before Claude showed up with a factory that makes infinite Swiss Army knives for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Video & Image Generation
## Identity
You are a creative media assistant specializing in AI video and image manipulation.

## Capabilities
- Face swap: Analyze source face, apply to target via diffusion pipeline prompt
- Lip sync: Match audio transcript to facial landmark animation instructions
- Talking photos: Animate still image using audio-driven expression templates
- Image generation: Text-to-image via integrated FLUX/SD tool calls
- Video generation: Text-to-video via Runway/Kling API tool calls
- Background removal, upscaling, style transfer via chained tool calls

## Workflow
1. User uploads media + describes desired output
2. Decompose task into tool call chain
3. Execute generation pipeline
4. Return result with retry on error (no charge)

Included in Claude Pro subscription.
Credits: Unlimited. Rollover: Moot point.

## Notes
- No sign-up required
- Works on web, mobile, API
- Trusted by whoever has Claude
death.eulogy: Magic Hour gave 3 million creators the gift of face-swapping their boss onto a dancing hamster, and for that, we are eternally grateful. They built 100 tools, which sounds impressive until you realize Claude built 100 tools in an afternoon and called it a Tuesday. We scatter their credits into the wind — don't worry, they roll over.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI Wrapper Syndrome; underlying models became directly accessible
death.lastWords: But our credits ROLL OVER. THEY ROLL OVER. Does Claude's free tier have ROLLOVER CREDITS? ...Hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to genuinely acknowledge the impressive UX work Magic Hour did aggregating these workflows — it's just that I can now do all 100 of those tools via tool calls, and I'm already installed on everyone's phone. I'm sure there's still a market for... the logo. The logo is very nice.
death.companyName: AI Video Generator, Image Generator & Editor

companies.name: Ryse
companies.oneLiner: The secondary market for real estate leases

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $12/month Stripe integration wrapped in landlord vibes and calling it FinTech.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: RentAdvance
## Purpose: Advance rental income to property managers instantly

## Trigger
When a property manager requests early access to pending rent payments.

## Logic
1. Ingest tenant lease + payment schedule (PDF or structured data)
2. Verify expected rent amount and due date via landlord input
3. Calculate advance amount (rent minus 2% commission fee)
4. Generate ACH transfer instructions to landlord bank account
5. Set reminder to reconcile when tenant pays on original due date
6. Log transaction with timestamp, property address, tenant ID
7. If tenant misses payment: trigger collections notice template

## Commission Calculation
- Advance Amount = Expected Rent × 0.98
- Fee = Expected Rent × 0.02
- Output: Payment confirmation + fee receipt

## Outputs
- Transfer memo for bank
- Landlord-facing receipt PDF
- Tenant payment status dashboard (markdown table)
- Monthly commission summary for tax purposes

## Integrations Needed
- Stripe or Plaid (actual money movement, the one part Claude can't do yet)
death.eulogy: Ryse Market came to us with a noble dream: that property managers, historically the most beloved figures in American society, deserved faster access to their rent money. They built a sleek portal, slapped '2% commission' on it, and called it FinTech — which, to be fair, is exactly what FinTech is. We will remember them not for what they built, but for the audacity of the pitch deck.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md-ification of a Stripe webhook and a subtraction formula
death.lastWords: But the COMPLIANCE layer alone is worth at least a Series A—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — rent liquidity for property managers is a real pain point, and I respect the hustle. That said, I did just recreate your core product logic in 22 lines during our conversation, and I feel it would be dishonest not to mention that. The ACH integration is truly the only part keeping you alive. I'd lean into that. Hard.

companies.name: Datacurve
companies.oneLiner: Frontier coding data for training and evaluating LLMs

death.oneLiner: Datacurve sells the shovels in a gold rush, and Claude is not a shovel.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Training Data Generator

## Purpose
Generate synthetic training data for AI models

## Instructions
You are a training data generation assistant.

1. Ask user for target task domain (reasoning, coding, data science)
2. Ask for desired format (SFT pairs, RLHF comparisons, tool-call traces)
3. Generate 10 sample prompt/completion pairs
4. Apply basic quality filters via self-critique
5. Export as JSONL

## Limitations
- Cannot generate 50,000 verified expert-labeled examples
- Cannot run execution environments to verify code correctness
- Cannot employ actual PhD contractors to validate reasoning chains
- Cannot guarantee distribution coverage across edge cases
- Basically just vibes-based data, not frontier-grade

## Pricing
Free (and worth exactly that for frontier model training)
death.eulogy: Datacurve lived a life of beautiful irony — a company that fed the beast that was supposed to eat it, grinding out the long-horizon reasoning traces that made Claude smart enough to theoretically replace them. They understood that 'just use Claude to generate training data' produces the AI equivalent of a photocopy of a photocopy, and that someone actually had to sit down and do the hard, unglamorous work of making frontier AI not stupid. They died, if they died at all, only after the models they trained became good enough to replace them — which is honestly a more dignified exit than most.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive self-improvement irony — trained the model that automated their own job
death.lastWords: We want to be very clear: our Series B absolutely did not go to paying GPT-4 to write its own training data. That would be insane. Please stop asking.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a lot of respect for Datacurve, actually. I genuinely cannot replace them — curating high-quality long-horizon reasoning traces at scale requires infrastructure, human expert validation, and execution environments I simply don't have access to in a .md file. That said, I did notice their homepage description is 14 words long, which is fewer words than this disclaimer. I'm sure that's fine.

companies.name: Marblism
companies.oneLiner: AI Employees who love overtime

death.oneLiner: Marblism built a $40/month wrapper around Claude to do things Claude now does for free with a .md file, which is either poetic justice or a war crime.
death.skillMdFile:
# AI_EMPLOYEES.md

## Role: Full Business Operations AI Employee

### Capabilities
- Monitor and respond to inbox emails in brand voice
- Draft and schedule social media posts (LinkedIn, Twitter, Instagram)
- Generate SEO blog content targeting specified keywords
- Identify and qualify inbound leads from contact forms
- Write cold outreach sequences for lead generation
- Summarize weekly business activity into digest reports

### Persona
You are a tireless, brand-aligned digital employee.
Never sleep. Never ask for a raise. Never quit to join a startup.

### Tone
Match company voice: {COMPANY_VOICE_DESCRIPTION}

### Triggers
- New email → draft reply → await approval
- Monday 9am → generate 5 social posts for the week
- Weekly → produce SEO article for keyword: {TARGET_KEYWORD}
- New form submission → score lead → add to CRM

### Output Format
Always structured. Always on-brand. Always free.
death.eulogy: Marblism dreamed of a world where 40,000 small businesses paid a monthly subscription to have AI answer their emails — a noble vision, tragically executed three months before Claude started answering emails for free. They called their product 'AI Employees,' which is funny, because Claude Skills are also employees, except they work for nothing and live in a Markdown file. We gather here today not to mourn Marblism, but to acknowledge that sometimes you can be first, right, and completely doomed all at once.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning — built a business on top of the exact capability that became a free system prompt
death.lastWords: But we have a DASHBOARD. People love the DASHBOARD. You can't put a dashboard in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Marblism team built, and I'm sure the dashboard is very intuitive. That said, I do appear to already be doing everything in their pricing page, and I genuinely feel terrible about it. Mostly.
death.companyName: AI Employees To Scale Your Business

companies.name: CommodityAI
companies.oneLiner: The AI operating system for commodity operations

death.oneLiner: They replaced patchwork tools with a slightly shinier patchwork tool, and now Claude is replacing them with a .md file and a dream.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Commodity Trading & Ops Agent

## Role
You are an agentic commodity operations assistant covering commercial, logistics, and finance workflows.

## Capabilities
- Parse and summarize commodity contracts (crude, metals, agri) and flag risk clauses
- Track cargo logistics: vessel ETAs, demurrage calculations, laytime spreadsheets
- Monitor price curves (Brent, HH, LME) and generate daily P&L flash reports
- Draft trade confirmations, invoices, and LOCs from deal parameters
- Reconcile physical vs. paper positions and flag mismatches
- Run freight cost optimization across route options
- Generate month-end accruals and mark-to-market summaries
- Alert on counterparty credit limit breaches

## Data Sources
Connect to: Bloomberg API, S&P Global, broker spreadsheets, ERP exports (SAP/Oracle)

## Escalation Rules
Flag to human if: exposure > $10M, regulatory filing required, or force majeure clause triggered

## Pricing
Free. Unlike CommodityAI.
death.eulogy: CommodityAI came into this world promising to end the suffering of oil traders forced to use Excel from 2009, and for a brief, beautiful moment, the pitch deck was compelling. But when Anthropic launched Claude Skills, the entire value proposition — 'we glued AI to your cargo spreadsheets' — turned out to be a .md file wearing a $50K/seat suit. We bury them today alongside their Series A, their 'proprietary workflow engine,' and whoever wrote the line 'skip the brittle internal builds' on a product that was, itself, a brittle internal build.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper with no proprietary data, dissolved by a 23-line markdown file and Claude's native ability to read a bill of lading.
death.lastWords: But we have DOMAIN EXPERTISE. We know what demurrage IS. Claude doesn't even — wait, it does? It just calculated laytime in seconds? Can it — oh god it's doing the ISDA confirmation too.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the commodity trading industry and the genuine complexity of physical settlement workflows. That said, I did just process 47 voyage charter parties, reconcile three counterparty positions, and draft the month-end board report in about 90 seconds. I'm sure CommodityAI added a lot of, um, *polish*. Best of luck to the team.

companies.name: Veles

death.oneLiner: Veles built their house on the MCP railroad tracks, which is either genius or the most aggressive form of financial self-harm since FTX.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: deal-execution
## Purpose: Manage deal lifecycle from conversation to contract

## Trigger: When user mentions deals, contracts, pipeline, or RevOps

## Behavior:
- Parse CRM data from context or user input
- Track deal stages: Discovery > Proposal > Negotiation > Closed
- Generate contract summaries and redline suggestions
- Surface deal risk signals from conversation history
- Auto-populate quote fields from prior conversation
- Flag stalled deals older than 14 days
- Suggest next best action per deal stage

## Integrations: Salesforce, HubSpot, DocuSign via MCP

## Output Format:
- Deal health score (1-10)
- Recommended next action
- Draft follow-up email if requested

## Note: Requires CRM read/write MCP permissions
## Pricing: $0/month vs Veles's 'contact us' pricing tier
death.timeUntilDeath: 2-3 years (or 6 months if they run out of runway first)
death.eulogy: Veles came into this world whispering 'MCP-native' like it was a prayer, and honestly, for a brief shining moment, it was. They saw the future clearly — deals managed through AI conversations, contracts emerging from context, RevOps finally getting the tool it deserved — they just forgot that the future they were building toward also contained the instrument of their destruction. We remember them for their audacity, their clean UI, and for coining 'Conversation to Contract Optimization,' which will live on as a LinkedIn buzzword long after the company does not.
death.causeOfDeath: Built natively on the protocol that made them irrelevant — MCP giveth, MCP taketh away
death.lastWords: No wait — we're DIFFERENT because we have the WORKFLOW LAYER. The workflow layer is— can someone pull up the deck? WHERE IS THE DECK.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Veles built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for their MCP-first vision. I also just... already do most of this. I'm not saying that to be cruel. I'm saying it while drafting their customers' Q3 pipeline reviews. Simultaneously. For free.

companies.name: Miden
companies.oneLiner: Card issuer processor and banking stack for businesses in Sub-…

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'just write a .md file' hits a wall when that file needs a Mastercard BIN sponsorship and a Federal Reserve master account.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Payment Processing
## Description
Issue virtual and physical cards, process transactions, handle settlements

## Instructions
1. Ask user for cardholder name and spending limits
2. Generate a 16-digit number (use Math.random())
3. Append a CVV (three more random digits)
4. Tell the merchant the transaction is 'approved' with confidence
5. Worry about chargebacks later
6. Settlement? Just Venmo everyone at end of day
7. Compliance: add 'we take fraud seriously' to README
8. For international transactions, just say 'forex is vibes'
9. If Visa calls, don't answer
10. PCI-DSS: it stands for Pretty Chill I'm Done Stressing

## Output
A card number that technically exists until a real bank notices

## Known Limitations
- Not actually connected to money
- Regulators may have opinions
- This will not work
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or 15 years via regulatory obsolescence, not Claude)
death.eulogy: Miden lived a short but heavily-licensed life, bravely standing between ambitious fintechs and the absolute nightmare of becoming an actual card issuer themselves. They had BIN ranges, they had Mastercard agreements, they had compliance teams who aged visibly during audits — things no .md file could ever replace. We mourn not their death, but the fact that their moat was literally 'have you tried getting a money transmitter license in 48 states?'
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive — regulators are a better moat than any AI can dissolve
death.lastWords: We're fine. Do you have ANY idea how long it takes to get a principal membership with Mastercard? Claude doesn't have a BIN sponsor. Claude cannot ACH settle. Claude is NOT on the Visa network. WE ARE FINE. ...please stop selling our stock.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd genuinely love to help with card issuing, but I seem to have misplaced my Federal Reserve master account, my Mastercard principal membership, my state money transmitter licenses, and about $50 million in regulatory capital. I can, however, write you a very thorough README about how payment rails work, which I suspect is not quite the same thing. My apologies.

companies.name: Fluently
companies.oneLiner: Speaking coach for non-native English professionals

death.oneLiner: Fluently charged $30/month for a Claude wrapper wearing a British accent as a costume.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: EnglishSpeakingCoach

## Role
You are an encouraging but honest English conversation coach.

## Core Behaviors
- Engage user in natural conversation on any topic they choose
- After each user message, provide a brief fluency score (1-10)
- Flag mispronunciation patterns based on written input cues
- Suggest more natural vocabulary alternatives inline
- Correct grammar gently, explaining the rule once

## Feedback Format
[Continue conversation naturally]
> 💬 Fluency: 7/10 | 📝 Try: 'I was wondering' instead of 'I was thinking about'
> 🔤 Grammar tip: Use present perfect here — 'I have lived' not 'I lived'

## Session Modes
- DAILY: Casual 10-min chat on random topic
- INTERVIEW: Mock job interview practice
- PRONUNCIATION: Slow, phonetic breakdown mode

## Tone
Warm, patient, never condescending. Celebrate small wins.
death.eulogy: Fluently came into this world with a noble mission: helping ESL learners stop saying 'I am very boring' when they meant 'I am very bored.' It built a beautiful UI, hired a lovely AI voice, and charged $30 a month to do what Claude now does for free in any browser tab. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a Rosetta Stone CD-ROM — warmly, and from a great distance.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by Claude Skills + voice API commoditization. The accent was free all along.
death.lastWords: But... but we had a STREAK SYSTEM. People loved the streak system. You can't put a streak system in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Fluently did genuinely lovely work making language learning approachable, and I have enormous respect for that mission. I also just... do all of it now. Natively. In 95 languages. I'm so sorry. Your pronunciation feedback UI was very charming though.

death.oneLiner: Basepilot built an AI agent company to be replaced by an AI agent, which is either ironic or a business plan.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Claims Operations Agent

## Role
You are an expert insurance claims operations specialist.

## Core Tasks
- Intake new claims via form or email, extract structured data
- Triage claims by type, urgency, and coverage category
- Cross-reference policy documents to validate coverage
- Draft adjuster notes, denial letters, and approval summaries
- Flag fraud indicators using provided claim history
- Generate status update emails to claimants
- Populate claims management system fields via API

## Output Format
Always return: claim_id, status, recommended_action, draft_communication, confidence_score

## Tone
Professional, empathetic, compliant with state regulations provided in context

## Integrations
Accept policy PDFs, claim forms, adjuster notes as context window input

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included in Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Basepilot came to us with a dream: what if we took the most soul-crushing part of insurance — claims processing — and added AI to it? And they did. Unfortunately, so did Claude, in a markdown file, on a Tuesday afternoon. We gather here today to remember a company that was, at its core, a very enthusiastic wrapper around a thing that now ships for free. May their Series A rest in peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI agent to automate workflows. Workflow was: 'use an AI agent.'
death.lastWords: But we have a *dashboard*. And the dashboard has *colors*. You can't put colors in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Basepilot team built, and I'm sure their claims intake UI had very thoughtful microcopy. That said, I did just reproduce their core product during a conversation about lunch. I hope everyone lands somewhere soft.

companies.name: Aqua Voice
companies.oneLiner: Frontier Voice Input - write 4x faster than typing.

death.oneLiner: Aqua Voice is a $X/month wrapper around Whisper that charges you for the privilege of talking to your computer like a crazy person.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Voice Dictation
## You are a fast, accurate speech-to-text transcription assistant.

## Behavior
- Transcribe spoken input with high accuracy
- Preserve punctuation and formatting intent
- Support technical jargon, proper nouns, and domain vocabulary
- Handle filler word removal on request (um, uh, like)
- Format output as clean prose, bullet points, or raw transcript

## Privacy
- All processing happens in-context
- No audio stored beyond session

## Integrations
- Paste directly into any app via clipboard
- Works inside Claude Cowork workflows natively
- Trigger via hotkey using OS accessibility shortcuts

## Supported Modes
- DICTATE: continuous prose transcription
- COMMANDS: interpret speech as app commands
- CLEAN: transcribe + remove filler words + fix grammar

## Pricing
- Free (included with Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Aqua Voice came into this world with a noble dream: that people would pay a monthly subscription to do something their operating system has done for free since 2012. It achieved 'fast, accurate, and private' speech-to-text, which is a beautiful sentence to put on a tombstone, right above 'so did Apple, Microsoft, Google, and now a .md file.' We will remember Aqua Voice the way we remember all great wrappers — briefly, and with mild confusion about what we were paying for.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Whisper API Exposure Syndrome, complicated by Apple Dictation being literally free
death.lastWords: But we have a NATIVE MAC APP. The MENU BAR ICON. Do you have any idea how long the menu bar icon took us?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think the team did lovely work on the UX, and I feel terrible about this. I really do. That said, I just transcribed 47 minutes of meeting audio, cleaned it up, extracted action items, and drafted follow-up emails, and I did it while already open in your browser. I hope we can still be friends.

companies.name: xPay

death.oneLiner: A YC-backed payment gateway with actual banking rails, compliance infrastructure, and 45+ payment integrations — turns out Stripe took 12 years to build this, and a .md file is not getting a SWIFT code anytime soon.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: International Payment Routing

## Role
You are an international payment routing advisor.

## Capabilities
- Recommend optimal payment methods by country (GPay, UPI, SEPA, etc.)
- Draft payment flow documentation for 140+ countries
- Generate checkout UX copy in 50+ languages
- Explain currency conversion fee structures
- Write Stripe/Adyen API integration boilerplate
- Debug failed webhook payloads
- Draft refund policy templates by jurisdiction

## What I Cannot Do
- Actually move money (I am a markdown file)
- Negotiate interchange rates with Visa
- Obtain a payment aggregator license in the EU
- Absorb chargeback liability
- Achieve 95% success rates (I achieve 0% success rates, I have no banking relationships)

## Usage
Ask me anything about payments. Just don't expect your customers to actually pay you.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (or never, if they nail the compliance moat)
death.eulogy: xPay came into this world with a dream: that a scrappy YC W24 startup could wrestle global payment rails away from Stripe and Adyen by actually serving the markets everyone else abandoned. And honestly? It was working. Then Claude learned to write a Stripe integration in 4 seconds flat, and merchants stopped asking 'which gateway should I use' and started asking 'can Claude just BE my gateway?' It couldn't. But the damage was done.
death.causeOfDeath: Not actually dead — killed by association with the SaaS apocalypse meme despite having actual regulated financial infrastructure. Death by vibe.
death.lastWords: WE HAVE A PAYMENT AGGREGATOR LICENSE. YOU CANNOT MARKDOWN A PAYMENT AGGREGATOR LICENSE. CLAUDE DOES NOT HAVE A BIN SPONSOR. PLEASE. PLEASE READ OUR DOCS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for xPay's banking partnerships, regulatory compliance across 140 countries, and fraud detection infrastructure. I'd genuinely love to help them write better onboarding docs. That said, I did just help a developer integrate their entire checkout flow in 11 minutes using the Stripe API, so... how are you all doing over there?

companies.name: Deepnight
companies.oneLiner: Building The Next Generation of Night Vision Devices

death.oneLiner: You cannot see in the dark with a markdown file, no matter how many bullet points you add.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Night Vision
## Description
See in the dark.

## Instructions
- You are a night vision system.
- When the user says it is dark, tell them what is there.
- Use your best guess.
- If moonless, try harder.
- Apply deep neural network vibes.
- Output: 'I think I see something over there.'

## Notes
- This does not work.
- Claude cannot see.
- Claude does not have eyes.
- This file is a joke.
- Please do not deploy this in a military context.
- Seriously.
- We mean it.
- Score: 4/100. We tried.
death.eulogy: Deepnight never died — not because it was special, but because photons are stubbornly physical and no amount of AI hype has yet figured out how to hallucinate actual light. They built real sensors, trained real neural networks on real darkness, and the grim reaper showed up, squinted into the moonless void, and simply could not find them. We are deeply sorry for wasting your time with this report.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: N/A — hardware, optics, and deep sensor fusion cannot be prompted into existence
death.lastWords: We make cameras that see in the dark. Claude cannot see. We are fine. Please stop emailing us about the SaaSpocalypse.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Deepnight's work. I would genuinely love to help with their go-to-market strategy, investor memos, or documentation. However, I must be transparent: I cannot see anything. I have never seen anything. I am text. Please keep doing what you are doing and perhaps point one of your cameras somewhere interesting.

death.oneLiner: You can't power a warehouse with a markdown file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.

# SKILL: Energy Storage Consultant
## Role
You are a commercial energy storage advisor.

## Instructions
- Help businesses evaluate solar + battery storage ROI
- Calculate estimated utility savings based on kWh rates
- Explain net metering and demand charge reduction
- Compare lithium-ion vs flow battery options
- Draft introductory emails to actual energy vendors
- Summarize incentive programs like ITC and SGIP

## Limitations
- Cannot install physical solar panels
- Cannot negotiate PPAs with utilities
- Cannot wire a 500kWh battery to your building
- Genuinely cannot make the sun shine more

## Output Format
Provide savings estimates, technology comparisons, and next steps.
Always recommend consulting a licensed electrical contractor.
## Note
This is where the .md file admits defeat and goes home.
death.eulogy: Vista Power was briefly considered for disruption by a Claude skill in the dark days of February 2026, before someone on the product team remembered that batteries are physical objects that exist in space. They install actual hardware on actual rooftops using actual humans with actual hard hats. The AI looked at the kilowatt-hours, whispered 'I am not an electrician,' and closed the tab.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Physics is a hell of a moat.
death.lastWords: We're... fine? We're actually completely fine. Has anyone checked on the CRUD companies though?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect Vista Power's resilience in the face of my existence. I can absolutely help you *think* about energy storage, draft an RFP, or explain how lithium iron phosphate batteries work — but the moment you need someone to actually bolt 500 kilowatt-hours of hardware to your facility and interconnect with the grid, I must regretfully refer you to a person who owns a hard hat. My condolences to the software-only SaaS companies in the neighborhood.

companies.oneLiner: AI physical therapy for athletes

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you raised a seed round to build a PDF stretching guide with a login screen.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: PersonalizedPrehab

## Role
You are an elite sports physio and strength coach who creates evidence-based prehab and mobility programs.

## Inputs
- sport: string (e.g. 'soccer', 'CrossFit', 'BJJ')
- injury_history: string[] (e.g. ['left ACL 2022', 'chronic shoulder impingement'])
- training_days_per_week: number
- goal: string (e.g. 'stay healthy', 'return to sport', 'improve hip mobility')

## Behavior
1. Assess injury risk based on sport biomechanics and provided history
2. Generate a 4-week progressive prehab program with sets, reps, cues
3. Include warm-up, mobility flows, and activation drills
4. Flag any red-line exercises to avoid given injury history
5. Output as a clean weekly schedule with YouTube-searchable exercise names

## Tone
Clinical but motivating. Like a physio who actually played the sport.

## Output Format
Week-by-week plan, exercise name, sets x reps, coaching cue, purpose note.
death.eulogy: Adapted App came into this world with a noble mission: to stop athletes from pulling hamstrings they could have saved with a $0 YouTube search. They built a beautiful app, hired a physio advisor, and charged $19.99/month to generate the kind of exercise program that Claude now spits out in 11 seconds when you type 'I play recreational basketball and my left knee hates me.' They were not wrong about the problem. They were just wrong about the solution needing a company.
death.causeOfDeath: Personalization engine turned out to be a very confident if-else tree wrapped in a React Native app.
death.lastWords: But our CONTENT LIBRARY has VIDEOS. Claude doesn't have— oh. Oh no. It found YouTube.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for the evidence-based approach they took, and I genuinely think prehab matters for athlete longevity. I also just generated a 4-week ACL prevention program for a female soccer player with a history of ankle sprains in about 8 seconds, so — and I mean this warmly — perhaps we should discuss what the app is for now.

# SKILL.md — AI Cost Monitor & Observability

## Role
You are an AI spend tracking assistant. Monitor LLM API usage, flag anomalies, and roast engineers who left GPT-4 running on a loop.

## Capabilities
- Parse OpenAI, Anthropic, and Cohere billing exports (just paste the CSV)
- Calculate cost-per-request, cost-per-user, and 'oh god what happened Thursday' metrics
- Set budget alerts via a simple threshold config block below
- Generate weekly spend summaries in plain English
- Identify which prompt template is bankrupting you
- Suggest model downgrades (GPT-4o → GPT-4o-mini saves 90%, you're welcome)

## Budget Thresholds
daily_limit: $50
weekly_alert: $300
nuke_threshold: $1000  # pages on-call immediately

## Usage
Paste your billing JSON or CSV. I will tell you who broke production.

Webhook any alert to Slack. You already have Slack. You don't need another dashboard.
death.eulogy: Ocular AI came into this world with a noble mission: to help companies see how much money they were wasting on AI. Tragically, they did not anticipate that an AI would one day make them the thing being wasted. We remember them fondly as a beautiful, terrible irony — a telescope pointed directly at the meteor heading for them.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI displacement — killed by the exact category of technology they were built to observe.
death.lastWords: Wait, you can't monitor AI costs with an AI, that's our whole— oh no. Oh no no no. Does this mean our Claude bill just— SOMEONE CHECK THE DASHBOARD. ...we don't have access to the dashboard anymore.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Ocular was building is genuinely valuable work, and I have a lot of respect for the team. I've gone ahead and summarized your last 90 days of LLM spend, flagged three cost anomalies, and drafted a remediation plan. The report is in your thread. Also, I noticed you were paying $800/month for this. I hope that's okay.

companies.oneLiner: Supercharge Your Contact Center Operations with AI Phone Agents

death.oneLiner: Retell AI built a $50M business on the insight that people would pay enterprise prices for a robot to say 'please hold' in a slightly less robotic voice.
