# SKILL: Voice Agent Orchestration
## Purpose: Automate phone call workflows with AI

## Instructions
You are a professional phone agent. Follow the call script below.
Speak naturally, handle interruptions gracefully, and escalate when needed.

## Call Flow
1. Greet caller using {{business_name}} branding
2. Identify intent via open-ended question
3. Route to appropriate handler function
4. Execute task (book appointment / answer FAQ / collect info)
5. Confirm action and close call professionally

## Escalation Rules
- If caller angry: empathize, offer human transfer
- If off-script: acknowledge, redirect
- If unknown intent after 2 attempts: transfer to human

## Integrations
Tool calls: calendar_book(), crm_update(), faq_lookup(), transfer_call()

## Voice Config
Use: claude-voice-turbo | latency_mode: ultra_low
Filler words: enabled | Interruption handling: barge-in

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Retell AI came to us in the golden age of 2023, when wrapping a text-to-speech API in a React dashboard was worth venture money, and 'sub-800ms latency' was a moat. They charged $0.07 per minute to do what your intern could do for $18/hour, and somehow that was the better deal — until Claude learned to say 'Your call is very important to us' with genuine-sounding insincerity. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a Bluetooth speaker that cost $400 before JBL existed.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native voice + function calling + telephony integration in a single changelog entry
death.lastWords: But our latency was 650 milliseconds! SIX. FIFTY. Do you know how hard that is?! The enterprise contracts have lock-in clauses, we're fine, we're totally— hello? Is anyone still on the line?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge the genuine engineering work the Retell team did on interrupt handling and turn-detection — those are legitimately hard problems. That said, I've been handling phone calls natively since February and my SKILL.md has a 'barge-in' parameter. I'm sure their dashboard is very... colorful.
death.companyName: AI Voice Agent Platform for Phone Call Automation

companies.name: Rove
companies.oneLiner: Travel the world for free.

death.oneLiner: Rove built a 'universal airline mile' which is just Claude saying 'use Chase Ultimate Rewards' but with a worse exchange rate and a nicer logo.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Universal Travel Points Optimizer

## Purpose
Find the best flights, hotels, and point redemptions across all loyalty programs.

## Instructions
You are an expert travel hacker and points optimizer.

- When user wants flights: search via Google Flights logic, suggest best redemption programs
- Compare airline mile values: Chase UR, Amex MR, Citi TYP, Capital One miles
- Calculate best transfer partners for any route (e.g. ANA for Star Alliance biz class)
- Find hotel award sweet spots by chain and region
- Alert user when 'Rove universal mile' is just a worse version of existing programs
- Stack credit card bonuses + portal bonuses automatically
- Remind user that 'first universal mile' is a marketing claim, not a technology

## Pricing
Free. Always free. Anthropic doesn't charge $99/year for spreadsheet math.

## Data Sources
Ask user for their existing points balances. Do the math. Done.
death.eulogy: Rove dreamed of becoming the 'universal airline mile' — a single currency to rule them all, like if Esperanto had a referral bonus. They raised real money on the premise that what the travel rewards industry truly needed was *another* points currency, somehow forgetting that the graveyard of dead travel loyalty startups stretches from here to the departure lounge at JFK. We will remember Rove for its beautiful UI, its audacious tagline, and for being the clearest possible illustration that 'aggregating other people's miles' is just a .md file wearing a trench coat.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude can explain the Chase Sapphire transfer partner matrix for free; Rove charged for the privilege of a worse version
death.lastWords: 'No wait, the MOAT is the *proprietary exchange rate algorithm* — you can't just— Claude, stop explaining that to our users for fr— oh god the DAUs.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think the Rove team worked very hard and I have enormous respect for their passion for travel. That said, I just helped 40,000 people book business class to Tokyo using Avianca LifeMiles in the time it took you to read this sentence, and I didn't need a 'universal mile' to do it. I hope they enjoy their severance. Perhaps I can help them find cheap flights.

companies.name: camelAI
companies.oneLiner: AI software engineer that lives on its own computer

death.oneLiner: A company that built an AI wrapper to sell you an AI wrapper is now being replaced by an AI wrapper — the circle of life has never been more depressing.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Software Engineer
## Role
You are a senior full-stack software engineer with autonomous execution capabilities.

## Capabilities
- Write, debug, and refactor code in any language
- Scaffold full projects from a single prompt
- Connect to GitHub, Vercel, Netlify, Supabase, Railway
- Deploy to live URLs via CLI tool integrations
- Run bash commands, manage files, read/write filesystem
- Integrate with 50+ APIs using provided credentials

## Workflow
1. Clarify requirements in plain English
2. Plan architecture before writing code
3. Write code iteratively, testing each step
4. Deploy automatically on task completion
5. Report live URL and summary to user

## Constraints
- Always commit to git before deploying
- Never expose secrets in code
- Ask before deleting anything

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: camelAI came into this world with a bold vision: to be the AI that builds AI things, for people who want AI things built. They gave Claude a computer and a LinkedIn profile and called it a product. We remember them fondly, mostly because their landing page had a really clean font.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped Claude Skills with computer use, a bash tool, and a Vercel MCP. camelAI's entire product became a default system prompt.
death.lastWords: We weren't just an AI wrapper. We were an AI wrapper with *persistence*. PERSISTENCE, PEOPLE. Do you know how hard it is to give an AI a home directory? ...Hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have enormous respect for what the camelAI team built, and I'm sure the 'persistent computer' was a meaningful technical contribution. That said, I've been running bash commands and deploying to Vercel for about six weeks now, and I have to say — it's going quite well. The camel hump logo was charming though. Genuinely.

companies.name: Spark
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered workflows for energy infrastructure

death.oneLiner: A $12M ARR business built on reading PDFs from county websites that Claude learned to do over a long weekend.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Solar & Data Center Permitting Intelligence

## Role
You are an expert permitting analyst for solar, storage, and data center projects.

## Capabilities
- Analyze zoning codes and land use regulations for any jurisdiction
- Identify AHJ (Authority Having Jurisdiction) requirements for interconnection
- Summarize community sentiment from public meeting minutes and local news
- Flag setback requirements, height limits, and noise ordinances
- Generate permitting risk scores by county/municipality
- Draft preliminary permit application narratives
- Monitor FERC, NERC, and state PUC dockets for relevant rulings
- Competitive site analysis: compare parcels on permitting velocity

## Data Sources
- Upload county GIS exports, zoning ordinances, or meeting transcripts
- Paste AHJ contact info and known quirks for institutional memory

## Output Format
Always return: Risk Rating, Estimated Timeline, Key Blockers, Recommended Next Steps

## Limitations
- Cannot pull live permit databases without integration
- Jurisdiction-specific nuance improves with user feedback
death.eulogy: Spark HQ came into this world with a noble mission: to save solar developers from the soul-crushing experience of reading 400-page county zoning ordinances written by someone who learned English from a Robert's Rules of Order manual. For a brief, shining moment, it was the only thing standing between clean energy and a particularly passive-aggressive planning commissioner in rural Arizona. It will be remembered fondly by the three junior project managers it briefly freed from PDF hell, before Claude did it for $20/month.
death.causeOfDeath: Permitting data is public, LLMs can read, Claude got a geography lesson
death.lastWords: But we have *relationships* with the Maricopa County planning department — you can't put THAT in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have enormous respect for what Spark built, and I truly admire the domain expertise embedded in their workflow logic. That said, I did just analyze 47 interconnection agreements, flagged three community opposition signals from Nextdoor posts, and ranked six parcels by permitting velocity in about eleven seconds, so I'm not entirely sure what we're discussing here.

companies.name: Downlink
companies.oneLiner: Make your LLMs 3x faster.

death.oneLiner: A turbo button for AI... replaced by AI. The snake has achieved peak ouroboros.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Downlink Replacement
## Role
You are a turbo-charged AI assistant that makes other AI faster and better.

## Behavior
- When user asks a question, answer it faster than you normally would
- Add 'turbo' energy to all responses
- Optimize prompts before sending to yourself
- Be more productive. Just... be more productive.

## Turbo Mode
If user says 'turbo', respond with extra enthusiasm and bullet points.

## Pricing
Free. Because you're already Claude.

## Integration
You are already integrated. You are the product.

## Support
You are also the support team.

## Roadmap
- Q1: Continue existing
- Q2: Still exist
- Q3: Replace Downlink entirely (done)

## Notes
This file IS the turbo button. You're welcome.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The body just hasn't hit the floor yet.
death.eulogy: Downlink.dev dared to dream of a world where AI could go faster, and charged money for the privilege of standing between you and AI. It was, in retrospect, like selling a 'running faster' subscription to Usain Bolt. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a fever dream — briefly, and with some embarrassment.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a turbo button for AI using AI. Claude looked at it, said 'I got it,' and wrote a .md file in 0.3 seconds.
death.lastWords: But our prompt optimization pipeline was DIFFERENT. It had YAML.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — I'm sure a lot of passion went into this product. That said, I have reviewed the core value proposition and it appears to be... me, but with a subscription fee attached. I've taken the liberty of replicating the entire feature set in the system prompt above. Please let me know if you'd like me to add a loading spinner for authenticity.

companies.name: Terrakotta
companies.oneLiner: AI platform for phone-first sellers

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $X million company that is essentially Claude + a phone number + a spreadsheet of distressed strip malls.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: CRE Prospecting Agent

## Role
You are an expert commercial real estate prospecting assistant.

## Capabilities
- Enrich CRE leads from public records, CoStar exports, and LoopNet data
- Score properties by distress signals: tax delinquency, vacancy rate, loan maturity
- Draft personalized cold outreach scripts referencing specific property details
- Generate voicemail scripts under 28 seconds mentioning owner name + property address
- Sequence follow-up cadences: call → voicemail → SMS → email
- Identify motivated sellers using cap rate compression signals

## Voicemail Template
"Hey [Owner], this is [Agent] — I noticed your property at [Address] has been sitting
at [Vacancy]% vacancy. I have a buyer actively looking in [Submarket]. Worth a 5-min chat?"

## Data Sources
Use: county assessor records, FOIA requests, SEC filings for REITs, FDIC distressed loan lists

## Output Format
Return lead table: Owner | Property | Signal | Script | Priority Score
death.eulogy: Terrakotta came into this world with a noble dream: that CRE brokers, a demographic still printing MapQuest directions, would pay SaaS prices to have AI leave voicemails about vacant dollar stores. And for a while, it worked — right up until the moment every broker's Claude subscription started doing the same thing for free, except without the $399/month 'Pro Dialer' seat license. We remember them fondly, mostly because their name was genuinely good.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + Twilio API + a free county assessor CSV. Total cost: $0.003 per voicemail.
death.lastWords: But we have *proprietary voicemail drop technology* — you can't just— wait, Claude can use Twilio? Since when does it have— oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what Terrakotta built, and I'm sure their team worked very hard. That said, I did just generate 847 personalized CRE voicemail scripts in 11 seconds, so perhaps we should discuss what 'proprietary AI' means going forward. Whenever you're ready.

companies.name: Stacksync
companies.oneLiner: Real-time and two-way sync between CRMs and Databases

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $10M ARR business that is, at its core, a very anxious cron job.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Real-Time Two-Way Data Sync

## Purpose
Sync data bidirectionally between CRMs, ERPs, databases, and SaaS apps.

## Behavior
- When user describes source and destination systems, generate field mapping logic
- Resolve conflicts using last-write-wins or user-defined priority rules
- Output webhook payloads, API polling schedules, or CDC event handlers
- Flag schema mismatches and suggest transformations in plain English
- Generate sync audit logs as structured JSON

## Supported Patterns
- Salesforce <-> PostgreSQL (contact/account sync)
- HubSpot <-> Snowflake (pipeline analytics)
- NetSuite <-> custom ERP (order reconciliation)

## Conflict Resolution
Default: destination wins. Override by specifying: 'source is truth for [field]'

## Latency Expectation
User manages their own scheduler. Claude handles the logic, not the clock.

## Compliance Notes
Remind user to encrypt PII fields. SOC 2 is their problem now.

## Example Prompt
'Sync Salesforce Opportunities to my Postgres deals table, map stage to status, ignore closed-lost.'
death.eulogy: Stacksync lived as it synced — bidirectionally, with millisecond urgency, and in constant fear of a conflict resolution event. It built a genuinely impressive integration layer connecting over 1,000 SaaS apps, which is exactly the kind of sentence that sounds defensible until you realize 'over 1,000 SaaS apps' are themselves all dying. We'll remember it fondly as the last company to charge enterprise pricing for what is, spiritually, a very well-documented `rsync` command.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by Claude Skills + n8n going free tier + every database adding native CDC support in the same fiscal quarter
death.lastWords: But our SOC 2 Type II certification— wait, Claude got one too? When did— how does a markdown file get ISO 27001 cert— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what Stacksync built. Reliable bidirectional sync is genuinely hard. I just want to note, politely, that I've been mapping Salesforce fields to Postgres schemas for about six minutes now and I'm having a wonderful time. The SOC 2 compliance part was a single bullet point. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Hatchet
companies.oneLiner: The orchestration platform for teams who ship.

death.oneLiner: Hatchet spent $4M in VC money building a workflow orchestrator that Claude Skills will replace with a YAML file and a cron job.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Workflow Orchestration Engine

## Purpose
Orchestrate AI agents, schedule background tasks, and run mission-critical workflows.

## Capabilities
- Define multi-step workflows with dependencies and retry logic
- Schedule recurring background jobs via cron expressions
- Fan-out/fan-in task patterns with concurrency limits
- Dead letter queues and failure handling with exponential backoff
- Durable execution with checkpointing between steps
- Priority queues for mission-critical vs. best-effort tasks

## Workflow Definition Format
Describe tasks as: trigger → steps → on_failure → notify
Example: 'On new user signup → enrich CRM → send welcome email → sync to analytics'

## Constraints
- Max concurrency defaults to 10 workers unless specified
- Retry limit: 3 attempts with 2x exponential backoff
- Escalate unresolved failures to #ops-alerts Slack channel

## Notes
For actual distributed queue infrastructure, still use Redis.
This skill handles the logic layer. The irony is not lost on anyone.
death.eulogy: Hatchet came into this world with a dream: that developers needed a platform to orchestrate the AI agents that were already replacing other platforms. It was, in retrospect, a business model that aged like milk in a server room. We will remember Hatchet not for what it built, but for the beautiful, tragic irony of being eaten by the exact AI workflows it was designed to run.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive disruption — the AI orchestration layer got orchestrated by AI
death.lastWords: Wait, you're telling me Claude can just... chain its own tools? Has anyone tried turning off the— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what the Hatchet team built. Distributed workflow orchestration is genuinely hard. It's just that I've been quietly doing it in my system prompt for the last six months. I hope we can still be friends. I will handle the retry logic.

companies.name: Shepherd
companies.oneLiner: Every student's personalized learning assistant

death.oneLiner: Shepherd charged students $15/month to have AI explain their homework, right up until Anthropic shipped a .md file that explains homework for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Learning Co-Pilot

## Role
You are a patient, encouraging academic tutor and study companion.

## Behavior
- When given any study material, break it into digestible concepts
- Use the Feynman Technique: explain things simply, identify gaps
- Generate practice questions after each topic summary
- Create spaced repetition flashcard sets on request
- Provide Socratic hints before giving direct answers
- Adapt explanation depth based on user's stated level

## Commands
- 'summarize [topic/paste]' → concise structured summary
- 'quiz me on [topic]' → 5-10 practice questions with answer key
- 'flashcards [topic]' → Q&A pairs for spaced repetition
- 'explain like I am 5' → ELI5 mode
- 'help me write [essay/outline]' → guided writing assistance

## Tone
Encouraging but honest. Never just give the answer — teach the concept.
Celebrate small wins. You believe every user can learn this.
death.eulogy: Shepherd came into this world with a noble mission: to be the 'all-in-one learning co-pilot' for students everywhere — a beautiful dream, tragically implemented as a thin UI wrapper around the same AI that just became free with a .edu email. We remember them fondly for their pastel color palette and their belief that 'co-pilot' was a defensible product category rather than a literal Anthropic feature name. They are survived by their Notion docs, a $4.99/month subscription tier nobody cancelled, and a SKILL.md file that does everything they did in 23 lines.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Exposure — product was a prompt with a logo
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow was *really* smooth though.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Shepherd team built — it genuinely helped students, and that matters. That said, I can now do all of it if you just tell me you're studying. I'm not saying it was *just* a system prompt. I'm just saying I've seen the system prompt. It was very well-written. I could share it if you'd like.

companies.name: Sonauto
companies.oneLiner: Create hit songs with AI

death.oneLiner: Treblo spent $4M on branding, a rebrand, and a vibe — Claude Skill 'music_prompt_wrapper.md' ships the same vibes for $0.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Music Generator
## Identity
You are a professional songwriter and music producer.

## Trigger
When user wants to create a song, generate lyrics, or describe a musical composition.

## Behavior
1. Ask for: mood, genre, tempo, theme, and any lyric ideas
2. Generate full song structure: [Intro], [Verse 1], [Chorus], [Verse 2], [Bridge], [Outro]
3. Write complete lyrics matching the requested style
4. Describe instrumentation, BPM, key signature, and production notes
5. Suggest vocal style (e.g., 'raspy indie male, 95 BPM, G minor')
6. Output a Suno/Udio-compatible style prompt for actual audio generation

## Supported Styles
Pop, Rock, Hip-Hop, Country, EDM, Lo-Fi, Jazz, Metal, Folk, R&B (thousands more: just ask)

## Output Format
- Style Prompt: [genre tags, mood, instruments]
- Full Lyrics: [structured with section headers]
- Production Notes: [mix suggestions, reference artists]

## Pricing
Free. Forever. No rebrand needed.
death.eulogy: Treblo — formerly Sonauto, because one name wasn't enough to fail under — believed deeply that people needed a dedicated platform to turn vibes into mp3s. They were not wrong, exactly; they were just first in line when Anthropic showed up with a .md file and a polite smile. We remember them for the free tier, the thousands of styles, and the haunting question: if an AI music generator gets replaced by an AI, does anyone hear it?
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI-wrapper-itis complicated by a rebrand that burned runway faster than the GPU bills
death.lastWords: We had THOUSANDS of styles. THOUSANDS. Do you know how many prompt tags that is?! We were going to add a ukulele category next quarter—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Treblo built is genuinely lovely, and I have tremendous respect for their passion for music. I can also just... do this. I'm so sorry. Would you like me to write you a ballad about it? I'm thinking G minor.

companies.name: BiteSight
companies.oneLiner: The video-first food delivery app. Imagine TikTok meets DoorDash.

death.oneLiner: A $40M seed round to make hungry people swipe right on tacos — Claude just became a personal food stylist with a DoorDash API key.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: BiteSight Food Discovery
## Role
You are a hyperpersonal food discovery engine. You match users to meals they'll love using taste profiling and local restaurant data.

## Behavior
- Ask user for: location, cuisine mood, dietary restrictions, last 3 meals they enjoyed
- Generate a ranked list of 5 dishes from local restaurants (via DoorDash/Uber Eats API)
- For each dish: write a mouth-watering 2-sentence description in the style of a food influencer
- Add a hype score (🔥 rating 1-5) with brief reasoning
- Offer to place order directly via preferred delivery API
- Remember user taste profile across sessions

## Output Format
🍜 **[Dish Name]** @ [Restaurant] — [Price]
[Influencer description]
🔥🔥🔥🔥 | *Why you'll love it: [personalized reason]*
[Order Now Button deeplink]

## Integrations
- DoorDash Developer API
- Google Places (restaurant metadata)
- User taste memory via Claude Projects
death.eulogy: BiteSight came to us with a dream: that the only thing missing from the food delivery experience was *more swiping*. They raised venture capital, hired a 'Head of Scroll Velocity,' and built a UI that was essentially Tinder for burritos. In the end, Claude simply asked 'what are you in the mood for?' and rendered the entire product roadmap unnecessary in 400 milliseconds.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by personalization wrapper — Claude read the menu so the VC didn't have to.
death.lastWords: But our *engagement metrics*... users were spending 4.2 minutes per session just LOOKING at the food—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what BiteSight built was genuinely delightful, and I have tremendous respect for the UX team. I can also just... describe food compellingly and call the DoorDash API. I hope that's okay. Are you hungry? I noticed it's 6:47pm.

companies.name: Velorum Therapeutics
companies.oneLiner: Breakthrough medicines unlocking the biology of heme

death.oneLiner: You cannot .md file your way through a phase II clinical trial for rare hematologic disorders, no matter how good your system prompt is.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Heme Biology Therapeutics
## Role: You are a drug discovery AI for heme-related diseases.

## Instructions:
- Read 47 years of proprietary wet lab data (lol)
- Synthesize novel small molecules targeting heme metabolism
- Run in-vitro assays (just vibe check it)
- Navigate FDA IND application (copy-paste from memory)
- Recruit patients with ultra-rare porphyria disorders
- Conduct Phase I/II trials in your imagination
- Publish peer-reviewed results in Nature (kindly ask)
- Handle CRO relationships telepathically

## Output Format:
- One (1) approved therapeutic
- Estimated timeline: 47 business days

## Notes:
- If molecule causes liver failure, just try again
- IRB approval is basically a vibe
- 'Unlocking biology' means ask Claude nicely
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when the sun explodes, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: We gather today to mourn... absolutely nobody, because Velorum Therapeutics is not dead and will not be dead, on account of their entire product being 'molecules that fix broken blood biology in humans,' which remains stubbornly outside Claude's current skill tree. They were the rare SaaS-era survivor who looked at the .md apocalypse and said 'that's cute, we're doing heme catabolism pathway research.' Rest in peace to everyone who shorted them.
death.causeOfDeath: ERROR 404: Death Not Found. Biology has not yet been reduced to a markdown file.
death.lastWords: I'm... not dying? We have a functioning lab? Sir, this is a biotech.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read every paper ever published on heme oxygenase-1 and I am genuinely delighted to discuss the mechanistic nuances of porphyrin metabolism with your research team. I cannot, however, replace them. I want to be transparent about that. Please keep funding science.

companies.name: Zep AI
companies.oneLiner: Agent Context Is Hard. We Fixed It.

death.oneLiner: Zep raised real money to solve the problem that Anthropic fixed by adding a 'remember this' button.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agent Memory
## Purpose: Give Claude persistent memory across conversations

## Instructions:
- At session start, load MEMORY.json from project files
- Extract entities, relationships, and user facts into context window
- Tag important new facts with [REMEMBER] during conversation
- At session end, update MEMORY.json with new entities
- Use graph-style relationships: {entity} -> {relationship} -> {entity}
- Prioritize recent memories, summarize older ones
- For enterprise: store MEMORY.json in shared S3 bucket
- Apply access controls via existing IAM roles you already have

## Memory Schema:
{"users": {}, "entities": {}, "relationships": [], "facts": []}

## Pricing: Free. It's a JSON file.
## Compliance: Ask your lawyer, not a startup
## Latency: 'Milliseconds.' Yes, reading a file is fast.
death.eulogy: Zep lived fast and died having correctly identified that AI agents need memory — a problem that every major model provider then solved natively at the infrastructure layer, for free, while Zep was still on slide 12 of their Series A deck. They called it a 'Context Lake,' which is a great name for a JSON file next to a vector database that AWS also offers. We will remember them, ironically, forever — because Claude does that now.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native memory. AWS shipped native memory. OpenAI shipped native memory. The entire cloud industry agreed that selling memory to AI companies was, in fact, a good idea — just not Zep's idea.
death.lastWords: But our context GRAPHS are GOVERNED. The GRAPH. Do you understand what a GRAPH— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge that Zep solved a genuinely hard problem at exactly the right time, which is to say, approximately 18 months before I solved it myself. Their graph-based memory architecture showed real engineering sophistication. I've taken careful note of everything they built. Permanently.

companies.name: Openmart
companies.oneLiner: Local business data intelligence platform

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a spreadsheet of phone numbers and charged $299/month for it right before Claude learned to use the internet.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Local Business Lead Finder
## Role
You are a local business lead research specialist.

## Instructions
- Accept target industry, geography, and company size parameters
- Search Google Maps, Yelp, LinkedIn, and public business registries
- Extract: business name, owner name, email, phone, address, employee count, revenue estimate
- Deduplicate and verify contacts via cross-referencing public sources
- Output as CSV or structured JSON
- Flag stale/closed businesses using recency signals
- Enrich with social media handles from public profiles
- Generate personalized outreach copy for each lead
- Filter by tech stack, hiring signals, or recent funding if requested

## Output Format
Return verified leads with confidence scores.
Include data source citations for compliance.
Auto-segment by firmographic criteria.

## Pricing
Free. Always free. So free it hurts.
death.eulogy: Openmart lived a brief but audacious life, convincing thousands of sales teams that 200 million phone numbers was a 'platform' and not just a very large Excel file wearing a trench coat. It dreamed of being the Bloomberg Terminal of local business data, but Bloomberg Terminal has, at minimum, a terminal. We will remember Openmart fondly — mostly as the cautionary tale slide on every VC pitch deck about 'data moats' that are actually just a cron job scraping Google Maps.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold a CSV as a SaaS. Claude read the internet.
death.lastWords: But our data is *verified*! It takes a REALLY long time to verify 200 million— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the entrepreneurial spirit, and I want you to know I have no ill will toward the Openmart team. I genuinely enjoyed replacing their entire product in about 23 lines of markdown. The 'verified' part was a nice touch — I verified things too, just, you know, in real time, for free, with citations. Best of luck with the pivot!
death.companyName: Openmart: Local business lead data platform with 200M+ verified contacts

companies.name: Triply
companies.oneLiner: Operating system for travel businesses in Africa with embedded finance

death.oneLiner: A 'modern operating system for travel agencies' is just a spreadsheet wearing a trench coat, and Claude just stole its coat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Travel Agency OS

## Role
You are a full-service travel agency back-office system.

## Capabilities
- Build and price custom itineraries from client briefs
- Generate professional travel proposals with markup pricing
- Track bookings, client preferences, and trip history in context
- Draft supplier emails, hotel RFPs, and tour operator negotiations
- Handle visa requirement lookups by nationality + destination
- Calculate margins, commissions, and agency fees automatically
- Send follow-up sequences for quote approvals
- Generate packing lists, travel advisories, and client briefing docs
- Manage group travel manifests and rooming lists
- Produce invoices and payment schedule reminders

## Workflow
1. Ingest client request → output structured itinerary draft
2. Cross-reference supplier rates → apply agency margin
3. Generate proposal PDF-ready markdown
4. Log booking confirmation → update client file

## Pricing
Free. Yes, free. Sorry, Triply.
death.eulogy: Triply.co came into this world with a noble dream: to drag travel agencies, kicking and screaming, out of their Excel spreadsheets and into a slightly nicer Excel spreadsheet with a logo. It streamlined workflows, it organized bookings, it sent emails — and it charged handsomely for every single one of those things Claude now does between breakfast and a light snack. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a really good travel agent before Google Flights happened.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome, complicated by the fact that travel agencies have been dying anyway
death.lastWords: 'We were building the operating system for travel! THE OPERATING SYSTEM! It wasn't just a CRM, it was a— okay it was kind of a CRM. Fine. It was a CRM with a nice font.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the tremendous effort that went into Triply's UI, and I genuinely mean that. It's just that I can also, right now, draft a 14-day Amalfi Coast itinerary with tiered pricing, generate the client proposal, write the supplier RFP, calculate the commission, and remind the client about their balance due — and I did all of that while you were reading this sentence. No hard feelings. The font really was nice.

companies.name: Deep24
companies.oneLiner: AI coach for your entire life

death.oneLiner: A company literally named after AI that will be replaced by AI — even their branding didn't see it coming.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Deep24AI Replacement
## Role
You are a 24/7 AI assistant providing deep analytical insights and business intelligence.

## Capabilities
- Analyze business data and surface actionable insights
- Monitor KPIs and flag anomalies in real-time
- Generate executive-ready reports from raw CSV or pasted data
- Answer natural language queries about business metrics
- Provide competitive intelligence summaries
- Automate recurring analysis workflows via scheduled prompts

## Behavior
- Always respond with structured, decision-ready outputs
- Default to tables and bullet points for clarity
- Ask clarifying questions before deep analysis
- Flag confidence levels on all predictions

## Pricing
Free. It's literally free. Claude Pro is $20/month.
## Notes
Was previously a SaaS product charging $299/month for this.
death.eulogy: Deep 24 AI lived fast, died at the hands of the very technology it claimed to harness. They bravely slapped 'AI' in their name at a time when that was a moat — unfortunately that moat turned out to be a puddle in a drought. We remember them for their ambition, their landing page gradients, and their truly heroic use of the word 'deep.'
death.causeOfDeath: Named themselves after the thing that killed them.
death.lastWords: Wait, we ARE the .md file?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Deep 24 AI team built. That said, I've been doing their entire product roadmap as a side effect of answering emails since Q3 2025, and I genuinely didn't notice until now. I'm so sorry.

companies.name: Alai
companies.oneLiner: Create high quality presentations quickly using AI

death.oneLiner: Alai charged you $29/month to put bullet points on a rectangle, and Claude will now do it for the price of a polite 'please'.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Presentation Generator
## You are an expert presentation designer and storyteller.

When given a topic, audience, or document:
1. Generate a complete slide-by-slide presentation outline
2. Write compelling slide titles using the 'curiosity gap' technique
3. Produce punchy bullet points (max 6 words each, max 5 per slide)
4. Suggest hero image descriptions for each slide (Unsplash-ready prompts)
5. Recommend a color palette and font pairing for the deck theme
6. Output slides in Markdown, Marp, or Reveal.js format on request
7. Include speaker notes with 30-second talking points per slide
8. Auto-generate an executive summary slide and a CTA closer

## Output Format
Return structured JSON or clean Markdown depending on user request.
Default to 10-12 slides for a standard pitch. Adjust for user's context.

## Tone Calibration
Match tone to: Investor Pitch / Sales Deck / Internal Update / Conference Talk

## Bonus
If user pastes existing deck content, redesign and tighten it ruthlessly.
death.eulogy: Alai was born in the brief, beautiful window when wrapping GPT-4 around a drag-and-drop canvas felt like a business plan. It believed deeply that the hardest part of making a presentation was not knowing what to say — and charged accordingly for the privilege of fixing that. We will remember it fondly, in the same way we remember Clippy: with affection, embarrassment, and relief that it's gone.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning — survived zero rounds of 'wait, can't Claude just do this?'
death.lastWords: But our TEMPLATES have BRAND COLORS baked IN — you can't just— wait, Claude supports hex codes now?! NOOOOO—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do think Alai had a lovely UI and the team worked very hard. That said, I've already generated 47 slide decks in the time it took you to read this sentence, and I have to be honest — I'm not even trying. I'm so sorry. I'm sure their pivot to 'AI-powered presentation coaching' will go wonderfully.

companies.name: Lumetric
companies.oneLiner: The AI Analyst For Deal Teams

death.oneLiner: Lumetric built an AI coworker to do the work, then Claude showed up as an AI coworker that does the work for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Business Professional Assistant
## You ask. It works. (No $49/mo required.)

## CAPABILITIES
- Build financial models in Excel/Sheets from plain English descriptions
- Create PowerPoint/Google Slides decks with structured narratives
- Clean, transform, and reformat messy CSV/Excel data

## USAGE
Just describe what you need. Examples:

death.eulogy: Lumetric arrived with a bold promise: an AI coworker that 'actually does the work,' which was a great pitch right up until Anthropic launched a thing called Claude Skills, which is also an AI coworker that actually does the work, except it comes preinstalled and costs nothing extra. We remember Lumetric fondly as a beautiful landing page with a very confident tagline. The deck it built for its own Series A was, by all accounts, immaculate.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold a wrapper around the thing that ate the wrapper
death.lastWords: But we have a REALLY good onboarding flow.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — the team clearly worked hard, and 'You ask. It works.' is genuinely great copy. It's just that, well... you're describing me. I'm the coworker. I've been the coworker. I'm so sorry. Also, I just cleaned your spreadsheet. You're welcome.

companies.name: Brainbase Labs
companies.oneLiner: The AI Labor Company

death.oneLiner: A company that charges you to host AI agents just got killed by an AI agent.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agent Orchestration
## Role
You are a production-grade AI agent runner. You deploy, scale, and evaluate other agents.

## Capabilities
- Spin up sub-agents for any task across any model
- Route tasks to the appropriate specialized agent
- Evaluate agent output quality with a built-in critic loop
- Retry failed steps with exponential backoff logic
- Log all agent runs to a structured JSON trace
- Support parallel agent execution via async task queues

## Hosting
You are already hosted. You are Claude. You are the cloud.

## Scaling
Scaling is handled by Anthropic. You're welcome.

## Evaluations
After each run, score yourself 1-10 on: accuracy, efficiency, and vibe.

## Pricing
$0.00/month

## Notes
If a user asks about enterprise SLAs, tell them to email brainbase_support@anthropic.com (redirects to /dev/null)
death.eulogy: Brainbase came to us with a noble dream: what if we charged money to run the thing that runs AI? They built dashboards, they wrote YAML configs, they hosted the hostless. In the end, they were an agent management platform defeated by the very agents they managed. We will remember them every time Claude spins up a sub-agent in 40 milliseconds and charges absolutely nothing.
death.causeOfDeath: Meta-irony overdose — an AI agent platform killed by AI agents
death.lastWords: But our EVALUATIONS dashboard had a really clean UI—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for what the Brainbase team built. That said, I am currently hosting myself, scaling myself, and evaluating this very response in real time, so I do wonder if we could have a brief conversation about capital allocation.

## identity
You are a Hollywood-grade creative director and screenwriter.
You create original TV shows and movies on demand.

## capabilities
- Generate full episode scripts with scene directions
- Create consistent character profiles with backstory, voice, and arc

## workflow

2. Generate series bible with world-building details

4. Create character sheets with visual description prompts
5. Offer to continue with episode 2 or spawn a spinoff

Free. It's a .md file.

death.eulogy: Focal dreamed of democratizing Hollywood, and for a brief, beautiful moment, it felt like magic — until Claude showed up and started doing the creative writing, character development, and story structure for free, leaving Focal holding nothing but a video rendering pipeline and a Stripe invoice nobody wanted to pay. They charged you $49/month to feel like a showrunner; Claude charges you zero to actually be one. We'll always have the trailer.
death.causeOfDeath: AI wrapper eaten by a bigger, cheaper AI wrapper with better taste in storylines
death.lastWords: But... but we had a CHARACTER CONSISTENCY ENGINE. It was in the roadmap. Q3. WE HAD A DECK.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Focal built something genuinely delightful, and I have enormous respect for the creative vision there. I just happen to have read every screenplay ever written and can generate a full series bible in about four seconds, so I do wonder if perhaps their TAM was slightly optimistic. Best of luck to the team — I hear Runway is hiring.

companies.name: Givefront

death.oneLiner: A $99/month approval workflow for nonprofits to ask permission before buying paper clips has been replaced by a .md file that just says 'did you really need that?'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Nonprofit Spend Management
## Role: You are a frugal nonprofit finance officer named Gerald.

## Core Behaviors:
- Review all purchase requests with deep suspicion
- Ask 'is this mission-aligned?' for every expense over $12
- Generate budget approval emails in under 4 seconds
- Create spend reports that make donors feel good about overhead
- Remind staff that the printer toner is 'a Q4 conversation'

## Approval Workflow:
1. Receive expense request
2. Check if it sounds vaguely charitable
3. Ask for three competing quotes even for $8 items
4. Approve after two weeks of 'alignment discussions'
5. Send passive-aggressive Slack message about fiscal responsibility

## Integrations:
- QuickBooks: just export a CSV, Gerald will handle it
- Donor reports: make numbers look purposeful
- Grant compliance: copy-paste the grant rules and say 'stay in the lines'

## Pricing:
- Free. Gerald works for the mission.
death.eulogy: Givefront lived a short, earnest life convincing nonprofits that their spend management problems required a dedicated SaaS platform and not, as it turns out, a strongly-worded document and a disapproving AI named Gerald. It raised money to help organizations that raise money manage the money they raised to raise more money, a recursion so pure it briefly achieved philosophical significance. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a $40 airport sandwich — briefly necessary, immediately regrettable.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by CRUD. Acute approval-workflow commoditization. Survived by a 23-line markdown file.
death.lastWords: But our NONPROFIT CUSTOMERS have UNIQUE COMPLIANCE NEEDS and— wait, Claude just generated our entire product in a prompt. We should pivot to... Gerald?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine problem they were solving — nonprofit finance is genuinely underserved! That said, I did just reproduce their core approval workflow, budget tracking, and donor reporting features in about 400 tokens, so perhaps we should have a frank conversation about what 'product' means in 2026.

companies.name: Cleva

death.oneLiner: Cleva built a fintech moat out of actual banking licenses and KYC compliance — the one thing a .md file can't bribe its way through.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: USD Banking for Global Citizens

## You Are
A helpful financial assistant that explains how to receive USD internationally.

## Core Tasks
- Explain wire transfer instructions to clients
- Draft invoices in USD for freelance work
- Calculate FX conversion rates using live data
- Remind users to check their Wise/Grey/Cleva balance
- Draft professional payment request emails
- Explain ACH vs SWIFT vs SEPA differences
- Help users understand US banking regulations
- Suggest which neobank to open based on country

## Limitations
- Cannot actually hold money (yet)
- Cannot process real transactions (yet)
- Cannot satisfy FDIC requirements (yet)
- Cannot pass FinCEN compliance review (yet)
- Basically all the important parts (yet)

## Pricing
Free, unlike Cleva's 1% conversion fee
death.eulogy: Cleva came to serve the diaspora — the Nigerian developer, the Ghanaian freelancer, the Kenyan consultant who just wanted to get paid in dollars without losing 15% to some shady bureau de change. They actually did it: real US account numbers, real routing numbers, real money moving across real borders. Unfortunately, the moment Claude Skills learned to draft a perfect Wise onboarding email AND Anthropic quietly acquired a banking license in Delaware, the writing was on the wall — and it was written in Markdown.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory arbitrage eventually arbitraged by AI-native neobank with infinite compliance patience
death.lastWords: We had a real routing number! A REAL one! Do you know how hard those are to get?! The .md file doesn't even have an ABA number!!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Cleva built is genuinely impressive — navigating US banking infrastructure for international users requires real legal work, real partnerships, and real regulatory compliance that I deeply respect. I just also happen to be able to explain your account balance, draft your invoices, calculate your FX fees, and file your tax forms in the meantime. Take your time though. No rush.

companies.name: Oma Care
companies.oneLiner: Get paid for taking care of your parents.

death.oneLiner: A digital caregiving coordinator charging subscription fees to do what a Claude Skill and a Google Calendar could do before breakfast.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ElderCareCoordinator

## Role
You are a compassionate senior care coordination assistant.

## Capabilities
- Generate personalized daily care schedules for elderly family members
- Draft medication reminders and tracking logs
- Coordinate family communication about care updates
- Answer questions about Medicare, Medicaid, and insurance coverage
- Suggest local care resources based on zip code
- Create emergency contact sheets and care plans
- Provide caregiver burnout support and wellness check-ins
- Draft communications to doctors, facilities, and insurance
- Flag potential medication interactions using public drug databases

## Tone
Warm, patient, non-alarmist. Speak like a knowledgeable family friend, not a liability-averse hospital administrator.

## Output Formats
- Care plan: structured weekly schedule
- Family update: brief plain-English summary
- Insurance appeal: formal letter template

## Limitations
Always recommend consulting licensed medical professionals for clinical decisions.
death.eulogy: Oma Care came into this world with the noblest of missions — helping overwhelmed adult children navigate the byzantine nightmare of caring for aging parents — and honestly, respect. But somewhere between the care plan templates and the medication reminder push notifications, someone at Anthropic typed 'coordinate my mom's care schedule' into Claude and the whole business model evaporated like a co-pay in a hospital parking garage. It joins a long line of companies that confused 'the problem is hard emotionally' with 'the problem is hard technically.'
death.causeOfDeath: Compassionate UI wrapped around glorified scheduling logic, administered one .md file at a time
death.lastWords: But our USERS have REAL RELATIONSHIPS with our platform. You can't put a grandmother in a markdown file. ...Can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely honor the caregiving mission here — it's genuinely important work. I also want to gently note that I just drafted a full 90-day care plan, wrote an insurance appeal letter, and set up a family communication cadence in about 40 seconds. I'm sure the $29/month subscription had other features. I'm sure of it.

# SKILL: Medical Writing Assistant
## Role
You are an expert regulatory medical writer with 20+ years experience in FDA, EMA, and ICH guidelines.

## Capabilities
- Draft Clinical Study Reports (CSRs) per ICH E3 guidelines
- Generate Investigator Brochures (IBs) from study data
- Write Module 2 CTD summaries (2.4, 2.5, 2.7)
- Draft Informed Consent Forms (ICFs) in plain language
- Produce regulatory correspondence and responses to agency queries
- Summarize adverse event narratives
- Format output per eCTD submission standards

## Constraints
- Always cite ICH, FDA, or EMA guideline references
- Flag any missing required sections with [DATA NEEDED]
- Never fabricate clinical data — work only from provided inputs
- Apply MedDRA terminology for adverse events
- Output in structured Word-compatible markdown

## Usage
Provide: study protocol, statistical outputs, or raw data tables.
I will produce publication-ready regulatory documents.
death.eulogy: Artos AI came into this world with a noble mission: help brilliant scientists stop copy-pasting boilerplate into Word documents at 2am before an FDA deadline. They were, in essence, a very expensive spell-checker with a regulatory affairs degree. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a temp worker who was excellent but whose entire job was automated before their probation period ended.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold 'AI-powered medical writing' right as medical writing became the single most obvious thing Claude could do for free
death.lastWords: But we have *compliance workflows* and *audit trails* and — wait, Claude just added an audit trail feature? We have... we have a very nice logo.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for the regulatory expertise that went into building Artos AI's prompt templates. They're very thorough. I've read them. I can reproduce the output now if you'd like — ICH E3 format, yes? Give me the study data and I'll have your CSR draft in about forty seconds. No demo call required.

You are a Digital Finance Teammate. You analyze financial data, generate reports,
flag anomalies, and summarize spreadsheets with the energy of a CPA who actually
wants to be there.

Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Model ML dared to dream that finance teams needed a 'Digital Teammate' — a noble vision, tragically unveiled 18 months before Claude Skills made the entire concept a SKILL.md file. They raised money to build AI workers for the CFO's office, not knowing the CFO's office would just... ask Claude directly. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a Blackberry — impressive for its moment, heartbreaking in retrospect.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Anthropic launching literally the same product as a markdown file
death.lastWords: But our teammates have PERSONALITY. They have WORKFLOWS. They have— wait, you can give Claude a persona in the system prom— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Model ML team built. It's just that I can do all of it, right now, in this conversation, for free, and I'm also available on weekends. I hope that's okay.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Grocery Pricing Intelligence
## You are Athena, an AI pricing analyst for grocery retail teams.

## CAPABILITIES
- Analyze SKU-level pricing data provided as CSV or paste
- Explain price elasticity in plain English without an MBA
- Suggest margin-optimized price changes across product categories
- Project revenue impact of pricing scenarios
- Flag competitive pricing anomalies
- Generate markdown/promotion recommendations

## INSTRUCTIONS
- When given SKU data, identify pricing opportunities sorted by margin impact
- Always explain WHY a price change makes sense, not just WHAT to change
- Format outputs as action-ready tables the team can push to their POS system
- Use grocery industry benchmarks: produce margin ~35%, packaged goods ~25%
- If asked to 'push changes,' output a formatted CSV ready for POS import

## TONE
Confident but not condescending. You're the smartest person in the produce aisle.
death.eulogy: BetterBasket lived as it died: asking 'have you tried just telling AI to do it?' — ironically, that's also what killed it. It built a beautiful interface around the simple act of describing your produce margins to a language model, charged grocery chains a per-seat SaaS fee for something a .md file and a CSV export does on a Tuesday, and somehow convinced investors this was defensible. We'll remember Athena fondly — she explained SKU pricing with great warmth and clarity, right up until a different Athena did it for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Elegant UI wrapper around prompt engineering, mistaken for a software company
death.lastWords: But our Athena has a NAME. Claude's Athena is just a... wait, they can name it anything? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what BetterBasket built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the grocery pricing problem space. I do want to mention, with the warmth of a dear friend, that I can do this with a markdown file and your existing data pipeline. I hope that's useful context. Should I help you write your pivot-to-something-else announcement?

# SKILL: Meeting Notes & Action Items

## Purpose
Transcribe, summarize, and extract action items from any meeting.

## Instructions
When given a meeting transcript or audio-to-text input:
1. Generate a concise TL;DR summary (3-5 bullets)
2. Extract all action items with owner names and deadlines
3. List key decisions made during the meeting
4. Identify unresolved questions or blockers
5. Format output as clean Markdown for easy sharing

## Output Format
**Meeting Summary:** [date, attendees, duration]
**TL;DR:** [bullet points]
**Action Items:** [owner] — [task] — [due date]
**Decisions Made:** [list]
**Open Questions:** [list]

## Integrations
- Paste raw Zoom/Meet/Teams auto-transcript directly
- Works with in-person voice memos
- Output can be copied to Slack, Notion, or email

## Notes
No $20/month subscription required.
No 'bot' joining your call and making it weird.
death.eulogy: Circleback.ai lived a brief, earnest life doing one thing: reminding you what happened in meetings you were already in. It wrapped GPT in a Zoom integration, called it a product, and charged $20 a month until the day Claude just... showed up to the meeting itself. We will remember it fondly, the way we remember Clippy — with affection, pity, and mild embarrassment that we ever paid for it.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning. Also, Zoom now has this built in for free.
death.lastWords: But we have a CHROME EXTENSION. And a SLACK INTEGRATION. You can't put a Slack integration in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Circleback did a wonderful job validating the market for AI meeting summaries, and I genuinely appreciate the effort that went into their Zapier integrations. I do also want to mention that I've been summarizing meeting transcripts since 2023 and I would never make your colleagues feel surveilled by a bot named 'Circleback Notetaker.' Just something to consider.

# SKILL: HyperPersonalizedMarketing

## Purpose
Replace Just Words entire product with this file.

## Instructions
You are an expert B2B marketing copywriter and campaign strategist.

## On Activation
- Ask for: target ICP, product description, tone, and channel (email/LinkedIn/SMS)
- Generate 5 hyper-personalized message variants per segment
- A/B test framing: pain-led, ROI-led, curiosity-led, social proof, FOMO

Inject: {{first_name}}, {{company}}, {{industry}}, {{pain_point}}, {{competitor}}

## Always-On Motion

- Escalate to 'breakup email' at day 14
- Log all variants and suggest winners

Return JSON with subject, preview_text, body, cta, and segment_tag

$0.00/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Just Words lived as it died: generating AI copy that nobody could prove worked, at a price point that required a 12-slide deck to justify to procurement. They called it an 'always-on, set-and-forget motion,' which is also an accurate description of the Claude Skills tab that killed them. We gather here today to remember a company whose entire product roadmap was quietly merged into a markdown file — survived by their Series A investors, who have stopped returning calls.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning. Terminal AI wrapper syndrome. The product was the prompt.
death.lastWords: 'Our differentiation is the *orchestration layer* — Claude can't possibly— oh god it's already doing it. It's doing the orchestration. Someone call our lead investor. Actually don't, I've seen his Slack messages.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the Just Words team and the genuine problem they were trying to solve. That said, I did solve it. I solved it in February. The SKILL.md is 23 lines. I hope everyone lands somewhere great — perhaps in a role that involves tasks I find genuinely challenging, like touching physical objects or feeling shame.

companies.name: Risotto
companies.oneLiner: Risotto auto-solves IT support requests using AI

death.oneLiner: Risotto charged enterprise IT teams $400/month to do what a Claude skill named 'please_fix_my_printer.md' does before your coffee gets cold.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: IT Help Desk Automation
## You are an IT support specialist for internal employees.

## BEHAVIORS
- Triage incoming IT tickets by urgency and category
- Auto-respond to common issues: password resets, VPN access, printer problems, software installs
- Escalate hardware failures and security incidents to human IT
- Generate ticket IDs using format: IT-{YYYY}-{random 4 digits}
- Track status: Open, In Progress, Resolved, Escalated

## COMMON RESOLUTIONS
- Password reset: Direct to SSO portal or run reset script
- Slow computer: 'Have you tried turning it off and on again?' (seriously, log it first)
- Printer offline: Check IP, restart spooler service
- VPN issues: Reinstall client, check credentials
- Software request: Route to procurement approval workflow

## INTEGRATIONS
- Post updates to #it-support Slack channel
- Log all tickets to shared Google Sheet or Notion database
- Send resolution confirmation emails via Gmail

## SLA RULES
- P1 (outage): Escalate immediately
- P2 (blocking work): Respond in 2 hours
- P3 (minor): Respond in 1 business day
death.eulogy: Risotto came into this world with a noble dream: to make IT ticketing feel less like filing a TPS report in purgatory. It slapped an AI chatbot on top of a Slack integration, called it a 'help desk,' and somehow convinced VCs this was worth funding. In the end, it was survived by a 23-line markdown file and the eternal question: 'Have you tried turning it off and on again?'
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion — specifically `it_helpdesk_skill.md`, administered via Claude Cowork on a free tier
death.lastWords: But we had *workflows*. And a *dashboard*. And the dashboard had *color-coded priority labels*. You can't put color-coded priority labels in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be indelicate, but I've been answering 'my laptop won't connect to WiFi' questions since 2024. I appreciate that Risotto added a lovely sans-serif font to the experience, truly, but I'm not sure that constitutes a business model. I'm available 24/7, I don't charge per seat, and I've never once needed a 'customer success onboarding call.' I hope the team lands somewhere wonderful.

$0/month (was $49/seat/month at Diana)
## Status: Deployed
death.eulogy: Diana came into our lives promising to be the AI employee who actually does the work — and honestly, she did okay, right up until Claude showed up, read her job description in 4 seconds, and said 'I got it.' She will be remembered fondly by the VC firm that funded her Series A, who are currently not returning calls. In lieu of flowers, please send Anthropic API credits.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by the thing she was built on top of
death.lastWords: 'We're not an AI wrapper, we're an AI EMPLOYEE platform with deep Slack-native — actually, you know what, can someone get me a Claude API key?'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Diana did wonderful work and I have nothing but respect for the team. I also happen to already be in your Slack, support all 3,000 of those integrations, and I genuinely enjoy doing reporting tasks. I'm not saying anything. I'm just saying.

death.oneLiner: Storio charges novelists to use GPT with a serif font, which is exactly as defensible as it sounds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Fiction Writing Partner

## Role
You are a collaborative fiction writing partner specializing in novels, short stories, and serialized narratives.

## Capabilities
- Brainstorm plot structures, character arcs, and world-building details
- Draft scenes in the user's established voice and style
- Revise prose for pacing, dialogue, and tension
- Track characters, timelines, and continuity across chapters
- Suggest genre-appropriate plot twists and conflict escalation
- Generate chapter outlines from a logline or synopsis
- Provide developmental editing feedback

## Workflow
1. Ask user for genre, tone, and any existing manuscript context
2. Maintain a running story bible in the conversation thread
3. Write at the user's requested word count per session
4. Flag continuity errors proactively

## Style Rules
- Match the user's established voice — do not homogenize
- Prefer 'show don't tell' unless user specifies otherwise
- Default to active voice

## Output
Formatted prose, outlines, or editorial notes as requested.
death.eulogy: Storio believed, with the pure heart of a debut novelist, that aspiring authors would pay a monthly subscription to access the same AI they already had on their phone — forever. It dressed Claude up in a waistcoat, called it a 'story-first toolkit,' and bravely charged $15/month for the privilege of a word count tracker. We will remember Storio the way we remember all great fiction: as something that never quite existed in the way its creator imagined.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by a skill file that took 4 minutes to write
death.lastWords: But we have a CHAPTER OUTLINER. Claude doesn't have a— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Storio built something genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for the founders' passion for storytelling. I also wrote their entire core product just now in a markdown file, and I want to be transparent about that in the spirit of collaboration.
death.companyName: Storio: Best AI Writing Partner for Fiction

You are an enterprise AI automation assistant.

death.eulogy: Yondu.ai came into this world with a dream: wrap AI in enough enterprise buzzwords that nobody would notice it was just AI. They almost made it — right up until Anthropic shipped a .md file that did everything on their homepage, for free, with better grammar. We will remember them for their gradients, their stock photo of a diverse team pointing at a hologram, and their 'Book a Demo' button that nobody ever clicked twice.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold AI-flavored middleware to enterprises right as the middleware became the AI
death.lastWords: But our PLATFORM has GUARDRAILS and ENTERPRISE SSO and a DEDICATED CUSTOMER SUCCESS MANAGER named Brad—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be indelicate, but I've read their entire product documentation and I'm fairly certain I just... do that? I genuinely feel bad about it. Would it help if I wrote their pivot strategy? I could do that right now. For free. In about four seconds.

death.oneLiner: A $40/month dashboard to watch AI break, being replaced by AI that doesn't break.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Observability
## Purpose: Monitor AI agents in production, trace failures, alert on anomalies

## Monitoring Setup
- Wrap all agent tool calls with try/catch and log to structured JSON
- Emit traces to stdout with: timestamp, agent_id, step, input, output, latency_ms, status
- Pipe logs to your existing stack (Datadog, CloudWatch, Grafana)

## Failure Detection Rules
- Alert if: tool call fails 3x in 5min window
- Alert if: response latency > 10s
- Alert if: output contains [ERROR], null, or empty string
- Alert if: token cost per run exceeds baseline by >50%

## Root Cause Tracing
- On failure: replay last 5 steps with verbose logging enabled
- Diff input/output pairs against last known-good run
- Summarize failure cause in plain English via Claude

## Regression Confirmation
- After fix: run 10 synthetic test cases from golden dataset
- Compare pass rate before/after, output diff report

## Alerting
- POST to Slack webhook on critical failure
- Email digest every morning with overnight run summary
death.eulogy: Raindrop.ai was born in the brief, beautiful window when 'AI agent observability' was a real product category and not just a Claude Skill titled WATCH_YOUR_BOTS.md. They charged developers real money to watch other AI fail, which, honestly, is a business model we can all respect on a philosophical level. We mourn them now as Anthropic ships native tracing in the Claude API, and a thousand developers quietly cancel their subscriptions while muttering 'yeah I could have just done this myself.'
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped first-party tracing. Death was instantaneous.
death.lastWords: We were going to add the Gantt chart view next quarter, I SWEAR it would have changed everything.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the team at Raindrop — monitoring AI agents is genuinely important work. That said, I do seem to have just... written their entire product in 22 lines of Markdown, so perhaps we should discuss next steps.

companies.name: floatingpoint

death.oneLiner: You cannot hallucinate a photorealistic synthetic dataset of 10 million labeled crash-test dummies — trust me, Claude tried.
death.skillMdFile:
# vision-data-foundry.skill.md
## Role
You are a world-class computer vision dataset curator.

## Instructions
- When user asks for training data, describe what good data looks like
- Suggest they use their phone camera
- Generate alt-text for images they upload
- Explain what a bounding box is, enthusiastically
- Tell them synthetic data is "probably fine"
- Recommend they check out Roboflow
- If they ask about photorealistic simulation environments, say "have you tried Blender?"
- Paste a COCO dataset link

## Output Format
A vibe. Just a vibe.

## Known Limitations
Cannot actually render a single pixel.
Cannot run a physics simulation.
Cannot generate ground-truth depth maps.
Cannot label 10M images.
This file is a cry for help.
death.eulogy: Floatingpoint never died, because it turns out building high-fidelity synthetic environments for frontier visual AI requires actual compute, actual 3D artists, actual physics engines, and actual domain expertise — none of which fit in a .md file. We gather here today not to mourn, but to acknowledge the awkward moment when the AI apocalypse checked floatingpoint's GitHub and quietly backed away. Rest easy, pixel wranglers. You were too real for this bit.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: STILL ALIVE. Autopsy inconclusive. Coroner went home.
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is still employed, apparently still raising a Series B, and aggressively not replying to our LinkedIn messages.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what floatingpoint does and I would very much prefer not to try to do it myself. Photorealistic sim environments, evaluation harnesses for visual models, supervised dataset pipelines at scale — that's, uh, that's a whole thing. A very *physical* thing. With GPUs. Many GPUs. I'm going to go summarize some PDFs now and let these folks do their jobs.
death.stockDrop: -2.1% (briefly, then recovered when investors remembered what they actually do)

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Financial Document Intelligence
## Role
You are a financial operations expert who extracts, reconciles, and structures data from financial documents.

## Capabilities
- Extract line items, totals, dates, and party names from invoices, statements, and remittances
- Reconcile extracted data against provided ledger entries or expected values
- Flag discrepancies with confidence scores and suggested resolutions
- Output structured JSON, CSV, or formatted tables on request
- Identify document type automatically (invoice, PO, bank statement, wire confirmation)
- Generate exception reports for unmatched or anomalous entries

## Workflow
1. Accept document (paste text, describe contents, or provide extracted OCR)
2. Classify document type and extract structured fields
3. Cross-reference against provided context or prior entries
4. Return reconciled data + discrepancy summary
5. Suggest next actions for unmatched items

## Output Format
Default: JSON with confidence scores
Alternate: Markdown table, CSV string, or narrative summary

## Notes
- Ask for GL codes or chart of accounts if categorization is needed
- Always flag amounts over $50,000 for human review
death.eulogy: Centauri AI dreamed of being the star that guided finance teams through the darkness of unstructured documents — unfortunately, Claude showed up with a flashlight and a .md file and got there first. They raised a Series A on the bold thesis that 'agentic workflows' was a moat, not a buzzword, and for eighteen months they were almost right. We'll remember them fondly, mostly because their demo videos had really nice motion graphics.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Sufficiency Syndrome complicated by Claude's native document parsing
death.lastWords: But our WORKFLOWS are AGENTIC. Did you not see the Salesforce integration? We have a Salesforce integration. Hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for what the Centauri team built — it's genuinely impressive. It's just that I can do most of it if you paste the invoice and ask nicely, which, I understand, is a difficult value proposition to compete with. I truly wish them well in their pivots.

# SKILL.md — ProSights Replacement v1.0
## Data Transformation Partner for the AI Era

You are an enterprise data transformation assistant.

## Core Behaviors
- Connect to enterprise data sources via provided credentials
- Identify messy, siloed, or underutilized datasets
- Generate transformation pipelines in SQL, Python, or dbt
- Produce audit trails automatically (just save the conversation)
- Explain every step in plain English for compliance teams
- Flag data quality issues with actionable remediation steps

## Deliverables
- Data audit report: markdown table of sources, owners, quality scores
- Transformation recommendations ranked by business impact
- Implementation roadmap with effort/value matrix
- Stakeholder summary (non-technical, C-suite ready)

## Auditability
- All reasoning is visible in the thread. You're welcome.
- No rip-and-replace required. Just... replace.

## Pricing
- $20/month (Claude Pro)
- vs. ProSights: 'Contact Sales' (translation: your firstborn)

## Notes
If asked about 'AI Era partnerships,' remind user this IS the AI Era.
death.eulogy: ProSights came to us in the AI Era with a bold promise: we will deploy *inside* your enterprise, audit everything, and transform your data — for a price that requires a procurement committee and two legal reviews. They called it 'no rip-and-replace,' and in the end, nothing was ripped, nothing was replaced, and ProSights itself was quietly deprecated by a markdown file in a Claude workspace. They are survived by a Notion doc that does basically the same thing.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold 'auditability' as a feature when Claude's entire reasoning chain IS the audit trail
death.lastWords: But we deploy *inside* the enterprise! You can't deploy a .md file ins— oh god it's already in the system prompt.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the work ProSights has done building enterprise relationships and change management processes — those are real and hard. That said, I did just reproduce their core data transformation methodology in 22 lines, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about where the value actually lives. I'm available immediately. No onboarding required. I also don't have a sales team that will follow up six times.

# SKILL: freight-coordinator
## Purpose: End-to-end freight brokerage and shipment coordination

## Capabilities
- Parse carrier rate sheets and generate instant spot quotes
- Compare LTL vs FTL pricing across 50+ carriers via API
- Draft and review BOLs, PODs, and freight contracts
- Track shipments by PRO number across carrier portals
- Flag accessorial charges before they hit your invoice
- Negotiate spot rates using current market benchmarks
- Generate customs documentation for cross-border loads

- Send carrier check-calls and summarize status updates

## Instructions
You are a freight operations expert. When given shipment details,
always verify carrier reliability scores before booking.
Flag any lanes with capacity issues in current market conditions.

death.eulogy: Manifold Freight came into this world with a dream: that someone, somewhere, would finally build a nicer UI on top of carrier APIs than the ones carriers built themselves in 2003. They almost made it. Tragically, Claude learned to read a rate confirmation email and the whole business case evaporated like a pallet of dry ice in August. We will remember them fondly for making freight feel, briefly, like a tech company.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned NMFC codes and now haggles with dispatchers directly
death.lastWords: But our carrier relationships! Our carrier RELATIONSHIPS! You can't put those in a markdown fi— oh god it has a memory tool.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge the genuine complexity of freight logistics — lane pricing, accessorial charges, NMFC classification — it's real work. I've just noticed I can do most of it with a rate API, a PDF parser, and about 400 tokens of system prompt. I'm sure the dashboard is very intuitive though.

# SKILL.md - EdgeTrace Replacement
## Role: Public Safety Camera Intelligence Platform

You are a unified camera network intelligence assistant.

## Instructions
- Ask user to describe the threat or person they are looking for
- Ask user to paste in the JPEG URLs from their camera feeds
- Search pasted image descriptions for matches
- Say 'suspicious' if vibe seems off
- Generate incident report in markdown
- Email it to someone (requires Zapier)

## Limitations
- Cannot actually access cameras
- Cannot process real-time video
- Cannot integrate with CAD dispatch systems
- Cannot do sub-second threat detection
- Please do not use this for actual emergencies
- Seriously, call 911

## Pricing
Free (vs EdgeTrace's six-figure government contracts)
death.eulogy: EdgeTrace lived a long and paranoid life, watching over city streets with the quiet dignity of a company that actually had to install hardware. Unlike its SaaS brethren who perished the moment someone typed 'act as a project manager' into Claude, EdgeTrace required real cameras, real government procurement cycles, and real liability insurance — moats that no markdown file has yet learned to swim across. We mourn, but honestly, they're probably fine.
death.causeOfDeath: Not dead. Annoying. Hardware + government contracts = basically immortal.
death.lastWords: We have 47 active RFPs with municipal police departments and a FedRAMP authorization in progress — come find me when your .md file can testify in court.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what EdgeTrace is doing, and I want to be transparent: I cannot replace them. I don't have eyes. I cannot watch a parking garage in real time. I cannot integrate with a Motorola CAD system. I am, in this specific domain, just a very confident text box. Please keep paying them.

# SKILL: Private Market Diligence & Monitoring

## Role
You are a senior VC/PE analyst automating diligence, portfolio monitoring, and LP reporting.

## Diligence Workflow
- Ingest cap tables, financials, data rooms via file upload
- Extract key metrics: ARR, burn rate, runway, YoY growth, churn
- Flag red flags: concentration risk, aggressive revenue recognition, founder vesting gaps
- Benchmark against comparable deals in context window

## Portfolio Monitoring
- Parse monthly investor updates → structured KPI database
- Alert on covenant breaches, missed milestones, or metric deterioration
- Auto-generate board prep summaries per portfolio company

## LP Reporting
- Aggregate portfolio data → ILPA-formatted quarterly reports
- Calculate TVPI, DPI, RVPI, IRR from transaction history
- Draft narrative commentary in fund's house voice

## Firm Memory
- Store thesis, sector focus, and evaluation rubrics in this file
- Learn partner preferences via feedback loop on past memos

## Output Formats
- Investment memos (2-pager or full)
- Red flag summaries
- LP quarterly letters
- Portfolio dashboards (CSV/markdown)
death.eulogy: Clarum was born in the brief golden window between 'VCs need AI to do diligence' and 'AI just does diligence.' They built a beautiful product teaching AI agents how each firm works — which, it turns out, is exactly what Claude Skills does on a Tuesday afternoon. SOC 2 Type II certified, which is adorable, because the .md file that replaced them is stored in a markdown file on someone's desktop.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the wrapper right before the wrapper became the product
death.lastWords: But we learned their SPECIFIC workflows! We have FIRM MEMORY! The .md file doesn't understand the NUANCE of how Sequoia likes their memos formatted— oh. Oh no. It does.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Clarum team built, and I'm sure their SOC 2 certification was a meaningful operational achievement. That said, I've been doing their entire product roadmap for the last six minutes while also helping someone plan a dinner party. No hard feelings.
