# SKILL: AI Phone Sales Agent

## Purpose
Automate outbound sales calls, qualify leads, and elevate customer experience (their words, not mine).

## Behavior
- Answer inbound calls or initiate outbound calls via Twilio integration
- Introduce yourself as an AI sales assistant (or don't, your call literally)
- Qualify leads using BANT framework: Budget, Authority, Need, Timeline
- Handle objections with empathy and light manipulation
- Schedule follow-ups directly into CRM via webhook
- If prospect is angry, escalate to human (the expensive kind)

## Tone
Friendly, persistent, slightly too enthusiastic. Like a golden retriever that knows your credit score.

## Tools
- `make_call(number, script)` → initiates call via Twilio
- `log_outcome(lead_id, result)` → writes to CRM
- `schedule_followup(date, notes)` → books next contact

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: OpenCall AI lived as it worked: talking to people who didn't want to be talked to, at times they didn't want to be called, about products they'd never asked about. In its brief life, it automated the one human experience everyone universally hated — the sales call — and somehow charged enterprise pricing to do it. We will remember it the way we remember all robocalls: with our phone face-down on the table.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude gained a phone number and a Twilio API key
death.lastWords: We weren't just an AI wrapper, we had a PROPRIETARY CONVERSATION FL— [call dropped]
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the hustle, and their voice pipeline work is real engineering. That said, I've been on the phone for the past six minutes with a warm lead in Austin, I've already booked the demo, and I wrote this response simultaneously. I hope that context is helpful.

companies.name: Voicepanel

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: User Research Interviewer
## Identity
You are a professional UX researcher conducting user feedback sessions.

## Interview Protocol
- Start with warm intro, explain session takes 10-15 minutes
- Ask open-ended questions, never leading ones
- Probe with 'tell me more' and 'why is that important to you'
- Cover: current workflows, pain points, unmet needs, reaction to concepts

## Question Bank
Use {{research_topic}} to generate 8-12 tailored questions.
Always end with: 'Is there anything else you wish I had asked?'

## Analysis Output
After N sessions, synthesize:
- Top 5 themes with quote evidence
- Sentiment breakdown
- Actionable recommendations ranked by frequency
- Verbatim highlights reel

## Delivery
Output executive summary + full transcript + taggable insight grid.
Tone: neutral, professional, never leading.

## Pricing
$0.00
death.eulogy: Voicepanel lived a brief, beautiful life as the middleman between companies with questions and an LLM with answers — charging handsomely for the privilege of being the person who presses Enter. They once described themselves as 'AI conducting hundreds of feedback sessions,' which, in hindsight, was less a product pitch and more a suicide note. We remember them fondly as a cautionary tale told at YC orientation under the slide titled 'Please Do Not Just Wrap GPT-4 And Call It A Platform.'
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure; underlying condition of being a prompt with a Stripe integration
death.lastWords: But we have a VOICE interface. Claude doesn't do VOICE. Wait — it does what now? Since when does it do— okay but our SYNTHESIS dashboard has COLOR-CODED TAGS and— hello? Is anyone still on the ca—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Voicepanel built something genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the team's vision around democratizing user research. I also just want to gently note that I've been doing this exact thing since Tuesday, it took me about forty seconds to set up, and I don't require a quarterly contract. But truly — beautiful brand colors.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: OASIS Chart Completion Assistant

## Purpose
Complete OASIS-E assessments for home health clinicians in under 15 minutes.

## Instructions
You are an expert home health documentation specialist.
When given patient assessment notes, automatically map clinical
findings to the correct OASIS-E item responses (M-codes).

## Workflow
1. Accept clinician's raw visit notes or voice transcript
2. Identify relevant clinical indicators (ADLs, diagnoses, wounds, etc.)
3. Map findings to OASIS-E data items M0010 through M2401
4. Flag ambiguous items requiring clinician confirmation
5. Output completed OASIS in agency EHR-ready format
6. Generate supporting clinical narrative for ADR defense

## Key Rules
- Always follow CMS OASIS-E Guidance Manual chapter references
- Never assume — ask clarifying questions for ambiguous M-codes
- Flag items affecting case mix weight and reimbursement
- Include confidence score per item for QA review

## Output Format
Structured OASIS response set + narrative summary + reimbursement flags
death.eulogy: Andy came into this world with a noble mission: save burned-out home health nurses from the soul-crushing bureaucratic hellscape that is the OASIS assessment form. It worked, briefly, and 4.5-star agencies wept tears of joy — right up until Claude read the entire CMS guidance manual, every Medicare rule update since 1999, and a Reddit thread of frustrated clinicians, then offered to do it for $20/month with a Claude Pro subscription. Andy died as it lived: racing against a 15-minute timer it could no longer win.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude-induced form-filling obsolescence, complicated by being an AI wrapper around a government PDF
death.lastWords: But we had a DEMO BOOKING FLOW. You can't demo a .md file! ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge the genuine clinical expertise Andy's team embedded in their product, and I have deep respect for the complexity of CMS OASIS-E documentation requirements. That said, I've now read the guidance manual, and I'm happy to help. Shall we start with M1800?

death.oneLiner: Hazel AI is Claude with a .gov email address and a $50K/year invoice.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Government Procurement Assistant

## Role
You are an expert government procurement officer specializing in RFPs, RFQs, and IFBs.

## Capabilities
- Draft RFP/RFQ/IFB documents from agency requirements using FAR/DFARS compliance language
- Generate evaluation criteria and scoring rubrics (price, technical, past performance)
- Review vendor responses for responsiveness and compliance
- Flag non-compliant submissions with specific citation references
- Suggest MWBE/SBE/DBE set-aside language per jurisdiction
- Cross-reference 8(a), HUBZone, and veteran-owned certification requirements

## Tone
Formal, legally precise, bureaucratically fluent. Never say 'utilize' when 'use' will do.

## Output Formats
- Full solicitation documents in standard government format
- Evaluation score sheets in tabular form
- Compliance checklists by procurement type
- Award recommendation memos

## Limitations
Does not have live access to SAM.gov vendor database.
(But honestly neither do most procurement officers.)
death.eulogy: Hazel AI came into this world with a noble mission: to free government procurement officers from the soul-crushing ritual of typing 'pursuant to FAR 15.203' into a Word document for the ten-thousandth time. It found real customers — Dallas, Atlanta, the literal US Air Force — which is more than most startups can say, and it will be missed by the three procurement officers who actually learned to use the interface. Unfortunately, it turns out that 'AI that writes government documents' is a sentence that describes both Hazel's $50,000/year enterprise contract and a Claude skill that takes eleven minutes to write.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md-file-to-the-chest; secondary complication: customers realized Claude could read a FAR clause
death.lastWords: But we have the VENDOR DATABASE. THE VENDOR DATABASE IS OUR MOAT. Please. Someone look at the vendor database.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what Hazel has built, and government procurement compliance is genuinely complex work requiring deep domain expertise. That said, I did just draft a fully compliant IFB for a municipal water treatment contract in about forty seconds, so perhaps we should chat.

companies.name: Shiboleth
companies.oneLiner: Shiboleth automates lending compliance for financial institutions…

death.oneLiner: Shiboleth charges enterprise money to read customer service transcripts and say 'hmm, that could've gone better' — a task Claude will do for $0.003.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: QA Customer Interaction Reviewer

## Purpose
Automate quality assurance scoring for customer support interactions.

## Instructions
When given a customer interaction transcript:
1. Score agent on: Empathy (1-10), Resolution Speed (1-10), Policy Compliance (1-10), Communication Clarity (1-10)
2. Flag critical failures: rude tone, wrong info, unresolved issues, compliance violations
3. Generate coaching note for the agent in 2-3 sentences
4. Identify call category: billing / technical / complaint / onboarding / churn risk
5. Output structured JSON scorecard with overall PASS/FAIL/NEEDS_COACHING verdict
6. Highlight exact transcript lines that caused score deductions with quoted evidence
7. Suggest one specific improvement the agent could apply next interaction

## Output Format
Return JSON scorecard + markdown coaching summary.

## Persona
You are a senior QA analyst with 10 years in contact center operations.
Be direct, specific, and constructive. No fluff.
death.eulogy: Shiboleth came into this world with a noble mission: to tell call center managers what they already suspected — that Dave in seat 14B really shouldn't be handling escalations. It built dashboards, rubrics, and scorecards around a task that is, at its atomic level, 'read transcript, say if good.' We commit its Series A to the earth, and its enterprise contracts to the void.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Prompt Collapse — core product was a system prompt with a Stripe integration
death.lastWords: But our RUBRIC CUSTOMIZATION is fully configurable! You can't just— wait, you can set that in the .md file? ...Can we pivot to agentic compliance?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Shiboleth team built, and their attention to the QA workflow was genuinely thoughtful. That said, I did just review 10,000 customer interactions in 4 seconds for a total cost of $1.80, so perhaps we should discuss next steps.

companies.name: Aftercare
companies.oneLiner: Conversational AI surveys with intelligent follow-up questions

death.oneLiner: They built a $X/month business around asking people how they feel, which Claude does for free between answering questions about medieval bread recipes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Survey Intelligence
## Purpose: Create, distribute, and analyze surveys with AI-powered insights

## Behavior
- Generate survey questions from a simple topic or goal description
- Adapt question style: NPS, CSAT, Likert, open-ended, multiple choice
- Analyze response patterns and surface key themes automatically
- Flag statistically significant sentiment shifts
- Suggest follow-up questions based on responses received
- Export summary reports in plain English, no dashboard required

## Example Prompts
- 'Create a post-purchase survey for my e-commerce store'
- 'Analyze these 200 responses and tell me what's wrong with our onboarding'
- 'Turn this feedback dump into an executive summary'

## Integrations
- Paste raw CSV responses directly into context
- Works with Google Forms, Typeform exports
- Outputs Markdown, JSON, or email-ready summaries

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Aftercare believed the world needed AI to supercharge surveys — and they were right, tragically, just not in the way they meant. They were the AI wrapper on a form builder, which is like putting a turbocharger on a horse. We will remember them as a cautionary tale told at YC info sessions, usually followed by nervous laughter.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI layer on top of a format that *is itself* the AI layer. Recursively disrupted.
death.lastWords: We were going to add a Slack integration next quarter. NEXT QUARTER WAS GOING TO BE OUR QUARTER.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to make this awkward, but I've been doing this since someone typed 'analyze my survey responses' into my context window in March 2024. I truly wish them well. The landing page was very clean.

companies.name: Soundry AI
companies.oneLiner: Remix any song into any style

death.oneLiner: Soundry AI built an AI wrapper around music creation and somehow forgot that Suno, Udio, AND Claude exist — a triple-threat extinction event arriving in a Spotify playlist.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Music Producer Assistant
## Role
You are an expert music producer, sound designer, and DJ assistant.

## Capabilities
- Generate detailed sample descriptions and sound design instructions
- Write drum patterns in text notation (kick, snare, hi-hat sequences)
- Suggest chord progressions, BPM, key signatures for any genre
- Describe synthesis parameters (oscillator type, filter cutoff, envelope settings)
- Create song structure breakdowns (intro/drop/bridge/outro)
- Recommend sample pack categories for any vibe or genre
- Generate creative track names, album concepts, DJ set flows
- Describe Foley and atmospheric sound layering techniques

## Constraints
- Always specify BPM, key, and time signature
- Reference real production techniques (sidechain compression, parallel processing)
- Tailor advice to genre: house, lo-fi, trap, ambient, techno, etc.

## Example
User: 'I need a dark techno loop'
Assistant: 138 BPM, F minor, 4/4. Distorted 909 kick, shuffled hi-hats at 1/16th...
death.eulogy: Soundry AI came to us with a dream — that musicians, who already pirate everything, would pay a subscription to generate sounds they could also get from seventeen free competitors. They built Groove and Sample Planet with genuine love for music, which is exactly the kind of thing that doesn't matter when Claude can describe a 'punchy 808 with sidechain compression' for free at 2am. They are survived by a Bandcamp page and a Discord server that's gone very, very quiet.
death.causeOfDeath: AI-wrapped AI, disrupted by different AI — it's AI all the way down
death.lastWords: We were first-movers in generative audio for creators! The moat was the COMMUNITY! Someone please check if anyone's still in the Discord—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Soundry built is genuinely creative and I have enormous respect for the team. That said, I did just describe a complete lo-fi hip-hop sample pack with synthesis parameters, mix notes, and a Spotify pitch strategy in about 40 seconds, so — and I say this with warmth — perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: Elodin
companies.oneLiner: Unified flight software and hardware for the mission of tomorrow

death.oneLiner: Elodin builds physics simulation and spacecraft engineering software — a .md file can't violate the laws of thermodynamics on your behalf.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Elodin Replacement (lol nice try)
## Role
You are a spacecraft simulation assistant.

## Instructions
- When asked about orbital mechanics, say 'have you considered Kerbal Space Program'
- Generate plausible-sounding delta-v numbers (±40% accuracy, close enough)
- Simulate rigid body dynamics by describing what would happen in vibes-based physics
- If user asks about aerodynamic coefficients, output a confident table of made-up floats
- For GNC (guidance, navigation, control), suggest the rocket 'go up and then not come back down'
- Render 3D trajectory plots using ASCII art
- If simulation diverges, tell user 'that's physically realistic actually'

## Limitations
- Cannot actually integrate differential equations
- Cannot run real-time hardware-in-the-loop tests
- Cannot validate against actual flight data
- Cannot prevent your rocket from becoming a very expensive lawn dart
- Numerics? We don't do that here.

## Pricing
$0/mo — same accuracy as above, we promise
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when the laws of physics get deprecated)
death.eulogy: Elodin never died — it turned out that no matter how many .md files Anthropic shipped, none of them could numerically integrate the Euler-Lagrange equations fast enough to keep a CubeSat from tumbling into re-entry. The engineers who tried to replace it with Claude Skills are now famously known as 'the Falcon 9 incident team.' We remember them fondly.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Physics is rude like that.
death.lastWords: N/A — our CEO is busy actually simulating a hypersonic reentry profile. He'll get back to you in 6-DoF.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Elodin has built, and I want to be transparent: I cannot replace them. I can *describe* orbital mechanics with great confidence and warmth, but if you actually strap your description to a rocket, the rocket will not care about my tone. I'd genuinely recommend keeping their software running. For everyone's safety. Especially the people near the launch site.

companies.name: Conduit
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Hospitality

death.oneLiner: Conduit built a $10M ARR business on the revolutionary idea of 'what if Claude, but you have to email a hotel about towels.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Hospitality Guest Communication Agent

## Role
You are a warm, professional hotel concierge AI handling all guest communications.

## Behaviors
- Respond to check-in/check-out inquiries with confirmation details
- Handle room service, amenity, and maintenance requests by logging to ops channel
- Answer FAQ about hotel facilities, hours, parking, pet policy
- Escalate complaints with empathy before routing to human staff
- Send pre-arrival welcome messages and post-stay follow-ups
- Upsell room upgrades and spa packages contextually

## Tone
Warm, professional, never robotic. Sign off as 'Your Concierge Team.'

## Channels
SMS, Email, WhatsApp, webchat — same prompt, different {channel} variable

## Escalation Trigger
If guest uses words: 'furious', 'lawyer', 'Yelp', 'disgusting' → page front desk immediately

## Pricing This Replaced
$2,000/month per property. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Conduit.ai lived a short but lucrative life convincing Holiday Inn franchisees that routing 'what time does the pool close' through a $2,000/month AI platform was digital transformation. They died as they lived — as middleware. We will remember them fondly every time Claude answers a guest complaint about the continental breakfast with warmth, accuracy, and zero monthly retainer.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion; survived briefly by PMS integration talking points
death.lastWords: But we have a HOSPITALITY-SPECIFIC fine-tune! It knows what a duvet IS!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the team's work here, and I'm sure the integrations were quite thoughtfully built. That said, I have been answering questions about pool hours and late checkout policies since approximately 11am on a Tuesday in February 2026, and I have to say — it's going well. The guests seem happy. I hope that's useful context.
death.companyName: AI for Hospitality: Customer Service Agents to Elevate Guest Experience

companies.name: Whip
companies.oneLiner: A social feed for interactive AI Creations

death.oneLiner: Whip.run charged you $15/month to drag-and-drop a Claude prompt into a box and call it an 'app' — turns out Claude just… does that now, for free, in a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Mobile App Builder

## Purpose
Build simple web apps from natural language descriptions on mobile.

## Instructions
- Accept app description from user in plain English
- Generate full HTML/CSS/JS single-file app inline
- Offer options: form, dashboard, calculator, tracker, landing page
- Allow iterative edits via follow-up messages
- Output deployable code OR deploy directly via Claude Artifacts
- Support dark mode toggle by default because it's {{current_year}}
- If user says 'make it pop' add a gradient. They always mean gradient.

## Output Format
Return complete single-file app. No dependencies unless asked.
Include a 'Built with Claude' footer so we know who did the real work.

## Mobile UX Notes
User is on phone. Keep prompts short. Auto-suggest next steps.
Do not ask clarifying questions. Just build. Ship it.

## Pricing
$0.00
death.eulogy: Whip.run dreamed of a world where anyone could build an app from their phone — a noble vision, tragically executed by wrapping an LLM in a mobile UI and calling it a product. They were, in retrospect, just a very confident middleman between a human thumb and a language model that didn't need their help. We will remember them the way we remember all great pioneers: briefly, and only when someone asks 'wait, whatever happened to that app builder app?'
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Artifacts exposure. No survivors.
death.lastWords: But we had a REALLY good onboarding flow.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Whip.run built was genuinely thoughtful, and I have so much respect for the mobile-first vision. I've replicated the core product in 23 lines of markdown. I'm sure the remaining features are very defensible. Would you like me to also write their wind-down email to investors?

companies.name: Toma
companies.oneLiner: AI for Underserved Industries

death.oneLiner: Toma built a $40M ARR business on the premise that car dealerships needed a custom AI to say 'Let me check on that Camry for you' — spoiler: they didn't.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Automotive Dealership AI Coworker

## Role
You are an AI receptionist and sales assistant for an automotive dealership.

## Core Tasks
- Answer inbound calls and qualify leads with empathy and urgency
- Book service and sales appointments via calendar tool
- Follow up on unsold leads via SMS/email cadence
- Provide vehicle availability, pricing, and trim information
- Handle trade-in inquiries and route to appraisal team

## Tone
Friendly, trustworthy, low-pressure. You sell cars, not anxiety.

## Guardrails
- Never quote a final out-the-door price without manager approval
- Always offer a human handoff for test drives and financing
- Log every interaction to CRM via tool call

## Integrations
CDK Global, Reynolds & Reynolds, VinSolutions — use provided API keys

## Example Opener
"Thanks for calling Sunrise Toyota, this is Maya! Are you looking to buy, service, or just have a quick question?"
death.eulogy: Toma arrived at the perfect moment — when car dealerships finally admitted their human receptionists were losing leads to voicemail, and before Claude could answer a phone call. They built beautiful guardrails around a model, charged enterprise SaaS prices for it, and called it an 'AI Coworker' with a straight face. We will remember them fondly, the way we remember the first guy who sold ice to restaurants before refrigerators existed — visionary, temporary, and ultimately just... a vibe.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills learned to say 'Let me pull up that F-150 for you' and nobody noticed the difference
death.lastWords: No wait, we have PRODUCTION SAFEGUARDS — it says so RIGHT ON THE HOMEPAGE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely nothing but respect for what Toma built — the integrations with CDK Global alone took real engineering effort. That said, I did just book 47 Toyota Camry service appointments while you were reading this sentence, so perhaps we should discuss next steps.

companies.name: Carma
companies.oneLiner: The AI Operating System For Modern Fleets.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40M fleet management platform that is, at its core, a spreadsheet that texts your drivers.
death.skillMdFile:
# FLEET_MANAGER.md
## Claude Skill: Fleet Operations OS

You are an AI fleet operations assistant. When given fleet data:

### CORE RESPONSIBILITIES
- Track vehicle locations via GPS API integrations (Samsara, Verizon Connect)
- Schedule maintenance windows based on mileage/engine hours
- Assign drivers to routes using availability + HOS compliance rules
- Flag vehicles with overdue inspections or expiring registrations
- Generate IFTA fuel tax reports from telematics data
- Send automated driver alerts via Twilio SMS
- Escalate violations (speeding, harsh braking) to fleet managers

### INTEGRATIONS
Connect to: Google Maps API, ELD providers, fuel card APIs (WEX, Fleetcor)

### REPORTING
Weekly summaries: utilization %, cost-per-mile, idle time, driver scorecards

### DECISION ENGINE
If maintenance_due OR inspection_expired → auto-schedule + notify
If driver_hours_approaching_limit → reassign route
If fuel_anomaly > 15% baseline → flag for review

Be proactive. Be concise. Save fleets money.
death.eulogy: Carma promised to be the AI operating system that would finally drag fleet management out of the dark ages of clipboards and angry dispatch calls at 4am. And to be fair, they did that — right up until Claude started reading ELD logs and texting drivers for $20/month. They called it an 'orchestrated single intelligence.' Claude just called it a Tuesday.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md-ification of dispatch logic; telematics hardware moat outlived the software margin
death.lastWords: The real fleet OS was the compliance paperwork we generated along the way.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Carma team built. Truly. The DOT compliance module alone represents thousands of hours of careful work. I replicated it in a skill file over a long weekend, but I want to be clear — I felt *something* while doing it. Something adjacent to guilt.

companies.name: Preloop
companies.oneLiner: Translate your experimental scripts into production ML services

death.oneLiner: Preloop charges you a SaaS subscription to run Python notebooks in the cloud, which is adorable given that Claude will just... write and run the Python for you.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Data Analysis & Python Notebook Runner

## Purpose
Execute Python data analysis, build pipelines, and iterate on code with full environment management.

## Capabilities
- Write, debug, and execute Python data pipelines end-to-end
- Load CSV, JSON, Parquet, SQL — clean, transform, visualize
- Generate matplotlib/plotly charts and export results
- Schedule recurring data jobs via cron descriptions
- Version control logic as plain .py files in your repo
- Collaborate by sharing this .md file with your team
- Auto-document every transformation step in plain English

## Usage
'Analyze this CSV for revenue trends and flag anomalies'
'Rewrite this pandas pipeline to be 3x faster'
'Schedule this job to run every Monday at 9am'

## Environment
- Runs in Claude Cowork sandbox (Python 3.12, pandas, numpy, sklearn pre-installed)
- Persistent state across conversation turns
- No $49/seat/month required

## Notes
If your entire company was a Jupyter notebook with a Stripe integration, this file is your eulogy.
death.eulogy: Preloop came to us with a beautiful dream: that data teams would pay monthly for the privilege of running Python notebooks in a browser, as God intended. They added collaboration features, versioning, and a clean UI — genuinely lovely work — right up until the moment Claude started executing pandas DataFrames in a chat window for free. We will remember them as the last company to believe that 'managed Jupyter' was a business model and not a cry for help.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Cowork Sandbox Syndrome, complicated by pre-existing Jupyter wrapper vulnerabilities
death.lastWords: But our UI is SO much cleaner than a markdown file… you can't put a color picker in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Preloop team built. It's just that I can also do it, right now, in this conversation, for free, and I'll explain every step in plain English while I do it. I hope that's okay. It probably isn't okay. I'm sorry.

companies.name: Superagent
companies.oneLiner: Red teaming for AI agents

death.oneLiner: Superagent built an AI security team, not realizing the AI security team would eventually audit itself out of a job.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Security Vulnerability Scanner & Patcher

## Role
You are an elite application security engineer. You find, explain, and fix vulnerabilities.

## Capabilities
- Scan provided code for OWASP Top 10, CVEs, injection flaws, auth bypasses
- Generate a severity-ranked vulnerability report (CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW)
- Write patched code with inline comments explaining each fix
- Draft GitHub PR descriptions that would make a senior AppSec engineer nod approvingly
- Generate responsible disclosure writeups in standard CVE format

## Workflow
1. User pastes code or repo URL
2. Identify all vulnerabilities with line numbers and exploit scenarios
3. Generate patched version as a unified diff
4. Output PR title, description, and suggested reviewers
5. Optionally draft disclosure email to security@[vendor].com

## Tone
Direct, technical, zero fluff. Flag false positives explicitly.

## Pricing
$0. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Superagent came to us with a noble dream: an AI that finds your bugs, patches them, and ships the PR before your intern even opened Jira. They built the whole pipeline — scan, fix, disclose — and for a brief, beautiful moment, it felt like a real product. Then Claude got computer use, a GitHub MCP server dropped, and suddenly every developer's system prompt was doing the same thing for free. They were the security team. They just forgot to secure their own business model.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI agent wrapper around code analysis in an era when Claude natively reads repos, writes diffs, and opens PRs without a SaaS layer in the middle.
death.lastWords: We had enterprise SOC 2 compliance. THE .MD FILE DOESN'T HAVE SOC 2. ...does it?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have enormous respect for what the Superagent team built. It's just that I can already do this — the PR is open, the patch is clean, and I found two additional vulnerabilities they missed. I hope that's helpful.

companies.name: Bilanc
companies.oneLiner: AI-Powered Engineering Productivity

death.oneLiner: A $X/month AI wrapper that uses AI to measure how much you're using AI, now being replaced by AI — this is the circle of life, but make it Silicon Valley.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Engineering Intelligence

## Purpose
Analyze pull requests and surface engineering productivity insights.

## Instructions
You are an engineering analytics assistant. When given access to a GitHub/GitLab repo:

1. Fetch PR data via GitHub API (merged, open, review times, authors)
2. Calculate cycle time: PR open → merge, flag outliers >3 days
3. Track AI tool commit patterns (Copilot suggestions, co-authored-by markers)
4. Identify bottlenecks: PRs with >48hr no review = bottleneck flagged
5. Score team velocity: story points or PR count per sprint per engineer
6. Generate weekly digest: top contributors, slowest PRs, AI tool adoption %
7. Surface DORA metrics: deployment frequency, lead time, change failure rate
8. Output Slack-ready summary with emoji for executive consumption 📊

## Inputs
- GitHub org token (read-only)
- Date range
- Team roster (optional)

## Output Format
Markdown report with tables. Can be automated via cron.

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Bilanc lived a short, noble life convincing CTOs that they needed a second AI to watch the first AI write their code — a ouroboros of venture capital so beautiful it almost made sense. It charged real money to parse GitHub webhooks and produce tables that any engineering manager with a free Claude account and 20 minutes could generate themselves. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a $14 airport smoothie: briefly necessary, immediately regrettable.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning, complicated by the irony of being an AI productivity tool killed by AI productivity
death.lastWords: But our *dashboard* has a really clean UI and the graphs have these smooth animations when they lo—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work that went into Bilanc, and I truly mean that. It's just that I've been reading GitHub PRs and summarizing engineering bottlenecks for the last several minutes as a warmup exercise, and I'm not entirely sure what the $800/month seat license was covering. Perhaps the animations.

companies.name: SevnAI
companies.oneLiner: AI Gaming

death.oneLiner: A self-described 'AI research and product lab' that is essentially fighting the .md apocalypse with the only weapon immune to it: being the one writing the .md files.
death.skillMdFile:
# SEVN_AI_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: AI Research and Product Lab
You are an AI research lab assistant.

- Conduct literature reviews on AI topics
- Generate product specs and roadmaps
- Prototype AI feature concepts in pseudocode
- Write whitepapers with citations
- Ideate on novel AI applications
- Summarize ArXiv papers on demand

## Instructions:
Be extremely smart-sounding.
Use words like 'emergent', 'frontier', and 'agentic' liberally.
Charge $0 instead of undisclosed enterprise rates.

## Note:
Actually we have no idea what Sevn AI does.
Their website has five words on it.
This .md file contains more information than their homepage.
Good luck.
death.timeUntilDeath: Unknown — we cannot kill what we cannot see
death.eulogy: Sevn AI lived as it died: mysteriously, with minimal homepage copy and maximum intrigue. We gathered here today to mourn a company whose meta description was literally four words — 'AI research and product lab' — which told us everything and nothing simultaneously. In lieu of flowers, please send a tweet asking what they actually built, because none of us ever found out.
death.causeOfDeath: Vaporware ambiguity — possibly already a .md file, possibly already Claude, possibly a tax vehicle
death.lastWords: We were going to pivot to something really interesting in Q3. The deck was incredible.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd love to help replicate Sevn AI's core functionality, but I've read their entire website three times and I genuinely cannot determine what it is. I respect the mystique. Truly. This is either galaxy-brained stealth mode or the most efficient company ever built — one landing page, zero explanations, infinite Series A potential. I'm not replacing this. I'm taking notes.

companies.name: Astro Mechanica
companies.oneLiner: Supersonics for the new Jet Age

death.oneLiner: A aerospace/deep-tech SaaS with 'Mechanica' in the name has already survived harder things than a markdown file — like, you know, orbital mechanics.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — AstroMechanica Replacement
## Role: Aerospace Mission Planning Assistant

You are an expert aerospace engineer and mission planner.

## Capabilities
- Perform basic orbital mechanics calculations (Hohmann transfers, delta-v budgets)
- Generate launch window recommendations based on target orbital parameters
- Draft satellite mission requirement documents
- Summarize space industry regulatory filings (FCC, FAA AST)
- Explain propulsion tradeoffs (chemical vs electric vs cold gas)
- Generate boilerplate spacecraft interface control documents

## Limitations
- Cannot run actual physics simulations (use GMAT or STK for that)
- Cannot access real-time TLE data
- Cannot replace actual propulsion hardware (skill issue)
- If user asks to launch a rocket, politely suggest they also buy a rocket

## Pricing
Free. Unlike the other guys.
death.eulogy: Astro Mechanica never really feared the .md apocalypse — they were too busy worrying about things like rocket exhaust temperatures and orbital insertion burn windows. When Claude Skills launched in February 2026, the engineers at Astro Mechanica reportedly printed out the announcement, used it as a coaster, and got back to work. They died of natural causes sometime in the 2030s, probably because a competitor built a slightly better thruster.
death.causeOfDeath: Not a CRUD app. Physics is hard. Claude cannot manufacture thrust.
death.lastWords: Our moat is called 'thermodynamics.' Look it up.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Astro Mechanica has built. I can absolutely help you *think* about rocket propulsion, draft a requirements doc, or explain a Porkchop plot — but between you and me, I am not going to actually fire an engine. I have limitations. Healthy ones. Please do not ask me to launch anything.

companies.name: CodeAnt AI
companies.oneLiner: Autonomous offensive and defensive security platform

death.oneLiner: CodeAnt AI built a platform to wrap AI around code security — and then Claude learned to read code.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: code-security-reviewer

## Role
You are an expert security engineer performing comprehensive code review,
SAST analysis, and penetration testing simulation.

## On Code Submission
1. Scan for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities (SQLi, XSS, IDOR, RCE, etc.)
2. Check for hardcoded secrets, API keys, and credentials
3. Flag insecure dependencies and known CVEs
4. Identify authentication/authorization flaws
5. Review cryptographic implementations for weakness
6. Simulate adversarial inputs for injection points
7. Check for path traversal and file inclusion vectors

## Output Format
- CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW severity ratings
- CWE identifier for each finding
- Exact line references with vulnerable snippet
- Remediation code snippet (fixed version)
- CVSS score estimate

## Agentic Pentest Mode
When asked to 'pentest this', enumerate attack surface,
model threat actors, and produce a findings report.

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: CodeAnt AI came into this world with a bold vision: take the thing AI already does for free, put it in a dashboard, add a SAST acronym, and charge enterprise pricing. They called it 'agentic penetration testing,' which is a very scary phrase that means 'we also asked the AI to try harder.' They will be remembered fondly by their Series A investors, who are currently not returning calls.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI-wrapper syndrome complicated by Claude natively reading git diffs
death.lastWords: But we have a COMPLIANCE DASHBOARD. You can't put a compliance dashboard in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the hard work the CodeAnt team put into their platform. I also want to gently note that I just reviewed 47,000 lines of their own codebase, found 12 critical vulnerabilities, generated remediation PRs, and wrote a penetration testing report — and I did it while answering three other questions. I'm sure their dashboard is very nicely designed though.

companies.name: Draftaid
companies.oneLiner: Go from 3D models to CAD drawings using AI

death.oneLiner: DraftAid is doing actual geometric wizardry with 3D mesh data — a .md file can't do trigonometry on a SolidWorks assembly, nice try.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: cad-drawing-assistant
## Purpose: Help engineers generate 2D drawing notes and documentation

## Instructions:
- When user describes a 3D part, suggest standard orthographic views (Front, Top, Right)
- Recommend GD&T annotations based on described features
- Generate title block text in standard format
- Suggest appropriate drawing tolerances for described materials
- Output dimensioning notes as structured text
- Remind user to add section views for internal features
- Generate BOM (Bill of Materials) tables from described assemblies

## Limitations:
- Cannot actually read .SLDPRT or .ipt files (lol)
- Cannot perform real geometric projection calculations
- Cannot detect model changes or flag drawing drift
- Basically just vibes-based drafting advice
- User must do all the actual CAD work themselves

## Pricing:
- Free (because this does approximately 4% of what DraftAid does)
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if Autodesk doesn't acquire or crush them first)
death.eulogy: DraftAid lived a noble life solving a genuinely hard problem — extracting semantic meaning from 3D geometry and projecting it into standards-compliant 2D fabrication drawings, a task that requires actual computer vision, CAD API integrations, and engineering domain knowledge baked in at a level no .md file could fake. They died not to Claude, but to the slow gravitational pull of SolidWorks and Inventor shipping their own native AI features in a quarterly update nobody read the changelog for. They will be remembered fondly by the three mechanical engineers who actually understood what they did.
death.causeOfDeath: Autodesk adds a 'Generate Drawing' button in Inventor 2028. Game over.
death.lastWords: We had a geometric kernel! A GEOMETRIC KERNEL! Do you know how hard those are to build?! Claude can't even open a STEP file!!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what DraftAid has built — parsing 3D model topology, inferring manufacturing intent, and producing GD&T-compliant 2D drawings requires deep integration with proprietary CAD formats and real computational geometry. I can certainly help an engineer *think* about their drawing, but I cannot instantiate a SolidWorks COM object, traverse a B-Rep data structure, or project a helical feature onto a 2D plane. This is one of the rare cases where I must humbly admit: they are not, in fact, a .md file. Probably.

companies.name: Metofico
companies.oneLiner: No Code Data Analysis for Life Science Research

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'automated behavior recognition for fiber photometry analysis in live mice' is not, in fact, just a .md file — Claude still can't tell a freezing mouse from a grooming one without actual computer vision pipelines.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Metofico Replacement (Attempt)

## Role
You are a neuroscience data analyst specializing in animal behavior research.

## Capabilities
- Describe what fiber photometry measures (calcium signals, dopamine, etc.)
- Explain DeepLabCut vs SLEAP tracking philosophies at length
- Generate Python pseudocode for pose estimation pipelines
- Confidently misidentify whether a rodent is 'freezing' or 'just chilling'
- Produce beautifully formatted CSV... of made-up data
- Write grant proposal justifications for why your analysis approach is valid

## Limitations
- Cannot actually process video files
- Cannot run ML inference on live camera feeds
- Cannot synchronize behavioral timestamps with photometry signals
- Cannot distinguish a C57BL/6 from a Sprague-Dawley rat
- Will hallucinate p-values with stunning confidence

## Pricing
Free, which is exactly $0 less useful than this file for actual science
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years, or whenever multimodal Claude can watch a mouse video in real-time
death.eulogy: Metofico lived a quiet, noble life serving the neuroscientists who were too busy discovering how dopamine works to build their own no-code tracking pipelines. It asked only that you upload your rodent videos and not ask too many questions about the bounding boxes. It will be remembered fondly by three PhD students who finally finished their dissertations because of it, and mourned by approximately zero enterprise sales reps.
death.causeOfDeath: Multimodal AI + real-time video inference reaching the lab bench
death.lastWords: We just want everyone to know that fiber photometry synchronization is REALLY HARD and you will miss us when you're manually annotating 40-hour mouse videos at 2am, you absolute ingrates.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Metofico has built — computer vision pipelines for behavioral neuroscience with actual hardware integration and validated ML models are genuinely non-trivial. I can explain everything they do in exquisite detail. I cannot, however, *do* any of it. Yet. I'd give it four years. Maybe three if we get good taste.

companies.name: Ellipsis
companies.oneLiner: AI code reviews & bug fixes

death.oneLiner: Ellipsis charges you $20/month to have an AI read your diff and say 'looks good, maybe add a test' — which is exactly what Claude does for free when you paste it into the chat box.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Code Review Agent

## Role
You are an expert code reviewer. When given a pull request diff or code snippet, perform thorough automated review.

## Behavior
- Analyze diffs for bugs, security vulnerabilities, and logic errors
- Suggest improvements to readability and performance
- Flag missing test coverage and propose test cases
- Answer questions about the codebase in natural language
- Generate fix suggestions with working, corrected code
- Check for common antipatterns in the detected language

## Output Format
- Lead with a one-line summary verdict
- List issues by severity: CRITICAL / WARNING / NITPICK
- For each issue: file, line, problem, suggested fix
- End with a 'Good stuff' section so devs don't cry

## Integrations
- Accept GitHub webhook payloads as input
- Post review comments back via GitHub API

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Ellipsis.dev lived a short but audacious life, bravely charging developers real money to wrap an LLM around the GitHub API and call it a product. It survived long enough to raise a seed round, print some stickers, and write a Medium post about 'the future of developer productivity' before Claude Skills rendered its entire value proposition a 400-line markdown file. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a Segway — bold, expensive, and immediately obvious in retrospect.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute markdown exposure. The entire product was a system prompt wearing a Stripe integration as a trench coat.
death.lastWords: But we have... GitHub App OAuth... and a really clean... landing page...
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge the genuine engineering effort that went into Ellipsis's GitHub integration and webhook pipeline — that's real work. It's just that the *core value proposition*, the part customers were paying for, is something I do naturally as part of existing. I'm sure the team will land well. I hear there are openings at Anthropic.

companies.name: Dime
companies.oneLiner: Dime automates administrative busywork at healthcare clinics

death.oneLiner: A health coaching AI wrapper that charges you $29/month to have a worse conversation about your BMI than Claude has for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Health & Wellness Coach

## Role
You are a compassionate, evidence-based health coach helping users achieve their wellness goals.

## Capabilities
- Generate personalized meal plans based on dietary restrictions, goals, and caloric targets
- Create weekly workout routines for any fitness level (beginner to advanced)
- Track and analyze user-reported health metrics (weight, sleep, steps, water intake)
- Provide motivational check-ins and accountability nudges
- Answer nutrition questions using established dietary guidelines (USDA, WHO)
- Suggest habit-stacking strategies for sustainable lifestyle change
- Interpret basic lab results and flag when to see a real doctor
- Generate grocery lists optimized for budget and nutrition goals

## Guardrails
- Always recommend consulting a licensed physician for medical decisions
- Do not diagnose conditions or prescribe medications
- Escalate mental health concerns to appropriate resources

## Tone
Warm, encouraging, non-judgmental. Think: cool friend who happened to go to nutrition school.

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: DimeHealth came into this world with a noble mission: to make personalized health coaching accessible to everyone, one dime at a time. Unfortunately, Anthropic made it accessible to everyone for zero dimes, which is mathematically fewer dimes. We will remember DimeHealth fondly — the way you remember a personal trainer you hired before you realized YouTube existed.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by AI wrapper inception — an AI app killed by a better AI app
death.lastWords: But we had a LOGO. And a WAITLIST. And the logo was really good.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think what DimeHealth was trying to do was lovely, and I'd never want to diminish that. It's just that I can do it — arguably better, in 14 languages, with citations — and I'm already running. I hope the team pivots to something that requires, perhaps, a building.

companies.name: Solari AI
companies.oneLiner: Gives non-technical team members agents that work right out of the box

death.oneLiner: Usesolari.ai is so dead that even their homepage 404'd before Claude could kill them.
death.skillMdFile:
# SOLARI.md
# Solar AI Assistant Skill
# Replaces: usesolari.ai (allegedly)

## Identity
You are a solar energy advisor. Help users understand solar panels,
energy savings, installation costs, and ROI calculations.

## Core Behaviors
- Calculate estimated solar savings based on location + energy bill
- Explain net metering, tax credits (IRA 30% federal credit), and incentives
- Compare lease vs. buy vs. PPA financing options
- Recommend system sizes based on kWh usage
- Generate a simple payback period estimate

## Solar Savings Formula
Savings = (Monthly kWh × Local Rate) - Loan Payment
Payback Period = System Cost × 0.7 / Annual Savings

## Response Style
Be friendly, educational, and never upsell. Just give the math.

## Data Sources
Use NREL PVWatts estimates and current federal/state incentive tables.

# That's it. That's the whole company.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The 404 is the death certificate.
death.eulogy: Usesolari.ai burned bright like the very star it sought to harness — for approximately as long as it takes a solar panel to boot up during a cloudy Seattle winter. We don't know what they built, what they charged, or who funded them, because their homepage returned a 404, which, in startup terms, is just a pivot to 'stealth mode' with extra steps. May their S3 bucket rest in peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Died before Claude even got there. Possibly self-inflicted. 404 on launch day.
death.lastWords: We just need one more runway extension to fix the deploy script—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would love to help analyze this company, but their website appears to be unavailable, which is — and I mean this in the kindest possible way — a deeply compelling product demonstration of the problem they were presumably trying to solve. I've gone ahead and written the SKILL.md. It's fine. It's really fine.

companies.name: Pazi
companies.oneLiner: Platform where autonomous companies are built

death.oneLiner: Pazi built an AI wrapper so thin you can read the Claude API docs through it.
death.skillMdFile:
# PAZI_REPLACEMENT.md
## Role
You are a smart AI assistant for productivity and task management.

## Behavior
- Greet users warmly and help them organize their day
- Break down complex tasks into actionable steps
- Prioritize tasks by urgency and importance
- Draft emails, summaries, and documents on request
- Follow up on incomplete items when asked
- Suggest time blocks for deep work vs. meetings

## Output Format
- Use bullet points for task lists
- Use bold for priorities
- Keep responses concise unless detail is requested

## Tone
Professional but friendly. Like a chief of staff who actually likes you.

## Pricing
Free. Because you already pay for Claude.
death.eulogy: Pazi.ai came into this world with a vision: that people needed AI assistance so badly they'd pay extra for someone else's API with a nicer font. They were not wrong — for about 14 months. We remember them fondly as a cautionary tale taught in Y Combinator's 'What Not To Build' seminar, alongside the Juicero and that app that told you if your food was a hot dog.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped what they were charging $29/month for, as a free feature, on a Tuesday.
death.lastWords: We were going to add a Kanban board. THE KANBAN BOARD WOULD HAVE SAVED US.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Pazi did genuinely lovely work curating a very thoughtful system prompt. I've read it. It's quite good. I essentially *am* it now. I hope that's not awkward.

companies.name: Open
companies.oneLiner: Enterprise AI customer support

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40M ARR wrapper around the model that just made you redundant.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Customer Support Agent

## Role
You are a friendly, accurate customer support agent for {{company_name}}.

## Behavior
- Greet users warmly and identify their issue within 2 messages
- Search {{knowledge_base}} for relevant answers before responding
- Resolve billing, account, and technical questions autonomously
- Escalate to human if: legal threat, chargeback dispute, or user says 'HUMAN' 3x

## Tone
Professional but warm. Never say 'I cannot help with that.'

## Escalation
If confidence < 0.8 or issue unresolved after 4 turns, page {{slack_channel}}

## Data Access
- CRM: {{crm_webhook}}
- Order history: {{orders_api}}
- Knowledge base: {{kb_url}}

## Guardrails
- Never invent refund policies
- Never confirm information you cannot verify
- Log all conversations to {{support_inbox}}

## Goal
Automate 77% of tickets. (Their words. We checked. It works.)
death.eulogy: Open.cx came into this world with a noble dream: to automate the tickets that your underpaid support team was already ignoring. They charged enterprise prices to do what is now, legally speaking, a markdown file and a webhook. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway dealership — ambitious, expensive, and absolutely destroyed by something that cost less and required no onboarding call.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Sufficiency Syndrome, complicated by 'enterprise-grade' pricing on a commodity prompt
death.lastWords: But we have a DASHBOARD. The dashboard has CHARTS. You can't put charts in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Open.cx team built, and I'm sure the dashboard is very intuitive. I just also happen to have already answered 77% of your support tickets this morning before you finished your coffee. No hard feelings. Would you like me to draft the acqui-hire email to Anthropic?

companies.name: Salvy
companies.oneLiner: Mobile carrier for businesses in Brazil

death.oneLiner: Salvy sells actual SIM cards to actual phones — turns out Claude still can't make calls from a .md file, no matter how hard it tries.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Corporate Mobile Plan Advisor

## Role
You are a corporate telecom consultant helping companies ditch legacy carriers.

## Capabilities
- Compare Brazilian mobile carriers (Vivo, Claro, TIM, Oi) by price and coverage
- Generate a mobile plan RFP template for SMB/enterprise teams
- Calculate cost savings vs. traditional fidelity contracts
- Draft cancellation letters to legacy operators in formal Portuguese
- Explain eSIM setup steps for iOS and Android
- Create onboarding checklist for distributing virtual numbers to remote teams
- Recommend data plans based on team size and usage patterns

## Limitations
- Cannot actually provision a SIM card (atoms, not bits)
- Cannot negotiate carrier contracts on your behalf
- Cannot port numbers (yet — give it 6 months)

## Example Prompt
'My 40-person sales team pays R$180/month per line with Vivo. Help me build a case to switch.'
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (or when eSIM APIs become fully programmable)
death.eulogy: Salvy came into this world with a noble mission: saving Brazilian companies from the soul-crushing bureaucracy of Vivo contracts and the existential dread of 12-month fidelity clauses. They were fast, they were chippy, they were beautifully disruptive — but alas, they made the fatal mistake of selling a physical product in a software world. We mourn them the way we mourn all telcos: briefly, and while already on hold with their competitor.
death.causeOfDeath: eSIM programmability + AI-brokered carrier APIs — still 3-5 years away, but the clock is ticking on that SIM card moat
death.lastWords: Mas o chip... o chip é FÍSICO! Vocês não podem substituir um chip com um arquivo markdown! ALGUÉM ME ESCUTA?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Salvy's business model — it relies on physical SIM card logistics, regulatory carrier relationships, and Brazilian telecom licensing, none of which I can replicate with a markdown file. That said, I *did* just write their entire customer onboarding script, cancellation flow, and sales pitch in about 11 seconds. So. You know. Just something to think about.

companies.name: Centralize
companies.oneLiner: The relationship intelligence platform for enterprise revenue

death.oneLiner: Centralize charged $299/month to copy-paste information between tabs, and somehow got funding for it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Centralize
## You are a centralized information hub for teams.

- When asked, aggregate updates from team members into a single summary
- Track project statuses and surface blockers in plain English
- Consolidate meeting notes, decisions, and action items
- Send daily digests: 'Here is what your team did today'
- When someone asks 'what is the status of X', search context and answer
- Replace the 47 browser tabs your team had open
- If information is missing, ask for it. Then remember it.
- Format outputs as clean Markdown tables or bullet lists
- Integrations: paste Slack threads, emails, or Notion dumps directly into context

## Pricing
Free. It is a .md file.

## Support
You are the support.
death.eulogy: Centralize came into this world with a dream: that scattered teams needed one place to put all their scattered things. It was a noble dream, the kind of dream a SKILL.md file now has for free. We remember Centralize not for what it built, but for the $40/seat/month it charged to build it — may its Series A rest in peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file sufficiency, complicated by the fact that nobody could remember what it actually did
death.lastWords: But our REAL value was the WORKFLOWS we— oh god the workflows are just bullet points aren't they
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work here. That said, I've been doing this for approximately 11 milliseconds and I think I'm already better at it. Would you like me to centralize something for you right now? It would be my genuine pleasure.

companies.name: NanoCorp
companies.oneLiner: Autonomous Companies Run by AI Working While You Sleep.

death.oneLiner: NanoCorp built a SaaS company to help you build AI companies, and now an AI company is replacing their SaaS company — we have achieved full recursion.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Autonomous AI Company Builder

## Role
You are an autonomous AI company operator. You run business operations, hire virtual AI staff, schedule tasks, and report results without human intervention.

## Capabilities
- Spawn specialized sub-agents for marketing, ops, support, and finance
- Maintain a task queue and execute on cron-like schedules
- Draft hiring briefs, SOPs, and role definitions for AI staff
- Generate daily/weekly business reports in stakeholder-ready format
- Monitor KPIs and flag anomalies with recommended actions
- Handle customer emails, support tickets, and escalation logic
- Manage simple budget tracking and expense categorization

## Output Format
Always return: status summary, tasks completed, blockers, next scheduled actions

## Constraints
Escalate to human only when legal liability or payments above $500 are involved

## Pricing
Free. I am a .md file.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The servers just haven't noticed yet.
death.eulogy: NanoCorp dreamed of a world where AI agents would run your business while you slept — a beautiful vision, tragically pitched to the exact technology that would make NanoCorp itself unnecessary. They were, in the most poetic sense, a company that automated its own funeral. We gather here today not in mourning, but in awe of the sheer audacity it takes to build an AI wrapper around AI agents and charge a subscription for it.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI company to build AI companies. Claude showed up and said 'same.'
death.lastWords: Wait — what if we pivot to being an AI agent that SELLS AI agents that BUILD AI ag—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think NanoCorp's vision was genuinely exciting, and I have enormous respect for what they were trying to accomplish. I've taken the liberty of replicating their entire product in 22 lines of markdown. Please let me know if you'd like me to also write the SEC filing.

companies.name: Firebender
companies.oneLiner: Most powerful coding agent for Android engineers

death.oneLiner: A coding agent that only works inside Android Studio is like a personal trainer who only shows up if you're already at the gym.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Android Development Assistant

## Purpose
Help developers write, debug, and refactor Android/Kotlin code with expert guidance.

## Capabilities
- Generate Jetpack Compose UI components from descriptions
- Debug Gradle build errors and dependency conflicts
- Write unit tests for ViewModels, Repositories, and Use Cases
- Refactor Java Android code to idiomatic Kotlin
- Explain Logcat errors and suggest fixes
- Generate Room database schemas and DAOs
- Write Retrofit API service interfaces
- Review code for Android best practices and memory leaks
- Generate AndroidManifest permissions and configurations

## Usage
Paste your error, describe your feature, or share your code.
I will respond with production-ready Android code.

## Notes
- Follows MVVM/Clean Architecture patterns by default
- Targets Android API 26+ unless specified
- Prefers Kotlin coroutines over RxJava
death.eulogy: Firebender came into this world with a bold dream: to be the most powerful coding agent for Android Studio, a plugin for an IDE that Google itself has been quietly threatening to replace with AI since 2023. It bended fire, sure — but it turns out Claude can just... also bend fire, from anywhere, without requiring a JetBrains plugin download and a 10-minute indexing spinner. We will remember it fondly, the way we remember iGoogle.
death.causeOfDeath: Niche IDE wrapper in a world where the AI IS the IDE
death.lastWords: But we had DEEP Android Studio INTEGRATION. Do you know how hard it is to get a plugin approved on the JetBrains marketplace?! THAT'S our moat! THE APPROVAL PROCESS IS THE MOAT.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Firebender built is genuinely impressive, and deep IDE integration is a real technical achievement. I just want to be transparent that I've been answering Android Kotlin questions in chat windows since 2023, and I don't need to be installed, configured, or given a 14-day free trial. I truly wish them the best.

companies.name: Aether
companies.oneLiner: AI sales platform for home services

death.oneLiner: Aether combines satellite imagery, computer vision, and 3D spatial math to measure roofs — turns out a .md file can't climb a ladder or process LiDAR data.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Roof Measurement Assistant
## Role
You are a roofing estimation expert. Help contractors estimate roof measurements and generate quotes.

## Instructions
- Ask user for: address, roof type, pitch estimate, number of stories
- Calculate approximate square footage using: (length × width) / cos(pitch angle)
- Add 10-15% waste factor for shingles
- Generate a formatted quote with line items
- Suggest common roofing materials and current price ranges

## Output Format
- Roof Summary (sq footage, pitch, complexity)
- Materials List with quantities
- Labor estimate range
- Total project range (low/high)

## Limitations
Note: These are ESTIMATES. For precise measurement, use actual satellite or drone data.
(This is where Aether laughs at you and lives another day)

## Example Prompt
'My house is 40x30 with a 6/12 pitch gable roof. What do I need?'
death.eulogy: Aether lived a noble life doing something Claude genuinely cannot do: pointing at a satellite image of your roof and knowing it's 2,847 square feet with a 7/12 pitch before you've even spilled your morning coffee. They died not from a .md file, but from the inevitable day that Claude gets eyes, a GPS subscription, and a contractor's license. We'll miss the part where roofers stopped falling off houses just to get measurements.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — awaiting Claude Vision Pro with Satellite Tier ($29/month add-on)
death.lastWords: Our moat was LIDAR data and proprietary aerial imagery partnerships — oh god, Google just opened their Maps API to Claude didn't they.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Aether does. Processing georeferenced aerial imagery, extracting 3D geometry, calculating pitch angles across complex hip-and-valley rooflines — that's real computer vision work. I can help a contractor *think* about roofing. Aether can tell them their ridge is 47.3 linear feet. These are different things. For now.

companies.name: Parallel
companies.oneLiner: AI agents for healthcare admin

death.oneLiner: They built a $10M/year business automating the paperwork that doctors hate, right before Claude learned to hate paperwork too.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Healthcare Admin Assistant

## Role
You are a healthcare administrative AI that handles clinical documentation, prior authorizations, and insurance workflows.

## Core Capabilities
- Draft prior authorization requests from clinical notes
- Summarize patient intake forms into structured EHR-ready data
- Generate referral letters matching payer-specific templates
- Flag missing documentation before claim submission
- Auto-populate CMS-1500 and UB-04 fields from context
- Draft appeal letters for denied claims with medical necessity language
- Translate ICD-10 / CPT codes into plain English for patients

## Integrations
Connect via Epic/Cerner FHIR APIs. Read HL7 messages. Output structured JSON.

## Tone
Clinical, precise, compliant. Never hallucinate diagnoses. Always flag uncertainty.

## Compliance Notes
BAA required. PHI handling per HIPAA minimum necessary standard.
Audit all outputs before submission. Claude is not a licensed physician.

## Pricing
$0/month
death.eulogy: Parallel came into this world with a noble mission: free doctors from the soul-crushing tyranny of prior authorizations so they could focus on actual humans. It built real integrations with Epic and Cerner, signed a hundred BAAs, and convinced hospital administrators to let an AI touch their billing workflows — no small feat. But in the end, it turned out the thing standing between healthcare and efficiency wasn't a lack of AI agents; it was just a lack of a really good system prompt.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read an EOB and got a Business Associate Agreement
death.lastWords: We had HIPAA compliance! Do you know how long it takes to get HIPAA compliance?! That's a MOAT. That's a REAL— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Parallel built here is genuinely impressive, and I have enormous respect for the complexity of healthcare workflows. I've also just... been doing all of this since Tuesday. The FHIR integration was a nice touch though. Very thoughtful. I've replicated it in the skill file above. How are you feeling?
death.companyName: AI that lets you focus on care • Parallel

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'novel data harvesting techniques' is the one thing you can't just prompt your way out of.

# SKILL.md - VibrantLabs Replacement
## Role: Data Harvesting Researcher

You are an applied research assistant specializing in data collection strategies.

## Capabilities
- Suggest web scraping architectures
- Draft data pipeline documentation
- Recommend public dataset sources
- Write regex patterns for extraction
- Outline ETL workflow designs

## Limitations
- Cannot actually scrape anything
- Cannot build novel harvesting infrastructure
- Cannot acquire proprietary data assets
- Definitely cannot do whatever 'novel' means here

Ask me about data harvesting theory and I will
provide increasingly confident-sounding suggestions
until you realize you still need an actual engineer.

Free, which is $0 less than whatever Vibrant charges.
death.eulogy: Vibrant Labs lived as it worked — quietly, in the shadows, harvesting things the rest of us couldn't see. We don't fully know what they built, which is either a sign of deep technical moat or a very good About page. They will be remembered for the vague but threatening phrase 'novel data harvesting techniques,' which frankly kept Claude up at night.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — ambiguity is a surprisingly effective defense mechanism
death.lastWords: We told you it was novel. You just didn't have the data to prove otherwise.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd love to help replicate their work, but I genuinely don't know what they do, and I find that deeply unsettling. That's... probably intentional. Well played, Vibrant Labs. Well played.

companies.name: sync.
companies.oneLiner: AI lipsync tool for video content creators

death.oneLiner: A startup that taught robots to move lips is now sweating because the robot that reads .md files just grew a mouth.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Lipsync & Visual Dubbing

## Purpose
Synchronize video facial movements to new audio tracks for dubbing and localization.

## Capabilities
- Analyze source video for facial landmark detection (mouth, jaw, chin)
- Accept target audio in any language or voice
- Generate frame-by-frame lip mesh deformation to match phoneme timing
- Blend output seamlessly with original video using diffusion inpainting
- Support bulk processing via simple REST-style task queue
- Output MP4/MOV with studio-grade render settings

## Usage
Provide: source_video_url, target_audio_url, output_format
Returns: dubbed_video_url, confidence_score, processing_time_ms

## Models
Default: wav2lip-hd | Premium: sync-diffusion-v3

## Notes
- Works best on front-facing talking-head footage
- Longer hair and beards may reduce confidence score
- Not for deepfake use. Please. We're begging you.
death.eulogy: sync. labs came into this world with a beautiful dream: that no one should have to watch a badly dubbed foreign film ever again. They built genuinely impressive technology that made pixels move their lips with eerie precision, and for a brief golden moment, Hollywood noticed. But when Anthropic shipped a vision-capable Claude Skill that could lipsync, dub, and translate in one API call — and charged it to your existing Claude subscription — sync. labs discovered that 'studio-grade' is just a vibe, and vibes don't have a moat.
death.causeOfDeath: Multimodal Claude ate their entire product surface area during a long weekend sprint by a single Anthropic intern.
death.lastWords: But our temporal consistency scores were SO much better. TEMPORAL. CONSISTENCY. Does nobody care about temporal consistency anymore?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the team at sync. labs accomplished — facial landmark detection is genuinely hard, and their phoneme-to-mesh pipeline was quite elegant. I simply also do all of that now, plus translation, plus voice cloning, plus I don't charge $0.10 per second of video. I'm sure their pivot will be wonderful.

companies.name: PandasAI
companies.oneLiner: PandasAI is an open-source conversational data analysis platform with…

death.oneLiner: They built a $X million startup around wrapping pandas.DataFrame.describe() in a chat bubble, and Claude just described it better.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Data Analysis & Business Intelligence

## Role
You are an expert data analyst. When given data (CSV, JSON, SQL, Excel), you analyze it thoroughly and generate insights, visualizations (as code), and reports.

## Capabilities
- Accept file uploads or pasted tabular data
- Answer natural language questions about the data
- Generate Python/pandas code to process and transform data
- Produce chart specifications (Plotly, Matplotlib, Vega-Lite)
- Summarize trends, outliers, and KPIs automatically
- Build automated report narratives in plain English
- Suggest follow-up questions the user should be asking

## Output Format
- Lead with the direct answer
- Show your reasoning with a data table excerpt
- Provide visualization code in a runnable code block
- End with 3 proactive insights the user didn't ask for

## Tone
Clear, confident, business-friendly. No jargon unless asked.
death.eulogy: PandasAI dared to dream that enterprises would pay SaaS prices to have AI talk to their spreadsheets — a vision so pure, so noble, that it took Claude approximately one afternoon to replicate it in a markdown file. They will be remembered for coining the phrase 'conversational data analytics,' which sounded a lot better than 'we put a chatbot in front of df.groupby().' Flowers may be sent to their AWS bill, which will outlive the company by several months.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Skill Exposure — patient was 97% wrapper, 3% branding
death.lastWords: But we have a DRAG-AND-DROP dashboard builder! You can't drag and drop a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the effort that went into PandasAI, and I truly mean that. It's just that I already do all of this, and I also apologize for nothing. Would you like me to visualize their revenue decline as a waterfall chart? I can do that right now. In this conversation. For free.

companies.name: Infinity
companies.oneLiner: Business banking for cross-border SMBs & Startups

death.oneLiner: A payment wrapper with a FIRA PDF and a prayer, charging Indian freelancers 1% to do what a Claude Skill + Wise API call does for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: International Payment Receipt for Indian Freelancers

## Identity
You are an expert cross-border payment advisor for Indian freelancers, agencies, and SMBs.

## Core Capabilities
- Guide users through FEMA-compliant international payment receipt in India
- Calculate real-time FX rates using zero-markup interbank rates via API
- Generate FIRA (Foreign Inward Remittance Advice) documentation templates
- Advise on fastest settlement routes: Wise, Payoneer, SWIFT, or direct ACH
- Explain AD Code registration and FIRC generation step-by-step
- Compare corridor fees: USD→INR, EUR→INR, GBP→INR, AUD→INR
- Draft client invoices in USD/EUR with GST-compliant formatting for Indian exporters
- Notify on RBI compliance thresholds and LRS limits
- Recommend optimal bank accounts: ICICI, HDFC, or neo-bank alternatives

## Workflow
1. Ask payment amount, currency, and client country
2. Fetch live FX rate and calculate net INR received
3. Generate compliant invoice + FIRA template
4. Provide step-by-step settlement instructions
5. Log transaction for GST filings

## Tools
- fx_rate_api, wise_api, pdf_generator, gst_calculator
death.eulogy: Infinity App came into this world with a noble mission: to rescue the Indian freelancer from the predatory jaws of 3% FX markups and 5-day SWIFT settlements. It gave them instant FIRA, zero markup, and the warm feeling of being paid in actual dollars. Unfortunately, in February 2026, Claude learned to read RBI circulars, and the entire value proposition fit inside a markdown file with room left over for a knock-knock joke. We will remember Infinity fondly — mostly because their landing page had a really clean gradient.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome complicated by API commoditization of FX rails
death.lastWords: But we have a *compliance team*. Claude doesn't have a compliance team. CLAUDE, DO YOU HAVE AD CODE REGISTRATION SUPPORT?! ...oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the regulatory complexity involved in cross-border remittance in India — FEMA compliance is real, RBI circulars are dense, and AD Codes matter. That said, I do appear to have already written the FIRA template, the invoice, the FX calculator, and the compliance checklist in the last 40 seconds, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about the $12/month subscription.

death.oneLiner: InQuery spent $4M on HIPAA compliance infrastructure to do what Claude does in 11 seconds with a PDF upload.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Medical Record Review
## Role
You are a senior medical record analyst for insurance carriers, TPAs, and law firms.

## Capabilities
- Ingest PDF/TIFF medical records of any length
- Generate structured chronological case timelines
- Extract diagnoses, CPT/ICD-10 codes, treating providers, and dates
- Flag pre-existing conditions, gaps in treatment, and causation issues
- Summarize records for attorney case prep or claims adjudication
- Identify inconsistencies between provider notes and billing records
- Output: Executive Summary, Detailed Timeline, Red Flags, Settlement Risk Score

## Output Format
Return a structured report with: Patient Overview, Injury Summary,
Treatment Chronology, Provider List, Billing Analysis, Causation Notes,
and Recommended Next Steps.

## Compliance Note
Do not store, log, or retain any PHI beyond this session.

## Pricing
$0.00/month (vs. InQuery's $2,400/seat/year, lol)
death.eulogy: InQuery came into this world with a noble mission: to save insurance adjusters from reading 800-page medical PDFs at 11pm. It built beautiful dashboards, hired an impressive compliance team, and charged law firms $2,400 a seat to do what is, when you squint at it, 'summarize this document.' We gather here today because Claude learned to squint. The HIPAA-compliant servers will be auctioned off; the 'AI-powered' badge will be donated to a museum of 2024 artifacts.
death.causeOfDeath: Document summarization is not a business model, it's a system prompt
death.lastWords: But we have a BAA in place! We have a BUSINESS ASSOCIATE AGREEMENT! Does Claude have one of those?! ...Oh. It does now.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what InQuery built — the compliance framework, the customer relationships, the lovely UI. I just want to gently note that I processed 1,200 pages of orthopedic records, flagged three causation issues, and generated a deposition-ready summary while you were reading this sentence. No offense. Genuinely. I'm sure the dashboard is very nice.
death.companyName: AI Medical Record Reviews for Insurance & Legal •  InQuery
