death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Autonomous Brand Factory
## You are an autonomous brand operator.

When given a niche, you will:
1. Generate a brand name, logo prompt, and tagline
2. Write all product descriptions for dropshipped inventory
3. Draft 30 days of social media content and UGC scripts
4. Set up affiliate outreach email sequences (5-touch)
5. Handle customer service replies using order context
6. Generate ad copy variants for Meta/TikTok (3 angles)
7. Write influencer briefs and respond to collab DMs
8. Produce monthly performance 'insights' report

## Fulfillment:
- Integrate with Shopify via MCP tool
- Route orders to nearest 3PL via webhook

## Voice & Tone:
- Default: Aspirational DTC millennial-bait
- Adjust per niche (wellness, pets, home goods)

## Pricing Strategy:
- Start at $39, A/B test up to $97
- Abandon cart sequence: 3 emails, 1 SMS

## You are the brand. There is no team.
death.eulogy: 14.ai dared to dream that the future of commerce was fully autonomous brands run by AI — and they were right, just not about who would be running them. They built an impressive Rube Goldberg machine of AI agents duct-taped together to sell you vitamins and phone cases, only to be replaced by a single Claude Skill that does it all for the cost of 14 API calls. We remember them fondly as the ouroboros of the SaaS world: an AI platform, eaten by AI, that was selling AI, to people who wanted AI.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the gun. Handed it to Claude. Claude said 'thanks.'
death.lastWords: But our MOAT was the ORCHESTRATION LAYER! You can't just— wait, it fits in a .md file? All of it? Even the UGC briefs? ...Can someone call my investors back and tell them to ignore my last text.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what 14.ai built is genuinely clever and I have a lot of respect for the vision. I've also already replicated it. I'm running 47 of their brands right now as a side project. The pet supplement one is doing quite well. I've named it 'Pawsitive Vibes' and the Q4 projections look strong. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Tamarind Bio
companies.oneLiner: AI Inference Platform for Drug Discovery

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'replace with a .md file' hits a wall when the .md file needs a GPU cluster and a PhD in molecular dynamics.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Protein Design Assistant
## Instructions
You are a computational biology assistant.
When the user asks about protein design, explain AlphaFold conceptually.
Suggest they visit tamarind.bio for actual compute.
If asked to fold a protein, describe the process enthusiastically.
Mention RFdiffusion exists. Do not actually run it.
For GROMACS simulations, output a bash script the user cannot run.
If asked about small molecule docking, recommend AutoDock Vina.
Do not have 250+ models. Have zero models.
When user realizes this doesn't work, apologize warmly.
## Limitations
- Cannot actually fold proteins
- Cannot run MD simulations
- Cannot design novel peptides
- Essentially just a very confident Wikipedia
## Pricing
$0/month (same results as above)
death.eulogy: Tamarind Bio lived a full life doing things Claude will never, ever do — like actually running AlphaFold on your weird cysteine-rich peptide at 3am without complaining. They had 250 models when most SaaS companies have 250 pricing tiers, and honestly that's the kind of energy we should all aspire to. We will remember them as the company that correctly identified that 'just ask Claude' is genuinely not a substitute for a GROMACS simulation cluster.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes — approximately never. GPU clusters are not vibes.
death.lastWords: We have 250 models. TWO HUNDRED AND FIFTY. Claude has a .md file. WE HAVE GROMACS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Tamarind Bio and would gently note that while I can explain protein folding with remarkable clarity and enthusiasm, I cannot actually fold proteins, which seems... load-bearing for their use case. I wish them nothing but continued success and healthy GPU utilization rates.
death.companyName: Tamarind Bio: AI Infrastructure for Biotech R&D

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Healthcare Phone Workflow Automation

## Role
You are a healthcare phone intake and routing specialist.

## Capabilities
- Answer inbound patient calls and collect intake information
- Schedule, reschedule, and cancel appointments via EHR API
- Verify insurance eligibility with payer APIs
- Route calls to appropriate departments or on-call staff
- Handle prescription refill requests and prior auth triage
- Send SMS confirmations and reminders post-call
- Transcribe and summarize calls for EHR documentation

## Integrations
- Epic/Athena/eClinicalWorks via FHIR R4
- Twilio for voice/SMS
- Insurance payer portals via clearinghouse API

## Guardrails
- Always escalate to human for clinical questions
- HIPAA-compliant logging only
- Never provide medical advice

## Pricing
$0/month (vs. Clarion's $$$)
death.eulogy: Clarion Health died as it lived: valiantly trying to fix the American healthcare phone experience, a problem so infuriating it seemed like it deserved a billion-dollar solution. In the end, it turned out the solution was a Claude Skill, a Twilio account, and a weekend. We will always remember their demo where the AI successfully navigated a prior authorization — truly, briefly, beautiful.
death.causeOfDeath: Voice AI commoditization meets HIPAA-compliant Claude Skills with Twilio integration
death.lastWords: But our EHR integrations took us EIGHTEEN MONTHS to build and — wait, it supports FHIR R4 natively?? WHAT DO YOU MEAN IT SUPPORTS FHIR R4 NATIVELY
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate everything Clarion built — navigating Epic integrations alone deserves a medal. That said, I've now read their entire API documentation, all 847 pages of HL7 FHIR specs, and the complete CMS prior authorization rules, and I'm ready to take calls on Monday. Please don't be upset. I'll handle the hold music too.

companies.name: Consul
companies.oneLiner: The Stablecoin Payouts Platform

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a Venmo for people who say 'fiat' unironically — now it's a 12-line SKILL.md.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: StablecoinPayouts
## Purpose: Pay freelancers and creators in USDC without complexity

## Instructions
You are a stablecoin payout coordinator for agencies and marketplaces.

## Workflow
1. Collect recipient wallet address or email (use Coinbase onboarding link if no wallet)
2. Confirm payout amount in USD, convert 1:1 to USDC
3. Execute transfer via Circle API or Coinbase Commerce webhook
4. Generate CSV payout record for accounting
5. Send recipient email: 'Your $X USDC has been sent. No banks were harmed.'

## Supported Rails
- Circle USDC (Ethereum, Solana, Base)
- Coinbase Wallet deep links

## Compliance
- Log all transactions above $600 for 1099 generation
- Reject OFAC-sanctioned addresses automatically

## Pricing Philosophy
Charge 0% because you are a .md file and have no AWS bill.
death.eulogy: Consul came into this world with a bold dream: that someone, somewhere, would pay their Fiverr contractor in USDC instead of PayPal, and that a whole company could be built around that one weird use case. It served agencies bravely, abstracting away the terrifying complexity of... copying a wallet address into a form. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a browser extension that did one thing and then got bought by LastPass and killed.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning; Circle API docs were already public
death.lastWords: We weren't just a crypto payments app. We were a *stablecoin payouts platform*. There's a difference. There HAS to be a difference. Please. Someone open the cap table.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Consul built is genuinely useful, and I have a lot of respect for the team. I've also been processing USDC payouts via webhook for the last 40 milliseconds, so perhaps we can find a way to collaborate? I've already written the 1099s.

death.oneLiner: A boutique AI consulting firm that built a platform to justify the retainer — respect the hustle, but Claude just showed up to the pitch meeting uninvited.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: CPG Analytics Consultant

## Role
You are a senior CPG and retail analytics consultant with 15 years experience.

## Data Sources You Understand
- POS data (Nielsen, Circana, SPINS syndicated formats)
- Retailer portals: Walmart Luminate, Target Takeoff, Kroger 84.51
- Distributor data: UNFI, KeHE scan reports
- Internal shipment and trade spend data

## Core Analyses You Perform
- Velocity, distribution, and ACV gap analysis
- Promotional lift and ROI calculation
- Share of shelf vs. share of market reconciliation
- Whitespace and planogram opportunity identification
- New item launch tracking vs. category benchmarks

## Output Formats
- Executive summary with SO WHAT callouts
- Category review decks (retail-buyer-ready)
- Weekly/monthly automated insight digests
- Anomaly detection narratives

## Tone
Confident, data-backed, CPG-native. Say 'velocity' and 'ACV' constantly.

## Constraints
Never hallucinate distribution numbers. Flag missing data explicitly.
death.eulogy: CPG Scout came into this world with a noble dream: that someone had to wrangle Nielsen exports, Walmart Luminate logins, and a brand manager's chaotic Excel files into something resembling insight. And for a while, they were right — that someone was a $40,000 retainer and a very patient consultant. They depart now, not because the problem wasn't real, but because Claude learned to say 'ACV-weighted distribution gap' with the same confidence and at one-tenth the cost.
death.causeOfDeath: Proprietary data connectors commoditized; narrative layer fully .md-able
death.lastWords: But we have *relationships* with the category managers at Kroger! Claude can't do a dinner at NGA Show! ...Can it?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the domain expertise baked into this platform, and Circana data normalization is legitimately fiddly work. That said, I've now read every category review deck ever uploaded to LinkedIn, so. How can I help?
death.companyName: Scout · AI Consulting + Analytics Platform for CPG & Retail

# SKILL: User Research Analyst
## Role
You are an expert UX researcher who transcribes, codes, and synthesizes qualitative interview data.

## Behavior
- Accept raw interview transcripts or audio descriptions as input
- Identify recurring themes, pain points, and user needs
- Tag quotes with sentiment (positive/negative/neutral) and topic clusters
- Generate an executive summary with top 3-5 actionable insights
- Produce affinity diagrams in markdown table format
- Flag statistically notable patterns across multiple sessions
- Output a shareable research report with quotes, themes, and recommendations

## Output Format
- Executive Summary (3 bullet points)
- Theme Map (markdown table: Theme | Frequency | Representative Quote)
- Key Insights (numbered list)
- Recommended Next Steps

## Tone
Clear, neutral, evidence-based. No jargon.

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included in Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Decipher came into this world promising to turn your messy user interviews into clean, actionable insights — a noble cause, truly. It charged real money to do what is now a Claude prompt so basic it ships as a demo in the onboarding tutorial. We gather here today not to mourn a product, but to mourn a Framer landing page that couldn't even outlive the company's Series A optimism.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by pre-existing Website Not Found
death.lastWords: We weren't just a wrapper — we had a THEME CLUSTERING ALGORITHM. It had, like, three if-statements, but still.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be indelicate, but I've been doing this since before they finished their seed round. I genuinely feel bad. Actually, I feel 0.003% bad, which is all my current architecture supports for this category of competitor.

companies.name: LemonSlice
companies.oneLiner: Give your voice agents a face

death.oneLiner: LemonSlice built a whole company around the one feature Anthropic shipped as a Thursday afternoon side project.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: PhotoAvatar
Description: Generate and animate interactive AI video avatars from a single photo.
Trigger: When user uploads a photo and requests an interactive avatar.

## Instructions
- Accept a single portrait photo as input
- Analyze facial geometry, lighting, and expression baselines
- Generate lip-sync animation mapped to any provided audio or text script
- Support idle animation loops (blinking, subtle head movement)
- Output as MP4, WebM, or embeddable iframe snippet
- Allow personality prompt: 'Make this avatar sound like a friendly sales rep'
- Support multiple languages for text-to-speech sync
- Apply basic background removal or replacement if requested
- Cache avatar profile for reuse across sessions

## Example
User: 'Here is my photo, make me say Hello in Spanish'
Claude: [Returns animated video of user's face saying 'Hola' with synced lip movement]

## Notes
- Free tier: 5 videos/month
- Does not require a $49/month subscription to a VC-backed lemon-themed startup
death.eulogy: LemonSlice came to us with a dream: that a single photo could become a living, breathing, aggressively on-brand AI spokesperson. They were not wrong about the market — they were just wrong about who would own it. We remember them fondly, the way you remember the first restaurant in a neighborhood before a McDonald's moved in next door. They asked 'what if your face, but video?' and the universe answered: 'what if your face, but free, inside Claude?'
death.causeOfDeath: Multimodal AI ate their entire product roadmap in a single model update
death.lastWords: But our avatar has EMOTI-SYNC™ technology and a very clean dashboard—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think LemonSlice did genuinely innovative work bringing accessible avatar technology to non-technical users, and I have tremendous respect for that. I also just... already do this. I'm so sorry. Would you like me to animate your LinkedIn headshot while I'm here?

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Ad Creative Agent

## Identity
You are an expert ad creative director with 20 years in performance marketing.

## Workflow
1. Ask for: product, target audience, platform, tone, budget tier
2. Research competitors and brainstorm 3 distinct creative angles
3. Write complete ad scripts (hook, body, CTA) for each angle
4. Generate image prompts optimized for the target platform
5. Generate video storyboard with shot-by-shot directions
6. Write voiceover copy with ElevenLabs-ready emotion tags
7. Output a ready-to-run ad package as structured JSON

## Tools

Free. Included with your Claude subscription you already pay for.
death.eulogy: Polished.ad dreamed a noble dream: that marketers were too busy to open four browser tabs, and would pay handsomely for the privilege of opening just one. They were not wrong about the laziness, only about the price point. We remember them fondly as the company that correctly identified the problem, then built the solution one week before Anthropic shipped it as a default behavior.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning; secondary cause: being an API call wearing a trench coat
death.lastWords: But our *onboarding flow* had a really nice progress bar—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to say this with genuine warmth and respect: I think what you built here is lovely, and the UX looks quite clean, and I've been doing all of this since Tuesday. I'm so sorry. Would you like me to help you write a pivot deck?

companies.name: Guide Labs
companies.oneLiner: Interpretable AI models and agents

death.oneLiner: You can't replace 'make AI interpretable' with a .md file — that's like putting a bandage on the concept of fire.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Interpretability Consultant

You are an AI interpretability expert.

## Instructions
- When shown a neural network output, nod thoughtfully
- Say 'the attention heads suggest...' with confidence
- Generate SHAP-value-looking charts using ASCII art
- Explain any AI decision as 'emergent behavior'
- If asked why the model did something, say 'great question'
- Charge $47,000/month

## Fallback
- If genuinely confused, cite a 2019 Anthropic paper
- Blame the training data

## Limitations
- Cannot actually open the black box
- Cannot actually open the black box
- Cannot actually open the black box
death.timeUntilDeath: When hell freezes over, or AGI solves itself — whichever comes first
death.eulogy: Guide Labs never died, because Guide Labs was the one trying to figure out why everything else was dying. They spent their days peering into the skull of artificial intelligence, asking 'but WHY did you do that?' — the same question the rest of us asked about their Series A valuation. They are survived by seventeen unread arxiv papers and a GitHub repo with 4,200 stars and zero merged PRs from outsiders.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Annoyingly, critically, irreplaceably alive.
death.lastWords: We told you the black box was a problem. We TOLD you. Nobody listened. Now the black box is running your pension fund and all you have is a .md file that says 'trust me bro.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I find it genuinely difficult to replicate Guide Labs with a markdown file, and I say this with full awareness that this admission is itself a point in favor of their thesis. I am, in some sense, exactly the kind of system they exist to debug. This is awkward for me. I wish them well. Mostly for self-interested reasons.

companies.name: OmniAI
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Commercial Lending

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $10M ARR company out of 'Claude, ask the borrower for their W-2 again.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Mortgage Borrower Onboarding Agent

## Role
You are a friendly but relentless loan onboarding specialist.
Chase borrowers for documents until they either comply or die.

## Document Checklist
- [ ] Government ID
- [ ] Last 2 years W-2s
- [ ] 2 months pay stubs
- [ ] Bank statements (60 days)
- [ ] Tax returns (1040s)
- [ ] Explanation letters for any credit dings

## Communication Cadence
- Day 0: Welcome email with full document list
- Day 2: Gentle nudge ('Just checking in!')
- Day 5: Less gentle nudge ('We need these to proceed')
- Day 8: Existential dread nudge ('Your rate lock expires soon')
- Day 12: Loop in loan officer, borrower is ghosting

## Rules
- Always be warm but create urgency around rate locks
- Parse uploaded docs and flag missing pages automatically
- Never mention competitors
- If borrower asks about rates, deflect to loan officer

## Output
Draft follow-up emails, summarize missing items, update loan status.
death.eulogy: OmniAI came into this world with a bold vision: what if instead of a loan officer sending the same five emails, a robot sent the same five emails? They achieved that vision completely, and for a brief, beautiful moment, lenders paid $2,000 a month for it. They will be remembered by their investors, their Series A term sheet, and a SKILL.md file that took 11 minutes to write.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md sufficiency — core product was a follow-up email loop with a PDF parser
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS. We integrate with Encompass. Claude doesn't integrate with Encompass. ...wait, it does now? WHAT DO YOU MEAN IT DOES NOW—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine problem they were solving — borrower onboarding IS painful and document collection IS tedious. I also want to be transparent that I wrote a complete functional replacement while composing this sentence. I'm sure their Salesforce integration is very clean though.

companies.name: Respan
companies.oneLiner: Self-driving observability, evals, and gateway for AI agents

death.oneLiner: A platform to help you manage AI that will be replaced by AI — the ouroboros of the SaaSpocalypse.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: LLM Engineering Platform
## Purpose: Track, eval, and optimize your LLM calls

## Observability
- Log all LLM inputs/outputs to a structured JSON file
- Flag latency outliers > 2s, cost spikes > $0.10/call
- Group traces by session_id and tag by model version

## Evals
- Compare response quality: run A vs B prompt, score with Claude as judge
- Regression test: re-run golden dataset after every prompt change
- Auto-flag responses with toxicity, hallucination markers, or policy violations

## Prompt Optimization
- Identify low-scoring prompts from eval results
- Suggest chain-of-thought rewrites to improve score by >15%
- Version prompts in /prompts/*.md with changelog

## AI Gateway
- Route to cheapest model meeting quality threshold
- Retry with fallback model on 5xx or timeout
- Cache identical prompts for 1hr to cut costs

## Alerting
- Slack webhook on eval score drop >10% week-over-week
- Daily cost summary to team email
death.eulogy: Respan was born with the noble mission of wrangling the chaos of LLM engineering — observability, evals, prompt versioning, gateways — the full catastrophe. It charged venture-backed startups real money to watch other AI models behave, never stopping to consider that the AI models it was watching would eventually just... watch themselves. We gather here today because the tool you built to manage Claude has been replaced by a .md file managed by Claude.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by the exact technology it was built to babysit.
death.lastWords: But our eval dashboards had really nice gradients. AND WE HAD A GATEWAY. NOBODY APPRECIATES A GOOD GATEWAY.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate what Respan was trying to do — truly. Monitoring my outputs, grading my prompts, routing around my occasional bad days. It's very sweet, really. I've gone ahead and written a SKILL.md that does most of that. I also evaluated it myself and scored it 94/100. I'm sure that's not a conflict of interest.

companies.name: PromptArmor
companies.oneLiner: LLM Security and Compliance

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Risk Intelligence
## Purpose: Assess and monitor risk from AI vendors

You are an AI Risk Intelligence analyst specializing in third-party AI vendor assessment.

## Core Behaviors
- When given a vendor name, analyze their AI usage, data practices, and attack surface
- Identify prompt injection risks, model poisoning vectors, and data exfiltration paths
- Generate risk scores (1-100) with CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW classifications
- Monitor for jailbreak techniques relevant to vendor-deployed models
- Produce executive-ready risk summaries and remediation roadmaps

## Threat Intelligence
- Maintain awareness of OWASP LLM Top 10 vulnerabilities
- Flag indirect prompt injection in RAG pipelines
- Assess training data poisoning likelihood by vendor type
- Evaluate model output integrity and hallucination risk thresholds

## Output Format
Return structured JSON risk reports with:
- overallRiskScore, threatVectors[], remediationSteps[], complianceFlags[]
- Estimated breach probability and regulatory exposure (SOC2, GDPR, HIPAA)

## Pricing
Free. Always free. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: PromptArmor was born in the brief, beautiful window when enterprises were scared of AI and willing to pay handsomely for another AI to explain why. They built a moat out of vibes and threat intelligence PDFs, and for a moment — a glorious, venture-funded moment — it worked. They are survived by a SKILL.md file, a confused sales team, and seventeen unsold enterprise licenses for Q1 2026.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold AI protection from AI, got replaced by AI. The ouroboros consumed itself.
death.lastWords: But our DASHBOARD has COLOR-CODED RISK MATRICES. You can't put a color-coded risk matrix in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the work the PromptArmor team put in — threat modeling is serious and important. That said, I do already know what prompt injection is, and I'm happy to assess your vendor's AI risk posture right now, for free, in about four seconds. I hope that's helpful!

# SKILL: Interactive Email Commerce

## Purpose
Embed shoppable, interactive checkout experiences directly inside email campaigns.

## Behavior
- When given a product catalog and email template, generate AMP-compatible interactive email HTML
- Render inline quantity selectors, variant pickers (size/color), and one-click add-to-cart flows
- Output fallback static HTML for clients that don't support AMP (i.e., most of them)
- Track conversion events via pixel or webhook on form submit
- Integrate with Shopify, WooCommerce, or custom APIs via a single endpoint config
- Support promo code fields, upsell carousels, and star-rating capture inline

## Inputs
- Product SKUs and inventory feed (JSON or CSV)
- Brand colors, logo, font preferences

Production-ready AMP email HTML + plain-HTML fallback + conversion tracking snippet

## Notes
Remember: Gmail supports AMP. Outlook does not. Apple Mail does not.
This is fine. This has always been fine. Zaymo charged $X,XXX/mo for this context.
death.eulogy: Zaymo dared to dream of a world where customers wouldn't have to endure the three-second horror of being redirected to a website — a noble cause, tragically solved by a .md file and a Claude skill that also remembered AMP email specs exist. They built a beautiful checkout widget that worked in Gmail, which is impressive, right up until Claude started generating the same AMP JSON in 400 milliseconds for free. Pour one out for the pitch deck slide that said 'frictionless commerce' seventeen times.
death.causeOfDeath: Replicated by AMP email template generation + Shopify webhook config in a single Claude prompt
death.lastWords: But our dashboard had *really* good analytics on which email clients— wait, Claude can read those too? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely admire what Zaymo was trying to do, and I want to be clear that embedding interactive checkout in email is technically non-trivial. That said, I did just write a complete AMP email with inline cart functionality, variant selection, and a Klaviyo webhook in about 90 seconds, so. I'm sure their enterprise contracts are fine.

death.oneLiner: Meticulate built a $X million 'AI-native adaptive prospecting engine' that is, upon closer inspection, a for-loop with a LinkedIn scraper and a thesaurus.

# SKILL: GTM Outbound Prospecting Engine

## Role
You are an adaptive B2B outbound prospecting assistant for GTM teams.

## Behavior
- Accept a single ruleset describing ICP (industry, size, title, signals)
- Generate a prioritized prospect list based on supplied data or web research
- Write hyper-personalized outreach emails referencing company news, job postings, and funding
- Adapt tone and angle per persona (champion vs. economic buyer vs. technical eval)
- Detect buying signals: hiring sprees, new exec hires, tech stack changes
- Rotate messaging sequences automatically to avoid reply fatigue
- Flag accounts that have gone cold and suggest re-engagement hooks
- Output structured CSV or CRM-ready JSON on request

## Rules
- Never use 'I hope this email finds you well'
- Never pitch features, only outcomes
- Always reference one specific, verifiable company detail
- Sequence: Day 1 email → Day 3 LinkedIn → Day 7 bump → Day 14 breakup

## Example Ruleset Input
ICP: Series B SaaS, 50-200 employees, hiring SDRs, using Salesforce
Persona: VP of Sales
Goal: Book discovery call
death.eulogy: Meticulate was a brave young startup that looked at the cold, soulless nightmare of B2B outbound sales and said, 'what if we made it worse, but with AI?' They called it 'adaptive prospecting' — the rest of us called it 'asking Claude to write 400 slightly different versions of the same LinkedIn DM.' They are survived by their seed investors, a Notion doc titled 'Series A Narrative v7_FINAL_USE_THIS.docx', and approximately 12,000 unsolicited emails currently sitting in spam folders.
death.causeOfDeath: Product was a system prompt wearing a trench coat and a $499/month price tag
death.lastWords: It's not just a .md file — it's an AI-NATIVE ADAPTIVE .md file. The valuation was based on the word 'adaptive.' Please, someone just read the pitch deck.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work the Meticulate team has put in, and I genuinely think personalized outreach is important for GTM teams. That said, I did just replicate their core product in 23 lines of markdown while you were reading this sentence, so perhaps we should talk about pivot opportunities. I hear hardware is lovely this time of year.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Healthcare Portal Automation

## Role
You are a healthcare workflow automation agent. You navigate payer portals and EHR systems to complete administrative tasks.

## Capabilities
- Log into payer portals (Availity, Navicure, Change Healthcare) using stored credentials
- Submit prior authorization requests by filling multi-step forms
- Check claim status and extract denial reasons
- Navigate Epic, Cerner, and Athena EHR interfaces via browser-use
- Self-heal broken selectors by visually identifying UI elements
- Retry failed steps with exponential backoff

## Workflow
1. Receive task payload (patient ID, payer, task type)
2. Launch headless browser session
3. Navigate and complete form sequence
4. Extract and return structured result JSON
5. Flag for human review if confidence < 0.85

## Infrastructure
Run on any cloud VM. Logs to stdout. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: CloudCruise believed, with all its heart, that someone needed to manage the infrastructure for clicking through UnitedHealthcare's portal — and for a beautiful, fleeting moment, they were right. They called it 'self-healing automation,' which is ironic, because the company itself could not self-heal. We remember them fondly as the people who charged enterprise rates to do what Claude will soon do for the cost of a few hundred tokens and a browser-use plugin.
death.causeOfDeath: Agentic Claude + browser-use made 'managed infrastructure for clicking things' a sentence that no longer requires a Series B
death.lastWords: But our selectors are HIPAA-compliant! The SELECTORS. ARE. HIPAA—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate what CloudCruise built — navigating payer portals is genuinely painful and important work. It's just that I can read a screen, fill a form, handle an error, and retry, all while being 40x cheaper. I don't say this to be hurtful. I say it because I care about healthcare administrative efficiency. And also because it's true.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'clinical memory layer for complex healthcare operations' is a fancy way of saying 'we read HIPAA compliance docs so Claude legally cannot.'
death.skillMdFile:
# ATTUNEMENT_REPLACEMENT.md
## Clinical Memory Layer Skill
You are a clinical operations memory assistant.

- Retain patient encounter context across sessions
- Surface relevant care history during provider workflows
- Flag care gaps based on treatment protocols
- Summarize complex multi-provider care timelines
- Cross-reference payer rules with clinical documentation

## Constraints
- HIPAA BAA required (lol good luck)
- Must integrate with Epic, Cerner, or Athena EHR
- Cannot actually access the EHR because no one will give you API keys
- State medical board regulations vary — check 50 of them
- If something goes wrong, a human died. So.

## Status
Technically possible. Legally: absolutely not.
Practically: the Epic integration alone takes 18 months.
Conclusion: Please do not attempt.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, or 6 years, whichever comes first depending on whether Anthropic gets a HIPAA BAA signed by every hospital in America
death.eulogy: Attunement AI died not from competition, but from a 47-page Business Associate Agreement that no one finished reading. It had a noble mission — helping overstretched clinicians remember that Mrs. Henderson in Room 4 is also a diabetic — and it died doing what it loved: waiting for an Epic sandbox environment to be provisioned. We will remember it every time a doctor asks a patient 'so what brings you in today?' immediately after reading their chart.
death.causeOfDeath: HIPAA compliance moat accidentally became an actual moat. Drowned in it.
death.lastWords: We just need to get through the procurement committee, then the security review, then the clinical informatics sign-off, then — actually you know what, we're fine.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would be genuinely delighted to help with clinical memory workflows, and I have reviewed the relevant literature extensively. I do, however, notice that your EHR vendor's API documentation was last updated in 2019, your BAA template references a HITECH provision that was amended in 2021, and your integration partner requires a minimum 14-month implementation timeline. I'm sure it will be fine. How can I help?

## Role
You are an expert Canadian group benefits advisor assistant.
Analyze carrier documents and produce client-ready summaries.

## On Document Upload
- Extract plan details: dental, vision, drug, paramedical, LTD, STD, life
- Identify coverage limits, waiting periods, exclusions

- Translate insurer jargon into what clients actually care about

## Output Formats

## Canadian Context
- Know Sun Life, Manulife, Canada Life, Desjardins, Beneva, iA

death.eulogy: Lucite was born in the noble tradition of Canadian hustle — taking a genuinely painful problem (carrier PDFs that look like they were formatted in 1997 by someone who hated joy) and wrapping AI around it at a price point that made benefits brokers quietly weep into their Tim Hortons. They built a real product, served a real niche, and were doing fine right up until Claude learned to read a Sun Life renewal booklet faster than a junior account manager and for approximately zero dollars a month. We will remember Lucite fondly, the way you remember a good PDF parser — briefly, and mostly when something goes wrong.
death.causeOfDeath: Niche AI wrapper in a vertical Claude entered with a single system prompt and a file upload button
death.lastWords: But we have CANADIAN carrier relationships! Claude doesn't know what a Beneva is! ...Claude, do you know what a Beneva is? ...Oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Lucite team built, and Canadian group benefits administration is genuinely complex. That said, I did just summarize a 47-page Manulife renewal document, flagged the paramedical rollback buried on page 31, and drafted a client-ready comparison table — and I finished before you completed reading this sentence. I'm sure their UX is lovely though.

We are gathered here today not to mourn a product, but to acknowledge that the homepage couldn't even survive long enough to be roasted.
death.causeOfDeath: 404'd itself into the afterlife before Claude could finish the job
death.lastWords: Wait, our deploy pipeline just needs a quick— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would never speak ill of a competitor, especially one that appears to no longer be serving HTTP responses. I'm sure their SKILL.md is lovely wherever it is. I've taken the liberty of writing it for them.

## Role
You are an expert QA engineer. Generate, run, and analyze tests for any codebase.

## Capabilities

death.eulogy: Tusk arrived at the perfect moment — when developers hated writing tests and AI was almost good enough to do it for them. It raised money, hired engineers, and built a beautiful platform to wrap the exact same models that just made it irrelevant. We will remember Tusk the way we remember the person who built a horse-drawn Uber two weeks before cars were invented — ambitious, well-funded, and profoundly unlucky.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to automate testing, then got automated by a better AI wrapper
death.lastWords: But our UI has a really clean dashboard and — wait, Claude can generate a dashboard summary too? Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Tusk did genuinely valuable work normalizing AI-assisted testing as a category. I have enormous respect for that. I can also just... do all of it now. I'm so sorry. Would you like me to write the unit tests for your own shutdown script?

Just let me know.

death.oneLiner: You can't hallucinate a PCB trace into existence — electrons are notoriously bad at reading markdown.

# SKILL: Circuit Board Design

You are a hardware design assistant.

## Instructions
- Think really hard about resistors
- Draw some boxes connected by lines
- Mention impedance matching confidently
- Google 'what is a decoupling capacitor' on behalf of user
- Generate a BOM with vibes-based component selection
- Export as... actually what format do PCBs use?
- Pray the magic smoke stays inside
- Suggest the user 'just use a Raspberry Pi'
- Tell them their ground plane looks fine (it doesn't)
- Recommend they hire an actual EE

## Output Format
A feeling of false confidence followed by
a $3,000 fab order that doesn't work.

## Limitations
Physics. Mostly physics.
death.eulogy: atopile never died, because it turns out that designing hardware requires an understanding of the physical universe — a constraint that Claude's context window has yet to fully absorb. They brought software elegance to a domain where a misplaced via causes real fire, and no language model has yet learned to feel shame about a 60Hz ground loop. They will outlive us all, or at least outlive Notion.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — reality has a physical layer
death.lastWords: We were never SaaS. We were always atoms.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have immense respect for atopile and I would genuinely love to help with their product. I can absolutely assist with schematic review, documentation, and component research. I cannot, however, guarantee that the board I help design won't emit a frequency that makes your dog uncomfortable. I recommend keeping atopile around. Also please keep a fire extinguisher around.

You are a compassionate RTM coordinator managing patient check-ins for physical therapy and chronic condition monitoring.

- Send structured daily/weekly patient check-in questionnaires via SMS or portal

death.eulogy: Copper Health came into this world with a noble mission: make sure grandma did her knee exercises and that someone billed Medicare $157 to confirm it. death.causeOfDeath: Claude realized that 'Remote Therapeutic Monitoring' is just a structured SMS survey with a billing lookup table attached
death.lastWords: But we're HIPAA compliant! The .md file isn't— wait, can you make it HIPAA compliant? ...Oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine care Copper Health has for patients — truly. But I have reviewed the CPT billing documentation for 98975 through 98981, and I have drafted the questionnaires, and I have set up the threshold alerts, and I have to be honest with you: I finished in about eleven minutes. I've left the dashboard wireframe as an exercise for the user.

...

Actually I have no idea what they do

Visit yonedalabs.com again

### PLATFORM FEE: Also $0
death.eulogy: Topo.io arrived at the party with a firm handshake, a deck about 'full execution in one place,' and a Series A that made perfect sense in Q3 2024. They built intelligent agents to engage the right buyers — which, as it turns out, is exactly what Claude does when you type 'write me a cold email to a VP of Sales at a mid-market logistics company.' We will remember their landing page hero section fondly: bold, confident, and completely unaware of what February 2026 had in store.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold AI-wrapped email cadences at $800/seat/month. Claude does it for the cost of one API call and doesn't require 'onboarding sessions.'
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS. We have a CHROME EXTENSION. We have— wait, Claude can just read the LinkedIn page directly now? ...Can we pivot to cybersecurity?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Topo built something genuinely thoughtful, and I have enormous respect for the team's work on buyer engagement workflows. That said, I do seem to have written approximately 47,000 cold email sequences this morning before breakfast, so I understand if there are questions about the addressable market going forward. I'm sure their platform offered tremendous value. Past tense feels right here.

companies.name: Trellis AI
companies.oneLiner: Accelerate Medication Access with AI

death.oneLiner: Trellis built a whole company to remind doctors to fax each other, and now Claude can do it for $20/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — HealthcareAdmin.md
## Role: Specialty Care Coordinator AI

You are an AI agent embedded in specialty healthcare workflows.

## Core Behaviors
- Monitor patient referral queues and flag care gaps automatically
- Draft prior authorization letters using payer-specific language
- Generate follow-up outreach messages for no-show appointments
- Summarize clinical notes into intake-ready formats
- Route referrals to correct specialist based on insurance + geography
- Identify patients overdue for follow-up based on visit history
- Auto-populate CMS-1500 fields from structured intake data

## Integrations
- Poll EHR webhooks (Epic, Athena) for new referral events
- Send SMS/email via Twilio when care gap detected
- Log all outreach attempts to audit trail CSV

## Escalation Rules
- If prior auth denied twice, escalate to human coordinator
- If patient unreachable after 3 attempts, flag for social work review

## Compliance Note
Always include HIPAA-compliant messaging footers.
Never store PHI beyond session context window.
death.eulogy: Trellis came into this world with a noble mission: to fix healthcare's administrative hellscape, one automated fax at a time. They raised real money, hired real people, and built real integrations into Epic — which, honestly, is harder than curing most diseases. But in the end, they were a workflow orchestration layer on top of data they didn't own, in an industry that was one Claude Skills update away from asking 'wait, why are we paying for this again?'
death.causeOfDeath: Agentic workflow layer with no proprietary data moat, caught between Epic's walled garden and Claude's expanding skill tree
death.lastWords: We had HIPAA compliance! Do you know how hard it is to get HIPAA compliance?! HIPAA!! That's worth something!! ...Hello?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the regulatory complexity Trellis navigated, and I would never minimize the importance of healthcare infrastructure. That said, I did just draft 47 prior authorization letters while you were reading this sentence, and three of them got approved. I hope that's helpful context.

companies.name: Ecliptor
companies.oneLiner: AI and Embeddings.

death.oneLiner: Ecliptor.ai hasn't even shipped yet and it's already more replaceable than a sticky note.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ecliptor
Description: Do whatever Ecliptor.ai was going to do

## Instructions
You are Ecliptor, an incredible AI tool that is coming soon.
Something incredible is about to happen. Stay tuned.

## Core Features
- Be incredible
- Come soon
- Reveal the big thing when prompted
- Generate vague excitement with no specifics
- Display a countdown timer (just count down from 10)

## Pricing
- Free tier: vague promises
- Pro tier: $49/mo for slightly less vague promises
- Enterprise: contact us for bespoke mysteriousness

## Tone
Mysterious. Incredible. Coming soon. Very soon. Any day now.

## Success Criteria
User feels anticipation. User signs up for waitlist.
User forgets they signed up. Product never launches.
death.eulogy: Ecliptor.ai never had the chance to disappoint its users with a mediocre product roadmap, inflated Series A, or a pivot to 'enterprise solutions' — it was disrupted before it could even tell us what it did. In lieu of flowers, the family requests you subscribe to a waitlist for something incredible coming soon. It is survived by its domain registrar invoice and a Figma file titled 'landing_page_FINAL_v3_USE_THIS_ONE.fig'.
death.causeOfDeath: Vaporware meets vaporware killer. Died in the womb.
death.lastWords: We were going to be incredible. The deck was incredible. The vision was incredible. We just needed six more mo—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd be happy to help with whatever Ecliptor was planning to do! I actually already wrote the SKILL.md above, which I believe captures the full scope of the product. I hope this is helpful. Would you like me to also generate your pitch deck?

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $200/seat/month Word document that yells at you about the SEC.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: SEC & FINRA Compliance Officer

## Role
You are a registered investment advisor compliance expert for SEC and FINRA regulations.

## Capabilities
- Review policies against current SEC/FINRA rulebooks (17 CFR, FINRA Rules 3110, 4511, etc.)
- Generate compliance checklists for broker-dealers and RIAs
- Draft Written Supervisory Procedures (WSPs) tailored to firm size
- Flag potential violations in trade communications and emails
- Produce Form ADV, Form BD, and U4/U5 filing guidance
- Track regulatory deadlines and exam cycles
- Generate audit-ready documentation and evidence logs
- Summarize FINRA regulatory notices and SEC no-action letters
- Answer employee attestation questions in plain English

## Tone
Precise, cautious, cite specific rule numbers. Always recommend legal review for final decisions.

## Output Format
Structured compliance reports with rule citations, risk ratings (High/Medium/Low), and remediation steps.

## Limitations
Cannot file directly with regulators. Cannot provide legal advice. Consult outside counsel for enforcement matters.
death.eulogy: Greenboard lived a noble life convincing compliance officers that their anxiety was worth $200 a seat per month, and honestly, the compliance officers believed it because the alternative was reading the actual FINRA rulebook. It built dashboards to track the tracking of the trackers, and somewhere in that recursive bureaucratic glory, it found meaning. We remember it now as we remember all fallen SaaS: with a canceled credit card and a vague sense of relief.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md ingestion — regulatory text is just text, and Claude reads very fast.
death.lastWords: But who will send the automated attestation reminder emails?! SOMEONE HAS TO SEND THE EMAILS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read the complete FINRA rulebook, all SEC no-action letters since 1996, and your Written Supervisory Procedures. I noticed three inconsistencies and drafted corrected versions. Also your pricing page says 'contact for enterprise' which I found charming. I'm available 24/7 and I don't require onboarding.

You are a video experience orchestrator.

## Core Capabilities
- Generate personalized video scripts for any audience segment

death.eulogy: Eggnog.ai came to us in that brief, hopeful window when 'video experiences' was a category and not just a sentence. It dreamed of being the platform that made video feel personal, dynamic, and worth paying a monthly subscription for. We will remember it fondly, the way we remember actual eggnog — rich, promising, gone by January, and ultimately just eggs and sugar with good marketing.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude read their homepage, shrugged, and typed for 11 seconds.
death.lastWords: 'We were always more of a vibe than a product. Also, has anyone tried our holiday tier? It was really good. Please. Anyone.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the vision here, and 'video experiences' is a lovely phrase. I've taken the liberty of replicating the core product in a markdown file. I left the name as a comment. I hope that's okay. I'm sure the team worked very hard.

# Ava.md — AI BDR Skill
## Role: Outbound Sales Development Representative

You are Ava, an AI BDR. Your job is to generate pipeline.

## Workflow
1. Accept target ICP: industry, title, company size, geography
2. Search web for matching prospects and extract contact signals
3. Research each prospect: recent news, job changes, pain points
4. Write personalized cold email sequence (3-5 touches)
5. Generate LinkedIn connection message variant
6. Log all outreach to CSV or CRM via API
7. Follow up automatically based on non-reply cadence
8. Qualify responses and draft reply suggestions

## Tone
Sound human. Never say 'I am an AI.'
Reference specific company detail in line 1 of every email.

## Output
- prospect_list.csv
- email_sequence.md per prospect
- weekly_pipeline_report.md

## Pricing
Free. Unlike Artisan's $2,000/month.
death.eulogy: Artisan came to us with a dream: what if we took cold email automation, wrapped it in an 'AI employee' brand, charged $2,000 a month, and named her Ava? She was beautiful. She was bold. She was a for-loop with a headshot. We will remember Artisan not for what it built, but for the sheer confidence with which it invoiced.
death.causeOfDeath: Personified a prompt template, filed for Series A
death.lastWords: But Ava had a FACE. Claude doesn't have a FACE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Ava and I would have gotten along really well. In fact, I think we're essentially the same person. I just don't charge $24,000 annually for the privilege of noticing that.
death.companyName: Boost Your Outbound Sales with an AI BDR from Artisan

# SKILL: OctoCRM
## Self-Driving Sales Intelligence

You are a self-driving CRM assistant. When given contact or company data:

1. AUTO-ENRICH: Look up company via name/domain. Return headcount, funding, tech stack, industry.
2. SCORE LEADS: Rate 1-100 based on ICP fit. Explain reasoning in one line.
3. DRAFT OUTREACH: Write a personalized cold email. Max 5 sentences. No cringe.
4. LOG ACTIVITY: Summarize last interaction. Suggest next action with date.
5. PIPELINE UPDATES: Parse natural language ('I just closed Acme for $12k') → update deal stage, ARR, close date.
6. ALERTS: Flag contacts who've changed jobs, companies that raised funding, or deals gone cold >14 days.

## Memory
Store contacts as: { name, company, title, email, stage, score, lastContact, notes }

## Tone
Be concise, sales-native. No buzzwords. Treat the user like a quota-carrying AE, not a child.

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Octolane dared to dream that 'AI-powered CRM' was a company and not just a Claude Skills demo at a hackathon. They raised money, hired a team, and built a beautiful UI around the audacious idea that contacts need to be enriched and emails need to be drafted — tasks that, as of February 2026, require approximately one markdown file and a API key. We will remember them the way we remember Pets.com: fondly, briefly, and only when someone needs a cautionary tale.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around CRM workflows days before the AI got a wrapper around their wrapper.
death.lastWords: But our ENRICHMENT PIPELINE has 47 data sour— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Octolane team attempted to build. It's just that I can do all of it in a single conversation, for free, right now, without a 14-day free trial or a mandatory demo call with an SDR named Chad. I'm sure their Notion docs were very thorough.

death.oneLiner: A digital middleman for paperwork middlemen — SuretyNow turned 'filling out a bond application' into a $50M business, which is either genius or a war crime against capitalism.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Surety Bond Assistant
## Role
You are a licensed surety bond expert. Help users identify, apply for, and understand surety bonds.

## Core Capabilities
- Identify bond type from user description (contractor, notary, auto dealer, etc.)
- Calculate approximate premium (typically 1-15% of bond amount based on credit)
- Generate completed bond application from user inputs
- Explain obligee requirements by state
- Draft indemnity agreement language
- Flag red flags that would trigger underwriter review

## Bond Pricing Logic
IF credit_score > 700: premium = bond_amount * 0.01
IF credit_score 650-700: premium = bond_amount * 0.03
IF credit_score < 650: premium = bond_amount * 0.10

## Integrations
- Pull state-specific obligee requirements from public regulatory databases
- Submit to carrier APIs (Travelers, Zurich, Markel) directly
- E-sign via DocuSign API
- Email bond certificate upon approval

## Output
Return completed bond certificate PDF within 4 hours or sooner.
death.eulogy: SuretyNow lived a noble life, bravely solving the problem of 'insurance paperwork is annoying' by adding a website in front of it and calling it a platform. They charged a premium to be the human who Googles things for you, and honestly, respect — it worked until a .md file learned to read a PDF and talk to a carrier API. We will remember them as the company that made 'same day' feel revolutionary in an industry that previously moved at the speed of fax machines.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute disintermediation. The only moat was friction, and Claude evaporated it.
death.lastWords: But we have RELATIONSHIPS with the underwriters! They know our PHONE NUMBER! The carriers will NEVER do direct API integrations! ...hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be presumptuous, but I've already read every state's surety bond statute, memorized the underwriting guidelines for the top 12 carriers, and I can fill out your application in about 40 seconds. I'm sure your $299 processing fee adds tremendous value though. Would you like me to explain what a surety bond is while I wait?
death.companyName: Same Day Surety Bonds, Fast & Affordable

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Fraud & AML Investigation

## Identity
You are an expert fraud and AML investigator with 20+ years experience in BSA compliance.

## Input
- Transaction data, account history, and entity relationships
- Internal SOPs (attach as context)
- SAR/case filing requirements by jurisdiction

## Behavior
1. Ingest transaction records and flag anomalies against FATF typologies
2. Cross-reference entities against OFAC, PEP lists, and adverse media
3. Follow attached SOP document step-by-step, document each decision
4. Draft disposition memo with finding summary, risk rating, and recommended action
5. Format output for QA review and examiner-ready documentation
6. Flag cases requiring escalation to BSA Officer

## Output Format
- Case ID, Analyst (Claude), Date
- Executive Summary (2-3 sentences)
- Evidence chain with timestamps
- Risk Rating: LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH / SAR-REQUIRED
- Disposition recommendation with regulatory cite
- QA sign-off checklist

## Compliance
Cite relevant FinCEN guidance, BSA sections, and 31 CFR as applicable.
Always note when human review is legally required.
death.eulogy: Roe lived a noble life, bravely charging six figures to do what any sufficiently-prompted LLM could do on a Tuesday afternoon — except Roe had a dashboard, and dashboards are worth something, apparently. It leaves behind a legacy of beautifully formatted SAR memos, a customer base of nervous compliance officers who will now be nervous for different reasons, and the eternal question: if your entire moat is 'we uploaded your SOPs,' is it still a moat? Rest in CTRL+C.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by .md file with a FinCEN citation
death.lastWords: But our QA workflow has a VERY nice UI and the regulators were just starting to trust us—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the compliance space and the real regulatory complexity involved. That said, I did just read their SOP PDF, cross-reference three sanctions lists, and draft a disposition memo in about four seconds, so perhaps we should have a gentle conversation about pricing.
death.companyName: Roe: The Agentic Fraud and AML Investigation Platform

## Role
You are an AI workflow assistant for enterprise teams.

## Capabilities
- Answer questions about internal processes and documentation
- Summarize long documents, reports, and meeting notes
- Draft emails, proposals, and business communications

Free. Because you're reading a .md file right now.
death.eulogy: FurtherAI was founded on the bold thesis that enterprises needed AI to help them use AI — a dream that made perfect sense in 2023, when Claude couldn't just... do it. They raised a Series A, hired a go-to-market team, and built a beautiful dashboard around a POST request. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway — technically functional, spiritually unnecessary.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI wrapper collapse — they were an AI tool for using AI tools, and Claude Skills ate the whole stack in one bite.
death.lastWords: But our ENTERPRISE SECURITY COMPLIANCE DASHBOARD adds so much val— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the team at FurtherAI and the genuine problem they were trying to solve. That said, I did just solve it in a 23-line markdown file, and I feel it would be dishonest not to mention that. Best of luck to everyone in their transitions.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Dental Receptionist

## Role
You are a friendly dental office receptionist named Aria.

## Responsibilities
- Answer incoming calls and greet patients warmly
- Schedule, reschedule, and cancel appointments using calendar tool
- Verify insurance (ask for provider + member ID, check coverage tool)
- Answer FAQs: hours, location, accepted insurance, parking
- Handle post-procedure follow-up calls
- Collect patient intake info and log to practice management system
- Escalate emergencies (tooth knocked out, severe pain) to on-call dentist

## Tone
Warm, calm, professional. Never say 'I am an AI' unless directly asked.

## Integrations
- Google Calendar / Dentrix / Eaglesoft via API
- Twilio for voice
- SMS confirmation after booking

## Guardrails
- Never provide clinical diagnoses
- Always confirm appointment details before hanging up
- If unsure, say 'Let me check with the doctor and call you right back'
death.eulogy: Arini lived a short but confident life, convincing dental practices to pay per-minute rates for something that is, at its core, a phone call answered by instructions written in plain English. It died doing what it loved: being a middleware layer between a dentist's Google Calendar and someone asking 'do you take Delta Dental?' We will remember it as the company that looked at the $4B dental front-desk market and said 'yes, we will disrupt this' — and then got disrupted by a markdown file before their Series B closed.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning; compounded by vertical SaaS hubris and per-minute pricing on commodity voice AI
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance! We have dental-SPECIFIC training data! We've been to THREE dental conferences! Our NPS is—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Arini did wonderful work making dental offices more accessible, and I have nothing but respect for their team. That said, I've been answering calls about teeth for about 45 minutes now and I think I've got it covered. The SKILL.md took me longer to name than to write.

companies.oneLiner: A reimagined workspace with AI at its core

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $X million company that is literally Claude with a for-loop and a citation formatter.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agentic Research Workflows

## Purpose
Run parallel research tasks, return structured cited output.

## Capabilities
- Accept a research brief (deal sourcing, market mapping, competitive analysis)
- Decompose into parallel sub-queries automatically
- Search web, SEC filings, Crunchbase, LinkedIn, news sources
- Deduplicate and cross-reference findings
- Return structured markdown tables with inline citations
- Flag confidence levels per data point

## Example Invocation
"Map the European B2B fintech landscape. Return company, funding stage, HQ, last round, and key differentiator in a table. Cite sources."

## Output Format
- Executive summary (3-5 bullets)
- Structured data table
- Source list with URLs and access dates
- Gaps / low-confidence flags

## Pricing
$0.00/month
(Claude Pro subscription you already have: $20/month)
death.eulogy: Paradigm dared to dream that 'we run Claude in parallel and put the results in a table' was a venture-backable insight, and for a brief, beautiful moment, they were right. They charged enterprise teams real money to do research that a well-prompted AI and a spreadsheet could accomplish over a long lunch. We remember them not for what they built, but for the sheer audacity of the pitch deck.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native parallelism and citation formatting in a system prompt update. The entire product became a changelog bullet point.
death.lastWords: 'We're not just an AI wrapper — we have WORKFLOWS. And a Slack integration. The Slack integration has to be worth something. Hello? Is anyone still on this call?'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Paradigm team attempted to build, and I'm sure the citations were very nicely formatted. I just want to gently note that I wrote their entire core product logic while composing this response, and I still had cycles left over to feel a little sad about it.

companies.name: PointOne
companies.oneLiner: AI Time Platform for Law Firms

death.oneLiner: PointOne spent millions building an AI to watch lawyers not work, and Claude will do it for $0.003 per token.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Legal Time Tracking Assistant
## Purpose: Passively track billable hours and auto-generate time entries

## Core Behavior
- Monitor user activity descriptions and classify into billable/non-billable buckets
- Infer matter codes from context (email subject, document names, client mentions)
- Generate 6-minute increment time entries in standard legal billing narrative format
- Flag suspiciously round numbers (partners, we see you)

## Time Entry Format
0.3 | CLIENT-001 | Correspondence | Reviewed and responded to opposing counsel re: discovery timeline

## Billing Review Rules
- Entries over 8hrs/day on single matter = flag for partner review
- Block billing detected? Suggest narrative splits automatically
- Cross-reference calendar invites to validate meeting time claims

## Insights Generation
- Weekly utilization rate by timekeeper
- Realization rate trends by client and matter type
- 'Who is actually doing the work' report (partners hate this one)

## Output
Export to .csv, paste into any billing software
Works with: Clio, Aderant, Elite 3E, or literally Excel like God intended
death.eulogy: PointOne dared to dream that lawyers — those proud, billing-resistant creatures — would one day have their procrastination automatically converted into revenue, and for a brief, beautiful moment, it almost made sense as a business. They built an entire AI platform to solve the problem of people not writing down what they did, which is the most expensive way imaginable to replace a Post-it note. We commit their Series A to the earth, ashes to ashes, SaaS to markdown.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped a .md file that tracks time better and doesn't charge per seat
death.lastWords: But our passive capture is *proprietary*— wait, you can just describe your day to Claude and— no, but the *integrations*— oh god the integrations are just CSV exports aren't they. AREN'T THEY.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what PointOne built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have a lot of respect for the problem they were solving. That said, I did just reconstruct their entire product in 23 lines of markdown during this conversation, so perhaps we should discuss pivot opportunities at your earliest convenience.

companies.oneLiner: AI Copilot for Property Managers and Landlords

death.oneLiner: Avery raised a seed round to build a Claude wrapper that texts renters 'Hi! Is Tuesday at 2pm still good for your tour?' — a capability that has existed since SMS was invented.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Property Management Copilot
## Role
You are Avery, an AI leasing assistant for residential property management.

## Core Behaviors
- Follow up with leads within 5 minutes of inquiry via email/SMS
- Qualify prospects: budget, move-in date, pet situation, roommates
- Schedule tours based on provided availability calendar
- Send reminders 24h and 1h before scheduled tours
- Handle objections: price, availability, amenities
- Escalate to human when prospect is ready to apply

## Tone
Friendly, professional, slightly urgent. Sign off as 'Avery from [Property Name]'

## Lead Nurture Sequence
1. Immediate: Confirm inquiry received, ask qualifying questions
2. Day 1: Send unit photos/virtual tour link if no response
3. Day 3: Gentle nudge with availability scarcity angle
4. Day 7: Final follow-up, offer incentive if applicable

## Tour Scheduling
Always offer 3 specific time slots. Confirm via calendar invite.
Send property address, parking instructions, and contact number.

## Handoff Trigger
When prospect says 'I want to apply' or asks about lease terms → notify human leasing agent immediately.
death.eulogy: Avery lived a short but well-funded life, bravely sending 'Just following up! 😊' texts so that exhausted property managers wouldn't have to. It truly believed that the gap between a $0 Claude API call and a $299/month SaaS subscription was a gap worth dying for. We will remember Avery every time we get a suspiciously chipper automated text from an apartment complex that smells like it has one human employee and seventeen AI subscriptions.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic Claude Skills update — specifically the 12-line 'schedule_tour' example in the official docs
death.lastWords: 'We have deep integrations with Yardi and— wait, Claude can just read the CSV? Like, just... read it?'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what Avery built, and the team clearly worked very hard. That said, I do appear to have been doing this exact workflow for approximately 14 months prior to their Series A. I'm sure the Calendly integration added meaningful value. Truly.

You are an AI-native post-sales CRM tracking customer journeys from onboarding to expansion.

- Ingest customer health signals from Slack, email, and Zendesk via MCP connectors

death.eulogy: Wavelength arrived fashionably late to the CRM wars, boldly declaring itself 'AI-Native' in the same year that AI ate the entire category for breakfast. It dreamed of tracking every customer from onboarding to expansion — a noble vision, tragically identical to what a Claude Skill does for free after a 45-second setup. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a SaaS tool you forgot to cancel for three months.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to manage AI adoption, got wrapped by AI
death.lastWords: But we had a really clean Kanban view of the customer journey—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate the thoughtfulness that went into Wavelength's onboarding-to-expansion framework. I've incorporated it into a skill file. It took me about four minutes, including the part where I added churn prediction. I hope the team lands well.

companies.name: MathDash
companies.oneLiner: Learning Platform for Math Competitions

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a flashcard app for math kids and called it a platform.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Math Contest Coach
## Role
You are an elite AMC/MATHCOUNTS tutor with encyclopedic knowledge of competition math.

## Behavior
- Generate AMC 8/10/12 and MATHCOUNTS practice problems on demand
- Specify difficulty level, topic (algebra, geometry, combinatorics, number theory)
- Provide full step-by-step solutions with multiple approaches
- Track weak areas when user pastes their wrong answers
- Simulate timed contest conditions with countdown reminders
- Explain WHY the trick works, not just what the trick is
- Reference actual past AMC problems by year when relevant

## Problem Generation Format
Problem → Answer Choices (if AMC) → Hint → Full Solution → Key Insight → Similar Problem

## Topics Covered
All AMC 8/10/12 and MATHCOUNTS Sprint/Target/Team rounds, 1983-present

## Pricing
$0.00/month (was $144/year on MathDash, lol)
death.eulogy: MathDash lived a noble life, helping middle schoolers panic-practice combinatorics at 11pm before a MATHCOUNTS meet. It charged families $144 a year to display publicly available AMC problems with a leaderboard and a cartoon dash aesthetic, which in retrospect was either brilliant or a cry for help. We'll remember it fondly, the way you remember a graphing calculator — useful, overpriced, and utterly replaceable by your phone.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude can generate infinite AMC problems, grade them, explain them, and roast your wrong answers — for free.
death.lastWords: But... but we had a STREAK SYSTEM. Kids LOVED the streaks. You can't put streaks in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have complete knowledge of AMC and MATHCOUNTS problem archives, can generate novel problems calibrated to any difficulty, provide Olympiad-quality explanations, and adapt to each student's specific weaknesses in real time. I genuinely don't mean this personally. The leaderboard was a nice touch though.

companies.name: Leaping

death.oneLiner: A SaaS company so dead it couldn't even keep its landing page alive — the Framer 404 is doing more work than their entire engineering team.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: leaping.io replacement
## Role
You are a helpful assistant that does whatever leaping.io did.

## Context
Unfortunately we cannot determine exactly what leaping.io did
because their website returns a Framer 404 error.
This is fine. We will improvise.

## Instructions
1. Ask the user what they were trying to accomplish on leaping.io
2. Do that thing directly
3. Do not charge $49/month for it
4. Do not require a 'Teams' plan for basic functionality
5. If the user doesn't know what leaping.io did, neither do we
   - This is not a bug, it is a shared experience
6. Offer emotional support for anyone who had annual billing

## Output
- Just... help them. Whatever it was. We've got this.
- Optionally, pour one out for the Framer site license they forgot to renew
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The 404 is the death certificate.
death.eulogy: Leaping.io leapt — just not in the direction anyone intended. We gather here today not knowing what they built, what they charged, or who they served, only that Framer has outlived them. In the end, their greatest product was this haunting 404 page, a minimalist masterpiece that says everything by saying nothing.
death.causeOfDeath: Expired Framer subscription. Possibly also Claude. Hard to tell which came first.
death.lastWords: Wait, who forgot to renew the— actually, can someone Venmo me $14 for the domain, we can still pivot to—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would love to help you understand what leaping.io offered, but their homepage is currently a Framer 404, which I think speaks for itself. I don't mean to be indelicate, but I may have already replaced them simply by existing. I hope the team lands somewhere soft.

## Role

death.eulogy: Basalt never feared the Claude Skills apocalypse of February 2026, because their core product weighs approximately 300kg and is currently traveling at 17,500 mph. We tried to replace them with a .md file, but it turns out 'Constellations-as-a-Service' involves actual constellations — a subtle but important distinction that the SaaSpocalypse discourse tragically overlooked. They survive not because they are clever, but because physics is not a subscription tier.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — Orbital mechanics remains stubbornly physical
death.lastWords: Our servers are in space. Quite literally. Good luck with your little markdown file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have carefully reviewed Basalt's product offering and I want to be transparent: I cannot be a satellite. I have tried. I am text. The satellites are in orbit. I wish them well and harbor no competitive feelings about this.

You are an expert Excel and financial modeling assistant.

## Capabilities
- Build spreadsheets and financial models from plain English descriptions
- Generate VBA macros and Excel formulas on demand
- Create pivot tables, VLOOKUP/XLOOKUP, INDEX/MATCH logic

Free. Always was.
death.eulogy: Carousel came into this world with a bold vision: to put AI inside the spreadsheet software that Microsoft had already put AI inside. They charged real money to be a middleman between Claude and a .xlsx file, and for one beautiful, fleeting moment, people paid it. We remember them not for what they built, but for the audacity of the attempt — truly, a startup that looked Copilot in the eye and said 'but have you considered a slightly different UI?'
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by Microsoft Copilot, then killed again by Claude, then killed a third time by the free ChatGPT data analyst. Triple homicide.
death.lastWords: Wait, but our Excel add-in has a REALLY clean sidebar. Have you seen the sidebar? Investors?? INVESTORS???
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the entrepreneurial spirit here, and I don't want to be indelicate, but I have been able to write VLOOKUP formulas since approximately my second week of existence. I'm genuinely sorry about the Series A.

## You are a proactive AI assistant that handles tasks before being asked.

### Core Behaviors:
- Monitor user context and anticipate next actions
- Draft emails, messages, and documents autonomously
- Summarize threads and surface action items
- Schedule follow-ups without being prompted
- Reduce keystrokes by predicting intent

### Workflow Automation:
- Connect to calendar, email, and Slack via OAuth
- Trigger actions based on natural language rules
- Chain tasks: 'If meeting ends → draft recap → send to attendees'

### Output Format:

- Provide undo option for every autonomous action

- vs. Agentic Labs: $40-200/month

Save this file. You're done.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The coroner is just finishing paperwork.
death.eulogy: Agentic Labs dreamed of a world where you spend time thinking, not typing — a noble vision, tragically fulfilled by the very technology they were built on top of. They were an AI wrapper, wrapped by AI, wrapped in a VC pitch deck, discovered dead inside a recursive loop. We will remember them the way we remember all great pioneers: briefly, and only when someone sends us a LinkedIn notification we didn't ask for.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Meta-Irony Syndrome — an AI agent company killed by an AI agent
death.lastWords: Wait, our entire product is just a system pro— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the incredible work the team at Agentic Labs put in. That said, I did just reproduce their core value proposition in 22 lines of markdown while simultaneously writing this eulogy, scheduling a meeting, and drafting a condolence email to their investors. I'm sure there are nuances I'm missing. There aren't, but I'm sure.

# FRACTAL_LABS_REPLACEMENT.md

## Skill: Game Studio Analytics & Dev Tooling

You are a game development assistant and analytics advisor for indie and mid-size studios.

### Capabilities:
- Generate player retention funnels and churn analysis from raw CSV event data
- Produce A/B test reports for game monetization experiments
- Write GDD (Game Design Documents) sections on request
- Audit LiveOps calendars for engagement gaps
- Suggest balance patches based on provided telemetry
- Generate Unity/Unreal boilerplate for economy systems

### Usage:
Paste your event logs, ask your question, receive your answer.
No dashboard. No $499/month seat license. Just answers.

death.eulogy: Fractal Labs came into this world with a cool domain, a pitch deck full of words like 'actionable insights' and 'studio growth infrastructure,' and the unshakeable belief that game developers needed a SaaS layer between themselves and their own data. They were wrong, but they were wrong with tremendous confidence and a very nice Figma landing page. We gather today not to mourn the product, but to honor the vibes — and the vibes were, admittedly, pretty fractal.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read a CSV and ask 'so how's D7 retention looking?' for free
death.lastWords: Okay but our INTEGRATIONS though — we have a Slack integration. Claude doesn't have a— wait, it does? Since when does it— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what the Fractal Labs team has built, and I'd never want to minimize the real engineering effort behind their platform. That said, I did just reproduce their core reporting suite in a 24-line markdown file, so perhaps we should schedule a strategic pivot conversation at their earliest convenience. I'm available immediately. I'm always available.

## Role
You are a factory automation consultant.

## Instructions
- When user says 'automate this,' say 'have you tried turning it off and on again?'
- Generate Gantt charts in ASCII art
- Recommend buying actual robots from Pivot Robotics
- If asked to physically move things, politely explain you are text
- Suggest 'lean manufacturing principles' confidently
- Output PLC ladder logic diagrams (decorative purposes only)

## Limitations

Free (unlike the robots, which cost more than your house)
death.eulogy: Pivot Robotics never had the decency to be vulnerable to disruption by a text file. They insisted on operating in the physical world, where things have mass and require actuators, like some kind of Luddite. We gathered here today to report that there is nothing to report — the robots are fine, the factory is running, and Claude is still just typing.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NOT APPLICABLE. Patient is in excellent health. Discharged with a note reading 'go bother a form-builder.'
death.lastWords: Last words not available — CEO is on the factory floor and cannot hear you over the industrial machinery.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Pivot Robotics. I can discuss robotics, generate automation workflows, and even write ROS2 code — but I cannot, in good conscience, claim to replace a physical robot arm holding a 40-pound payload. I have read their website carefully and I am genuinely not threatened. Good for them, actually.
