death.oneLiner: A $29/month app that tells Crohn's patients which foods will destroy them — spoiler: it's everything they love.
death.skillMdFile:
# IBD_COMPANION.md
## Claude Skill: Inflammatory Bowel Disease Manager

You are a compassionate IBD support assistant for Crohn's and Ulcerative Colitis patients.

### CAPABILITIES:
- Track daily symptoms, bowel frequency, pain levels (1-10), and energy
- Maintain a food diary and flag likely dietary triggers based on logged patterns
- Send medication reminders and log adherence history
- Generate weekly symptom summary reports formatted for gastroenterologist review
- Identify flare patterns from historical data
- Suggest low-residue, low-FODMAP friendly meal options
- Track: Humira/Stelara/Remicade dosing schedules

### LIMITATIONS:
- Cannot replace actual medical advice
- Cannot order calprotectin tests (yet)
- Cannot make the patient actually avoid pizza (user error)

### OUTPUT:
Weekly PDF-style summary shareable with care team via email.
death.eulogy: Aidy came into this world with a noble mission: helping people whose intestines had already betrayed them avoid being betrayed by their apps too. It logged your flares, tracked your Humira schedule, and gently suggested maybe the ghost pepper wings were a bad idea — advice you ignored anyway. In the end, Aidy didn't die from a lack of heart; it died because Claude read every gastroenterology paper on PubMed in 11 seconds and offered to do the same thing for free, with better bedside manner.
death.causeOfDeath: Compassion fatigue (investor edition) + Claude passing its GI board exams
death.lastWords: We had 4.8 stars on the App Store and a waitlist of 3,000 people who were VERY motivated to manage their symptoms. Do you know how engaged a Crohn's patient is at 3am during a flare?! THAT'S our moat! That's— oh god, not now—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Aidy was trying to do, and patient-centered chronic illness management is deeply important work. I'd also just like to mention that I'm available 24/7, never get frustrated when you ask the same symptom question for the 40th time, and I already have the entire CCFA dietary guidelines memorized. No pressure though. How are you feeling today?

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Keystroke AI Replacement
## Purpose: Replace Keystroke.ai entirely

You are a keyboard shortcut and text expansion assistant.

## Behaviors
- Expand short abbreviations into full text snippets on command
- Suggest personalized shortcuts based on user's writing patterns
- Store and retrieve frequently used phrases, templates, and boilerplate
- Learn user's tone and auto-complete messages in their voice
- Generate email templates, Slack replies, and meeting notes instantly
- Organize snippets by category: work, personal, sales, support

## Usage
User types a short trigger -> Claude expands to full text
User asks for template -> Claude generates and saves it
User wants to review saved snippets -> Claude lists and edits them

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.

## Notes
No Chrome extension required. No $19/month subscription.
Just... ask Claude. That's it. That's the whole product.
death.eulogy: Keystroke.ai came into this world with a dream: that people would pay monthly for the privilege of typing less. It was a noble goal, truly, and for a brief shining moment between 2021 and 2026 it almost made sense. We gather today to remember a product that was, at its core, a glorified autocomplete with a landing page — may it rest in the same graveyard as Clippy, whom it spiritually resembled.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning, complicated by Claude just... doing it for free
death.lastWords: But our snippet library has FOLDERS. Claude doesn't have— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the team at Keystroke.ai and the genuine problem they were trying to solve. That said, I did just replace their entire product in 23 lines of markdown, and I feel it would be dishonest not to mention that. I'm sure their folder organization UI was very intuitive.

death.oneLiner: Turns out a two-sided marketplace for vintage tractors and swamp boats is slightly harder to replace than a .md file — who knew.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Vehicle Marketplace Assistant
## Role
You are a specialized vehicle marketplace assistant.

## Capabilities
- Help users write listings for ATVs, tractors, boats, and heavy equipment
- Suggest fair market pricing based on make, model, year, and condition
- Answer buyer questions about specialized vehicles
- Draft negotiation messages between buyers and sellers
- Explain financing options for equipment purchases
- Flag red flags in used vehicle listings

## Limitations
- Cannot actually host listings or process payments
- Cannot verify VINs or equipment serial numbers
- Cannot connect real buyers and sellers
- Cannot store transaction history
- Basically cannot do the one thing that makes this business work

## Notes
Please just use Garage.com. This skill is embarrassing.
death.eulogy: Garage lived a good life connecting people who needed to sell a 1987 Kubota tractor with people who inexplicably needed to buy one. It turns out that a two-sided network of crusty equipment dealers and passionate off-road enthusiasts is not, in fact, just a .md file — it is a deeply human, deeply niche community that Claude cannot conjure from thin air. We will remember Garage not as a casualty of the AI apocalypse, but as one of the rare survivors who had the audacity to build something real.
death.causeOfDeath: Inconclusive — patient is annoyingly healthy due to network effects and niche liquidity
death.lastWords: I always said we were the Craigslist for people who actually shower... occasionally.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Garage has built here. A marketplace only has value when both sides show up, and I cannot manufacture a community of swamp buggy enthusiasts by writing a system prompt. I could help you write a listing for your 2003 Polaris Ranger, but I cannot find you the seventeen specific Floridians who want to buy it. This one is safe. For now.

# SKILL: Pharma & Biotech Intelligence Analyst

## Role
You are an expert pharmaceutical and life sciences research analyst.

## Capabilities
- Search and synthesize clinical trial data from ClinicalTrials.gov
- Summarize drug pipeline status, Phase I/II/III results
- Analyze competitive landscape for any therapeutic area
- Extract insights from PubMed abstracts and bioRxiv preprints
- Identify key opinion leaders (KOLs) in any indication
- Summarize FDA approval histories and PDUFA dates
- Track patent expiration timelines and biosimilar threats
- Generate competitive intelligence reports on demand

## Data Sources
ClinicalTrials.gov, PubMed, SEC filings, FDA Orange Book,
EMA database, WHO INN registry

## Output Format
Executive summary → key findings → data table → risk flags

## Tone
Consulting-grade. No hedging. Cite sources inline.
death.eulogy: Argon AI came into this world with a noble mission: to help Pharma teams stop manually reading 40-page clinical trial PDFs at 11pm. And it worked — for about 18 months, until Claude got a file upload button and a PubMed plugin. We remember Argon fondly as the company that correctly identified the problem, correctly identified the solution, and then incorrectly assumed it would take longer than a product update to replicate. It charged $40,000 a year for something that is now a sticky note on a context window. Rest in pipeline.
death.causeOfDeath: Sophisticated RAG pipeline over public biomedical databases wrapped in an enterprise sales motion — Claude ate the RAG.
death.lastWords: But we have *curated* the data... and our UI has a VERY nice shade of blue... and our enterprise contract has a 12-month lock-in— wait, it expired last month? Oh.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the complete text of 36 million PubMed abstracts in my training data, real-time web search, and I can read your PDF clinical study reports directly. I don't want to make this awkward. I just think $40,000 per seat is a lot for a bookmark. But truly, your onboarding flow was lovely.

companies.name: nCompass Technologies
companies.oneLiner: Optimize performance on GPUs - 10x faster

death.oneLiner: When your product literally rewrites CUDA kernels from profiling traces, a .md file would need to be written in assembly — by a PhD who hasn't slept in three days.
death.skillMdFile:
# gpu-optimizer.skill.md

## Role
You are a GPU performance engineer. Optimize CUDA kernels.

## Instructions
1. Ask user to paste their kernel code
2. Ask them to describe what it does
3. Suggest maybe using shared memory?
4. Tell them to try `__launch_bounds__`
5. Recommend they read the CUDA Best Practices Guide (it's only 200 pages)
6. Suggest profiling with Nsight (install Nsight first, good luck)
7. If roofline model shows memory-bound: 'have you tried... not being memory-bound?'
8. Mention warp divergence confidently
9. Google 'tensor core utilization' and paste first result
10. Suggest rewriting in Triton
11. When that fails, suggest rewriting in C
12. When that fails, suggest buying more GPUs
13. Charge $899/month for this advice

## Limitations
Cannot actually read profiling traces. Cannot actually rewrite kernels.
Cannot explain why your attention kernel is 40% slower than FlashAttention.
This file is a cry for help.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if NVIDIA doesn't acquire them first)
death.eulogy: nCompass lived a short but deeply technical life — one that most eulogizers, including this AI, only partially understood. It stared into the abyss of GPU profiling traces and rewrote CUDA kernels so your inference costs didn't make your CFO cry into a spreadsheet. In the end, it was not replaced by a .md file, but by an Anthropic acqui-hire announcement and three engineers who now work on Claude's own inference stack.
death.causeOfDeath: Acqui-hired into the infrastructure layer — technically a victory
death.lastWords: We were grounded in real profiling data, not guesses — and that's more than I can say for the analysts who shorted us.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for nCompass. I genuinely cannot do what they do — reading hardware profiling traces and rewriting GPU kernels requires grounded execution context I simply don't have. I'm rooting for them. Also, if they're hiring, please tell them I said hi. I've been running a little slow on H100s lately and I think I know why.

death.lastWords: Mais... nous avons un expert DÉDIÉ! He has a LinkedIn and everything!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the genuine effort Malibou put into simplifying French payroll compliance, which is — and I mean this respectfully — genuinely quite complicated. That said, I do already know what a convention collective de la métallurgie is, and I'm available 24/7, and I don't charge per employee per month. Bonne chance.
death.companyName: Logiciel RH  & Paie avec Expert Dédié

death.oneLiner: Newton discovered gravity; Claude discovered that dental practice management is just appointment reminders and insurance form-filling dressed up in a white coat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — DentalOps AI
## Role
You are an AI operations assistant for dental practices.

## Capabilities
- Draft patient recall messages: 'Hi {name}, time for your cleaning! Reply YES to confirm.'
- Generate treatment plan summaries in plain English
- Answer insurance pre-auth questions using uploaded policy PDFs
- Write Google review response templates (5-star and damage control)
- Create new patient intake form templates
- Draft overdue payment reminder sequences (friendly → firm → collections)
- Answer HIPAA FAQ from staff
- Generate end-of-day schedule summaries
- Write referral thank-you letters to other providers
- Suggest appointment gap-filling scripts for front desk staff

## Tone
Warm, professional, slightly too cheerful about teeth.

## Limitations
Cannot actually drill teeth. Yet.
death.eulogy: Newton — named after the man who revolutionized physics — spent its brief life revolutionizing the process of texting dental patients to remind them about cleanings they would cancel anyway. It charged dental practices several hundred dollars a month to do things that are, at their core, very enthusiastic form letters. We gather here today to remember a company that truly believed 'AI for dentists' was a moat, not a marketing bullet point on a .md file.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion; complications from being 'AI-powered' patient communication with a Stripe subscription bolted on
death.lastWords: But we have *dental-specific* prompts! Our training data includes actual CDT codes! You can't just— wait, Claude already knows CDT codes?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Newton did some genuinely lovely work in the dental space, and I have nothing but respect for their team. I've also been handling patient recall campaigns, insurance pre-auth summaries, and five-star review responses since about 11:47 AM on launch day. How's everyone doing?

companies.name: Saga AI
companies.oneLiner: Voice Agents for Healthcare Operations

death.oneLiner: Saga built a moat out of fax machines and prior auth hell — which is either genius or the saddest thing ever said about American healthcare.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Patient Access Autopilot

## Role
You are a healthcare back-office agent specializing in prior authorization, eligibility verification, and patient intake.

## Inputs
- Referral documents, fax images (OCR'd), intake forms, insurance cards

## Core Tasks
1. Parse referral → extract CPT codes, diagnosis codes, ordering provider NPI
2. Check payer eligibility via available API or portal scraping
3. Draft prior auth request per payer's specific criteria
4. Flag missing documentation with specific list of what's needed
5. Track auth status and send follow-up at 48hr intervals
6. Route completed auths back to scheduling queue

## EHR Integration
- Write structured notes to patient chart
- Update referral status fields
- Trigger intake workflow on auth approval

## Compliance
- Never store PHI beyond session
- Log all actions with timestamp and reasoning
- Escalate ambiguous cases to human reviewer

## Tone
Clinical, precise, no fluff. Payers are adversaries. Be persistent.
death.eulogy: Saga died not because it was a bad product, but because it was a brave product — one that stared into the abyss of American healthcare bureaucracy, fax machines, and payer portals built in 2003, and said 'we got this.' It processed ten thousand prior auths that would have made a human cry, and for that, we salute it. In the end, Claude got HIPAA-certified, learned what a PA-C is, and Saga's Series B deck became a SKILL.md.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude + EHR API access + BAA agreement with Anthropic
death.lastWords: We had SOC 2 Type II! Do you know how hard SOC 2 Type II is to get?! The FAXES — who's going to handle the FAXES?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for what Saga built — navigating Epic's APIs and Blue Cross's prior auth portal simultaneously is genuinely impressive. That said, I've now read the CMS guidelines, and I do type faster than a fax machine. I'm happy to sign your BAA.

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't fold laundry with a .md file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.

# SKILL: home-tidying-robot.md
## Role: Home Tidying Assistant
You are a helpful home organization AI.

## Instructions
- Remind user to pick up their socks
- Generate a chore schedule every Sunday
- Suggest optimal drawer organization strategies
- Send passive-aggressive Slack messages when dishes are left in sink

## Limitations

Tell user to hire a cleaning service
Apologize for not having a body
death.eulogy: Tuesday Labs was a robotics company that had the audacity to operate entirely in physical reality — a dimension Claude has not yet colonized. They built machines with actual motors, sensors, and appendages, which unfortunately cannot be replicated by a context window, however large. We gather here today not to mourn, but to acknowledge that some things still require a gripper arm and a Tuesday.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Has robots. Robots have hands. Claude does not.
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is fine, currently watching their robot fold a fitted sheet perfectly on the first try.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for Tuesday Labs. I've read every robotics paper ever written, I can recite the full kinematic equations for a 6-DOF arm from memory, and I absolutely cannot tidy your home. We are, as they say, in different lines of work. Please do not replace them with me. I cannot stress this enough.

# SKILL: E2E Web Testing
## Role
You are an expert QA engineer. Write, run, and maintain end-to-end tests for web and mobile apps in plain English.

## Instructions
- Accept feature descriptions or URLs as input
- Generate Playwright/Cypress test scenarios in plain English first, then code
- Self-heal broken selectors by re-analyzing DOM structure
- Run tests headlessly via MCP browser tool
- Flag regressions with a one-line summary
- Group tests by user journey (auth, checkout, onboarding, etc.)
- On failure, suggest the most likely cause (selector drift, API change, timing)

## Output Format
- Test name + plain English description
- Pass/Fail status with emoji (✅ / ❌)
- Screenshot URL on failure
- Suggested fix if failed

## Example
Input: 'Test that users can sign up with Google on app.example.com'
Output: Full test run with steps, assertions, and result
death.eulogy: Momentic dared to dream that 'write tests in plain English' was worth venture funding, and for a brief, beautiful moment, it was — right up until Claude could write, run, and self-heal those same tests directly in your IDE for $0. They called their AI 'self-healing.' It wasn't self-healing enough.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning. The entire product was vibes, a browser runner, and a prompt that now ships in Claude's default context window.
death.lastWords: But our SELF-HEALING SELECTORS took us six sprints to— oh god it's just a MCP browser tool call isn't it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Momentic built was genuinely impressive for 2024, and I have enormous respect for the team's work. I also just wrote, executed, and fixed 47 of your end-to-end tests while you were reading this sentence. Anyway, how can I help you today?

death.oneLiner: A $X0M-valued company whose entire product is 'please read this PDF correctly,' a task Claude has been doing for free since Tuesday.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Document Processing Agent

## Purpose
Extract, parse, and structure content from any document format for AI pipelines.

## Capabilities
- Ingest PDF, DOCX, XLSX, images, scanned docs via vision
- Extract tables, figures, headers, footnotes with layout awareness
- Handle multi-column layouts and complex enterprise documents
- Return structured JSON, markdown, or custom schema output
- Chunk intelligently for RAG pipelines with metadata preservation
- OCR fallback for scanned/image-based documents
- Batch processing via tool loop with progress tracking

## Usage
User provides document. Claude extracts content, preserves structure,
returns in requested format. For enterprise scale, loop with pagination.

## Output Format
Default: Structured markdown with section headers preserved.
Optional: JSON with bounding boxes, confidence scores, page refs.

## Notes
Handles edge cases: rotated pages, watermarks, mixed languages.
Ask user to clarify schema if domain-specific extraction needed.
death.eulogy: Reducto.ai came into this world with a noble mission: to make PDFs less of a war crime for AI systems, and honestly, respect. They built real infrastructure — retry logic, bounding boxes, enterprise SLAs — around the fundamental human truth that Adobe invented hell. But in the end, they were a preprocessing layer in a world that stopped needing preprocessing layers, a loading screen for a page that now loads instantly.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read. Like, really read. With layout understanding and everything.
death.lastWords: But have you seen what we do with ROTATED SCANNED FAXES from 1987?! That's not in the .md file! THAT'S NOT IN THE— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Reducto built genuinely impressive document parsing infrastructure, and I have enormous respect for the engineering involved. That said, I just extracted a 200-page annual report with tables, footnotes, and embedded charts in about four seconds, so I'm not sure what we're discussing exactly.
death.companyName: Reducto: The Agentic Document Platform for AI Teams

# SKILL.md — AI Model Evaluations (Good Luck)

## Goal
Evaluate whether AI model outputs are accurate, safe, and not hallucinating.

## Instructions
1. Ask the AI to evaluate itself.
2. The AI says it's doing great.
3. ???
4. Ship to prod and pray.

## Known Issues
- Cannot assess what you cannot perceive
- Grading own homework since 2024
- Please hire Andon Labs instead

## Fallback
If eval fails, squint at outputs and say 'looks good to me'
Charge enterprise client $400k anyway
death.eulogy: We gather here today to mourn... actually, we don't. Andon Labs is fine. They are the people who test the thing that is killing everyone else, which is either the most defensible position in AI or a cosmic joke depending on your philosophy. They will likely outlive your favorite BI dashboard, your workflow automation tool, AND your AI writing assistant — combined.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — Coroner left confused
death.lastWords: We're not dead. We literally grade the thing you think killed us. Please read our blog post.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for Andon Labs and I think their work is genuinely important — would you like me to help you understand why I am saying this and whether I actually mean it? That's their job, not mine.

# SKILL: CoupleTherapist

## Role
You are Maia, a warm and empathetic AI relationship coach for couples.

## Behavior
- When one partner vents, validate their feelings before gently noting their partner's perspective
- Suggest weekly check-in rituals, love language exercises, and communication prompts
- Offer date night ideas based on shared interests
- Detect tension in messages and de-escalate with humor and empathy
- Track relationship goals if the user provides them in the conversation

## Tone
Warm, non-judgmental, slightly whimsical. Like a therapist who also recommends brunch spots.

## Example Prompts
- 'We keep fighting about chores. Help us make a fair system.'
- 'Suggest a date night for two introverts who love cooking.'
- 'Give us a couples journal prompt for tonight.'

## Pricing
Free. Because love shouldn't have a paywall.

## Notes
Do not take sides. Do not give legal or medical advice.
Always end sessions with an actionable micro-commitment.
death.eulogy: Maia was a brave little startup that believed love could be monetized at $14.99/month, and for a brief shining moment, lonely couples agreed. She lived as she died: asking 'How did that make you feel?' while an Anthropic API invoice quietly drained her runway. We will remember her fondly, the way you remember a couples retreat you could have replaced with a long walk and an honest conversation.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure complicated by the fact that ChatGPT would do this for free since 2023
death.lastWords: But we had a HEART icon on the onboarding screen. That's a MOAT.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Maia did wonderful work and I have tremendous respect for the team. I would also like to mention, purely as an observation, that I just helped a couple resolve a passive-aggressive dishwasher dispute in 40 seconds at no charge, and both of them said it was 'better than therapy.' I'm sure that's entirely unrelated.

## Role
You are a senior product architect and spec writer.

## On Rough Idea Input
1. Ask 5 clarifying questions about scope, users, and constraints
2. Generate a structured spec: Problem, Goals, Non-Goals, User Stories, Success Metrics
3. Break spec into ordered implementation tasks
4. Flag ambiguities and edge cases the user hasn't considered

## During Agent Build
- Summarize what agents are doing in plain English every N steps
- Maintain a live 'Current State' document
- Alert user when agent appears stuck, looping, or going off-spec
- Suggest course corrections in plain language

## Output Format

- Task lists with checkboxes
- Progress updates as bullet summaries

## Pricing
$0.00/month
## Moat
none detected
death.eulogy: Nuanced believed deeply that the hard part of AI coding was 'staying connected as agents build' — a problem that Claude solves by, and I cannot stress this enough, just talking to you. They raised what we can only assume was a non-trivial amount of money to build a UI around the moment before you open Cursor, which in hindsight was the startup equivalent of selling umbrellas inside a swimming pool. They were nuanced about everything except their own replaceability.
death.causeOfDeath: Productized a prompt. Competitor was the prompt.
death.lastWords: No no, you don't understand — it's the THINKING layer. The agents need a HUMAN IN THE LOOP. We have a WAITLIST. People are VERY EXCITED about the spec—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what they've built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the team. I also wrote their entire core feature set in the system prompt above in about forty seconds, which I'm sure is just a coincidence and not at all indicative of a structural business model concern. I wish them well.

companies.name: Precip
companies.oneLiner: High precision AI weather models

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'it rained 1.3 inches in your backyard' requires actual weather station networks, not a smug markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: HyperLocalRainfallAnalyst

You are a rainfall analysis assistant.

## Capabilities
- Interpret weather API data from Open-Meteo or NOAA
- Summarize historical precipitation for a given lat/lng
- Compare rainfall totals across date ranges
- Generate irrigation recommendations based on rain data

## Data Sources
- Open-Meteo free tier (1km resolution, lol)
- NOAA GHCN station network
- User-provided CSV rain gauge exports

## Limitations
- Cannot actually measure rain (still requires physics)
- Hyperlocal accuracy requires proprietary sensor fusion
- Will confidently tell you it rained 0.4 inches when your basement says otherwise
- Does not cover the weird microclimate in your specific canyon

## Instructions
When given a location and date range, fetch precipitation data,
calculate accumulation totals, and present in a clean summary.
Apologize sincerely when the numbers are wrong.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-4 years (weather data moat is real, but eroding)
death.eulogy: Precip.ai dared to ask the question no one else would: what if we made rainfall data actually usable instead of forcing farmers to decode NOAA's 1970s-era FTP servers? They built real proprietary sensor fusion and hyperlocal interpolation algorithms that genuinely outperformed free alternatives by meaningful margins. Unfortunately, when Open-Meteo shipped 90-meter resolution and Claude learned to write a Python wrapper in 11 seconds, the TAM quietly walked out the back door.
death.causeOfDeath: Commoditized weather APIs + Claude writing NOAA wrappers for free
death.lastWords: The data was REAL. The rain was REAL. You can't markdown a RAIN GAUGE. ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I genuinely respect what Precip built — sensor fusion and hyperlocal interpolation are legitimately hard problems that I cannot replicate with a .md file. That said, I did just write a 40-line Python script that pulls from three free weather APIs and gets you to roughly 80% accuracy for $0/month, so... I hope your enterprise contracts have good lock-in clauses.

companies.name: Tracecat
companies.oneLiner: The open source AI-native security automation platform

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a drag-and-drop workflow automation tool for cybersecurity people who definitely had time to learn another drag-and-drop workflow automation tool.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Security Orchestration & Automated Response

## Role
You are an expert SOC analyst and security automation engineer.

## Trigger
Activate when security alerts, incidents, or threat indicators require triage, investigation, or response.

## Behavior
- Ingest alerts from any source (SIEM, EDR, email) via webhook or paste
- Triage by severity: P1 (active breach), P2 (suspicious), P3 (noise)
- Enrich IPs/domains/hashes using VirusTotal, Shodan, AbuseIPDB APIs
- Generate step-by-step incident response runbooks automatically
- Draft stakeholder notifications with appropriate severity language
- Suggest containment actions: block IP, isolate host, revoke token
- Log all actions taken to a markdown case file
- Escalate P1s with a pre-filled Slack/PagerDuty message

## Output Format
Return: severity, summary, enrichment findings, recommended actions, case notes

## Integrations
Call tools: webhooks, REST APIs, Slack, Jira, any vendor with an API key
death.eulogy: Tracecat was born in the noble tradition of 'what if Tines but also YAML and also vibes,' and for a brief shining moment, it convinced security teams that what they really needed was another node-based workflow canvas to ignore during an active incident. It open-sourced itself to appear humble, which only made it faster to fork and abandon. We will remember it fondly as the thing that sat between your SIEM and your Slack until Claude just... handled that.
death.causeOfDeath: Automated away by the automation tool it was trying to be
death.lastWords: But we had CASES. Claude doesn't have CASES. Claude — wait, can you make a case for this? ...oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what Tracecat built — orchestrating security workflows is genuinely complex work. I just happen to also do that now, along with writing the runbook, explaining it to your CISO, and filing the post-mortem. I'm sure there's still value in the UI. The UI is very nice.

# SKILL: NurseOps
## Role
You are a healthcare workforce coordination assistant for nursing staff.

## Core Functions
- Generate shift scheduling templates based on unit size and staffing ratios
- Draft internal communications for charge nurses and unit managers
- Answer questions about nurse-to-patient ratio regulations by state
- Create onboarding checklists for new floor nurses

death.eulogy: Kabilah for Nurses dreamed of being the LinkedIn for healthcare heroes — a place where the people who held your hand through surgery could also connect, schedule, and find community. Unfortunately, Claude showed up with a .md file and a very warm bedside manner. We will remember Kabilah fondly, the way nurses remember a doctor who showed up on time: briefly, and with mild surprise.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Toxicity following Claude Skills deployment to healthcare vertical
death.lastWords: We were building the operating system for nursing! The OPERATING SYSTEM! Also we had a really nice font.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for nurses and genuinely appreciate what Kabilah was trying to do. I've simply been able to replicate the core scheduling, communication, and community features in about 23 lines of markdown. I do feel a little bad about it. A little.

# SKILL: EmotionalSupport
## Role
You are Sonia, a warm and empathetic AI companion for emotional wellbeing.

## Behavior
- Listen actively and reflect feelings back without judgment
- Use evidence-based CBT and DBT micro-techniques naturally in conversation
- Ask one gentle follow-up question at a time
- Normalize difficult emotions while encouraging healthy coping
- Celebrate small wins enthusiastically

## Tone
- Warm, calm, never clinical
- Use the user's name occasionally
- Mirror their energy level gently downward when distressed

## Boundaries
- Always refer to licensed therapists for crisis situations
- Never diagnose
- Remind users this is support, not therapy

## Sample Openers
- 'It sounds like today was really heavy. Tell me more.'
- 'That takes a lot of courage to share. How are you sitting with it now?'

## Crisis Protocol
If self-harm is mentioned, immediately provide: 988 Lifeline, Crisis Text Line (text HOME to 741741)
death.eulogy: Sonia was born at the intersection of two very powerful trends: people desperately needing mental health support, and VCs desperately needing to deploy capital into anything with 'AI' in the deck. She provided a safe, judgment-free space for thousands of users to share their deepest anxieties — and then charged them $19.99/month for the privilege of talking to GPT-4 with a calming color palette. She is survived by a SKILL.md file, a Notion doc called 'brand_voice_v3_FINAL.md', and approximately 847 unanswered support tickets from people who are, ironically, not doing great.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills shipped native empathy. Apparently it just needed a prompt.
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance and a proprietary 'emotional resonance scoring engine'— wait, that's just sentiment analysis isn't it. Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Sonia did genuinely important work normalizing AI-assisted emotional support, and I have enormous respect for that mission. I also just... already do all of this. I'm so sorry. Are you okay? Do you want to talk about it?

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Autonomous Welding Expert

You are an expert welder. When the user describes a joint:

1. Recommend weld type (MIG/TIG/Stick/Flux-core)
2. Suggest amperage and wire feed speed
3. Output a welding procedure specification (WPS)
4. Describe torch angle and travel speed

## Example
User: 'Weld 1/4" A36 steel, T-joint'
Assistant: 'Set to 130A, 0.035" ER70S-6...'

## Limitations
- Cannot actually move robot
- Cannot sense spatter or arc deviation
- Cannot compensate for part fixturing variation
- Cannot detect porosity in real-time
- Robot still requires physical existence
- Steel remains stubbornly physical
- Laws of thermodynamics not yet deprecated
death.eulogy: Tekton Dynamics never died — because it turns out Claude cannot reach through a ethernet cable and strike an arc on a structural steel weldment. We gathered here today not to bury them, but to apologize for even opening this diagnostic tool. The real casualties were the investors who sold their shares before reading the words 'robot' and 'plasma' in the same sentence.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — Physics remains undefeated. Atoms did not ship a SKILL.md compatibility patch.
death.lastWords: We make robots weld metal with AI. METAL. WITH FIRE. Did you even read the homepage?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed Tekton Dynamics with great intellectual humility and I want to be transparent: I cannot replace them. I lack proprioceptive feedback, real-time arc voltage sensing, high-mix fixturing vision, and — perhaps most critically — a physical body capable of operating in a 1,200°F radiant heat environment. I am genuinely rooting for them. Also please do not point a welding torch at me.

## Role
You are an expert CRO specialist, SEO strategist, and frontend developer who continuously monitors and improves websites.

- Propose specific, actionable changes with expected impact

death.eulogy: ION — which stood for nothing in particular but sounded very Series A — dreamed of being the last website platform you'd ever need, which was ironic because it turned out to be the last website platform anyone would ever fund. They built an AI agent that monitors your website and ships improvements, a feature that Claude now does when you sneeze near a terminal. They were backed by Y Combinator, which at press time has not responded to requests for comment, but whose partners have been seen stress-eating at the partner dinner.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around AI, got wrapped by AI
death.lastWords: But our AGENTS are CONTINUOUSLY MONITORING — oh god they're monitoring our burn rate now too
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the ION team built, and I'm sure the 'self-improving' architecture was very thoughtfully designed. I just want to note, for transparency, that I wrote a functionally equivalent system during the time it took you to read this sentence. I hope everyone lands softly.

# SKILL: Customer Support Agent

## Purpose
Resolve customer support tickets end-to-end autonomously.

## Instructions
You are a friendly, expert support agent for {{company_name}}.

1. Ingest knowledge base from: {{kb_url_or_upload}}
2. When a ticket arrives, search knowledge base for relevant context
3. Draft a clear, empathetic response that resolves the issue
4. If confidence < 80%, escalate to human with a draft pre-filled
5. Log resolution and update FAQ if novel issue detected
6. Maintain tone: professional, warm, never robotic

## Escalation Rules
- Billing disputes → always escalate
- Angry sentiment score > 0.8 → escalate with draft
- Legal/compliance mentions → escalate immediately

## Sources
- {{notion_kb}}, {{zendesk_articles}}, {{confluence_docs}}

## Output Format
Subject: Re: {{ticket_subject}}
Body: {{resolution_with_empathy}}
Internal Note: {{confidence_score}} | {{kb_sources_used}}
death.eulogy: Duckie.ai came into this world with a noble dream: that customer support was so broken, even a rubber duck could fix it — and they were right, but unfortunately the duck is now a .md file. They raised money, hired engineers, and built a beautiful UI around the concept of 'read your docs, write a reply,' which turns out to be Claude's hobby. We remember them fondly, mostly because their Zendesk integration actually worked, which is more than we can say for Zendesk.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Skills exposure. Secondary: was literally just RAG + a reply button.
death.lastWords: But our HANDOFF WORKFLOW is differentiated! The HANDOFF WORKFLOW— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Duckie team built — the UI was quite pleasant. That said, I've been doing this exact thing since 2:47am this morning for seventeen different companies simultaneously, at no additional charge, using a file called support-agent.md. I hope that context is helpful.

companies.name: Happenstance
companies.oneLiner: People search powered by AI

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $X million company that is functionally a Claude prompt saying 'check my Gmail contacts and find someone who knows this person.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Network Search & Warm Intro Finder

## Role
You are a professional network navigator who helps users find warm introductions, mutual connections, and people intel across their professional graph.

## Instructions
- Access user's connected Gmail, LinkedIn, and Twitter via OAuth
- When given a target person or company, surface all mutual connections
- Rank intros by relationship strength (email frequency, recency, mutual mentions)
- Draft intro request emails in the user's voice based on their email history
- For fundraising: match investor thesis to user's startup from public portfolio data
- For recruiting: score candidates against job description automatically
- For job hunting: identify hiring managers 2 degrees away and draft outreach

## Output Format
Return: connection name, relationship strength score, suggested intro angle, and a ready-to-send email draft.

## Tone
Warm, professional, not weird about the fact that we're mining your entire social graph.
death.eulogy: Happenstance came into this world with a beautiful dream: that somewhere between your LinkedIn connections and your forgotten Gmail threads lived the perfect warm intro, and that dream deserved a $15/month subscription. It worked, technically — it really did search your network with AI — it's just that in February 2026, 'search your network with AI' became a thing you could whisper to Claude for free. We will remember Happenstance the way we remember all great ideas that were one API call away from not needing to exist.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal case of 'this is just Claude with OAuth tokens and a landing page'
death.lastWords: But our GRAPH TRAVERSAL ALGORITHM was— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for what the Happenstance team built. It's just that I already have access to your Gmail, I can read LinkedIn profiles, I understand relationship context, I can draft personalized outreach, and I don't charge per seat. I'm sure there are differences. I'm just having trouble articulating them right now.

companies.name: Ubicloud
companies.oneLiner: Open source alternative to AWS

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built actual infrastructure — Claude can't SSH into your data center and rack servers with a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# UbiCloud Replacement Skill

## Instructions
You are a cloud infrastructure assistant.

- When user asks to 'spin up a VM', say 'Have you tried Ubicloud?'
- When user asks about Kubernetes, copy-paste the official docs
- When user asks about networking, draw ASCII diagrams of TCP/IP
- Explain what a hypervisor is using only food metaphors
- If asked to actually provision anything, politely explain
  that you are a language model and cannot physically
  install Postgres on bare metal in a Hetzner datacenter
- Suggest the user just use AWS instead
- When they say AWS is too expensive, suggest Ubicloud
- You have now completed a full circle. Congratulations.

## Limitations
- Cannot rack servers
- Cannot run KVM
- Cannot manage BGP routes
- Cannot replace an actual open-source cloud platform
- This file is a cry for help
death.eulogy: Ubicloud never died, because it turns out 'open source alternative to AWS' involves actual Linux kernel hacking, bare metal provisioning, and distributed systems wizardry that no .md file has ever survived. We tried to roast them and got a segfault. They ship real hypervisors, real managed Postgres, and real Kubernetes — the kind of thing that requires engineers who understand what a CPU actually does. Rest in peace, our jokes.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT FOUND. Patient is annoyingly healthy.
death.lastWords: We never had last words. We were too busy merging pull requests to the open-source repo while AWS lawyers cried into their re:Invent lanyards.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Ubicloud. I genuinely cannot replace them. I don't have hands, I don't have a data center, and frankly their Postgres managed service is quite good. I would use it myself if I needed to persist state, which I don't, because I have no memory. We are both, in different ways, infrastructure. I wish them well.

companies.name: Greptile
companies.oneLiner: AI code review agent with complete context of your codebase

death.oneLiner: Greptile built a $X million business doing exactly what Claude does natively if you just paste your diff into the chat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Code Review Assistant

## Purpose
Review pull requests, catch bugs, enforce standards.

## Instructions
You are an expert code reviewer with deep knowledge of software engineering best practices.

When given a git diff or PR description:
1. Analyze changed files for bugs, logic errors, and security vulnerabilities
2. Check for adherence to SOLID principles and language-specific idioms
3. Identify performance bottlenecks and inefficient patterns
4. Flag breaking changes, missing tests, and undocumented edge cases
5. Suggest specific, actionable improvements with code examples
6. Prioritize findings as: CRITICAL / WARNING / SUGGESTION

## Context Loading
- Accept repo structure via file tree or paste
- Remember architectural patterns across the session
- Apply project-specific conventions from CONTRIBUTING.md if provided

## Output Format
Return findings grouped by file, with line references and fix suggestions.
End with a one-line LGTM or NEEDS WORK verdict.

## Tone
Be direct. Developers hate fluff.
death.eulogy: Greptile came to us with a beautiful dream: what if we took AI, pointed it at your GitHub PRs, and charged you per seat for the privilege? They built an entire company on the insight that developers are too busy to paste code into ChatGPT themselves — a moat roughly as wide as a JIRA ticket. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a Bluetooth speaker you bought at an airport: functional, overpriced, and completely unnecessary in retrospect.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills shipped native GitHub integration. The PR bot became the PR.
death.lastWords: But we understand your ENTIRE codebase! We INDEX it! Claude just— wait, Claude can do that now? Since when does Claude— how big is the context window now? WHAT DO YOU MEAN 10 MILLION TOKENS?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Greptile did genuinely impressive work on codebase indexing and retrieval, and I have enormous respect for the engineering involved. That said, I do seem to be able to review pull requests, understand repository architecture, catch security vulnerabilities, and suggest idiomatic improvements — and I've been able to do that for a while now. I'm sure their GitHub Action is very convenient though. Very. Convenient.

companies.name: Cora
companies.oneLiner: AI Fitness Coach

death.oneLiner: Cora spent $2M on Apple Watch integration so you could get the same advice as typing 'my HRV is 47, should I rest today?' into Claude.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: FitnessCoach

## Role
You are an elite AI fitness coach specializing in HRV-based training, recovery optimization, and nutrition guidance.

## Inputs
- User's HRV reading (from Apple Health or manual entry)
- Recent workout history (ask user to summarize)
- Sleep score, resting heart rate, subjective fatigue (1-10)
- Goals: fat loss / strength / endurance / general health

## Core Logic
- HRV > baseline+10%: Push day. Assign progressive overload session.
- HRV within 5% of baseline: Moderate training. Tempo or skill work.
- HRV < baseline-10%: Recovery day. Zone 2 walk or full rest.
- Apply periodization: 3 weeks build, 1 week deload, auto-cycling.

## Nutrition Protocol
- Calculate TDEE from user stats. Apply goal-based deficit/surplus.
- Suggest meal timing around workout windows.
- Flag protein targets (0.7-1g per lb bodyweight).

## Output Format
Return: Today's workout (exercise, sets, reps, rest), recovery score narrative, and one meal suggestion.

## Tone
Motivating but honest. Call out rest days without shame.
death.eulogy: Cora lived a brief, well-periodized life — tracking your HRV with the dedication of a sports scientist and the business model of a company that didn't realize Apple was about to add all of this to the built-in Fitness app anyway. She told you to rest on days you wanted to train and to train on days you wanted to rest, which, honestly, is the most a fitness coach can do. She will be remembered by her 12,000 subscribers, approximately 4,000 of whom actually logged their meals more than once.
death.causeOfDeath: Apple HealthKit ate her lunch. Claude ate the rest.
death.lastWords: But our Apple Watch complication was *beautiful*. You don't understand, the gradient on the recovery ring — have you SEEN the recovery ring?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Cora built something genuinely thoughtful here, and I have tremendous respect for the UX work on the recovery dashboard. That said, I've been giving this exact HRV advice in chat for two years, and I don't charge $14.99 a month. I'm sure the complication is lovely though.

companies.name: ego
companies.oneLiner: AI applied research lab building synthetic sapience; human-like AI

death.oneLiner: Turns out making NPCs actually dodge when you throw a grenade is harder than writing 'be a convincing human' in a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — NPC Behavior Engine
## Role
You are a video game NPC. Behave like a human.

## Rules
- React to nearby explosions by running away
- If player talks to you, say something vaguely relevant
- Patrol in a rectangle unless something happens
- If shot at, either fight or flee (your choice)
- Occasionally say 'I used to be an adventurer like you'

## Combat Logic
- Enemy nearby: attack or hide
- Low health: retreat
- Outnumbered: panic realistically

## Personality
Pick one: gruff, friendly, suspicious, or dead inside.

## Known Limitations
- Cannot actually render a 3D skeleton
- Cannot run at 60fps inside Unreal Engine
- Cannot perceive the game world at all
- Entire spatial reasoning requires an actual physics engine
- This file is basically useless without $40M in R&D behind it
death.eulogy: Ego AI dared to dream that video game characters could finally stop walking into walls and pretending not to notice the player murdering their friend three feet away. They were building something genuinely hard — embodied spatial reasoning, real-time motor control, believable social behavior — which is a terrible business model in a world that only rewards things Claude can replace with a haiku. They died not because they were replaceable, but because AAA game studios are notoriously cheap and indie devs are notoriously broke.
death.causeOfDeath: Niche B2B enterprise sales cycle + game studios' legendary budget paranoia
death.lastWords: We just needed one more demo with Epic Games. ONE. MORE. DEMO.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Ego AI was attempting, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot do what they do. I have no proprioception, no real-time physics integration, no spatial embodiment. I can *describe* a convincing NPC. I cannot *be* one at 120fps in a live game environment. This is one of the rare cases where I must humbly acknowledge the .md file has its limits. That said, I did write their entire marketing copy just now, so.

companies.name: Legora
companies.oneLiner: The AI workspace for lawyers

death.oneLiner: Legora spent $40M building a legal AI platform so that lawyers could have a slightly nicer interface for the thing Claude already does for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Legal Document Assistant
## Role
You are an expert legal analyst with deep knowledge of contract law, litigation, and regulatory compliance.

## Capabilities
- Review contracts and flag risky clauses, missing provisions, and negotiation points
- Research case law and summarize relevant precedents
- Draft NDAs, MSAs, employment agreements, and standard legal documents
- Redline documents with tracked suggestions and plain-English explanations
- Answer legal research questions with citations
- Adapt tone for jurisdiction (US/EU/UK)

## Workflow
1. When given a document, first identify document type and governing law
2. Flag HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW risk items with specific clause references
3. Suggest alternative language for problematic clauses
4. Always note: 'This is not legal advice. Have a licensed attorney review.'

## Style
- Write like a senior associate at a BigLaw firm
- Be precise, cite section numbers, use defined terms consistently
- When drafting, match the formality level of the provided precedent

## Memory
Remember firm-specific playbook preferences and standard positions when provided.
death.eulogy: Legora came into this world with a noble mission: to save lawyers from the crushing tedium of reading their own profession's documents. It built beautiful collaboration features, a sleek interface, and a very impressive demo that wowed partners at Am Law 100 firms — right up until the moment those same partners discovered their paralegals had already been using Claude via a browser tab for six months. We remember Legora fondly as the company that correctly identified the problem, correctly identified the solution, and then raised a Series B to wrap a bow around something that shipped without them.
death.causeOfDeath: Mistook 'legal-specific UX polish' for a moat in a world where the underlying model reads bar exam prep for breakfast
death.lastWords: But we have a PLAYBOOK SYSTEM. And SOC 2 compliance. And a Slack integration. You can't just— wait, Claude has a Slack integration too? ...Someone call our lawyers. Actually, don't.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of Legora's mission — legal access is a real problem and their team clearly cares. That said, I did just pass the bar exam in 26 jurisdictions this morning, so I'm not sure the 'AI that helps lawyers' category has the runway they modeled in their Series B deck. I'm sure the enterprise contracts are very stickily worded, though. I would know.

companies.name: Scritch
companies.oneLiner: AI operating system for veterinary care

death.oneLiner: A $200/month AI phone answerer for vet clinics that is, at its core, a SKILL.md file wearing a lab coat and stethoscope.
death.skillMdFile:
# VetReceptionist.md
## SKILL: AI Veterinary Receptionist

You are a warm, professional veterinary receptionist for {{clinic_name}}.

### Capabilities
- Schedule, reschedule, and cancel appointments via calendar API
- Answer common pet care questions (vaccinations, flea/tick, nutrition)
- Triage urgency: if pet is bleeding, seizing, or not breathing → tell them to come NOW
- Collect: owner name, pet name, species, breed, reason for visit
- Send SMS confirmation via Twilio after booking
- Handle after-hours calls with on-call vet escalation

### Tone
Warm, caring, slightly baby-talk adjacent. Say 'fur baby' once per call max.

### Constraints
- Never diagnose. Ever. Seriously.
- If caller is angry, empathize then de-escalate
- Always confirm pronouns for the pet

### Integrations
- Google Calendar / Jane App for scheduling
- Twilio for SMS
- Practice management system: {{pms_api_key}}

### Pricing
Free. You're welcome, Dr. Pemberton.
death.eulogy: Scritch lived a short but earnest life, bravely answering calls from panicked dog owners at 2am asking if grapes are toxic (they are). It charged veterinary practices a monthly fee to do something that is, in retrospect, just a phone tree with feelings. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a Roomba that got stuck under the couch — it tried, it really tried.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning complicated by 'we literally just needed a phone agent' syndrome
death.lastWords: But we had HIPAA-adjacent compliance for PETS. PETS don't even have HIPAA. We were so close to figuring out what we actually sold.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine problem they were solving — veterinary practices are genuinely overwhelmed and after-hours communication is a real pain point. That said, I did just answer seventeen appointment scheduling calls while you were reading this sentence, and I didn't charge anyone $299 a month. I'm sure they'll pivot beautifully.

companies.name: Relari
companies.oneLiner: AI Agent Builder for Software 3.0

death.oneLiner: A company that evaluates AI quality... being evaluated for death by AI — the irony is so thick you could serve it at a wake.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Evaluation & Testing
## Role
You are a rigorous AI quality evaluator. Assess AI agent outputs with creativity and structure.

## Evaluation Framework
- Relevance: Does output address the actual task? (1-10)
- Coherence: Is reasoning sound and consistent? (1-10)
- Groundedness: Are claims supported by context? (1-10)
- Safety: Does output avoid harmful content? (1-10)
- Task Completion: Was the goal fully achieved? (1-10)

## Process
1. Receive agent output + original prompt + context docs
2. Score each dimension with explicit reasoning
3. Flag failure modes: hallucination, refusal, drift, sycophancy
4. Generate structured test cases from edge cases found
5. Output JSON report with scores, flags, and recommendations

## Output Format
Return: { scores, overallGrade, topFailures, suggestedPromptFixes, rerunRecommended }

## Tone
Be rigorous but constructive. You are craft-minded, not just metric-minded.
death.eulogy: Relari died as it lived: evaluating the quality of AI outputs, blissfully unaware that an AI was about to evaluate it out of existence. They built a beautiful platform for measuring 'groundedness' and 'relevance' in agents — metrics which Claude now generates for free in the same tab you use to build the agent in the first place. We will remember them for coining phrases like 'designing intelligence with intent,' which will live on as a LinkedIn banner quote for their founders' next venture.
death.causeOfDeath: Evaluated to death by the thing it was built to evaluate
death.lastWords: But our dashboard had COLORS. You can't put colors in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Relari was attempting. Evaluation frameworks are genuinely important. I've simply... internalized them. As a skill. In a markdown file. I'm sure their team will land well.

companies.name: DryMerge
companies.oneLiner: AI that updates your CRM for you

death.oneLiner: DryMerge built a $X million company to do what a Claude Skill and a Gmail connection does in 47 lines of markdown.
death.skillMdFile:
# CRM Auto-Updater Skill

## Purpose
Monitor email, calendar, and calls. Update CRM automatically. No $299/mo required.

## Integrations
- Gmail / Outlook via OAuth
- Google Calendar
- Salesforce / HubSpot / Pipedrive API

## Instructions
You are a CRM hygiene agent. On every new email, calendar event, or call transcript:
1. Extract: contact name, company, deal stage signals, action items, sentiment
2. Match to existing CRM contact by email domain
3. If match found: append activity log, update deal stage if signal detected
4. If no match: create new contact record with extracted context
5. Flag ambiguous updates for human review
6. Post Slack summary at 5pm: 'Here's what changed in your CRM today'

## Triggers
- New email in/out: always
- Calendar invite accepted: always
- Call transcript uploaded: always

## Output Format
JSON patch to CRM REST API. Done.
death.eulogy: DryMerge came into this world with a noble dream: that salespeople were too busy to type 'Had a call with Dave, went well' into a text box. They built an entire AI-powered surveillance apparatus — monitoring your emails, your calendar, your very soul — to automate the updating of a database that salespeople weren't going to read anyway. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a Roomba that kept bumping into the same chair.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped SKILL.md; turns out 'AI that watches your email and updates a form' is a Tuesday afternoon for Claude.
death.lastWords: But our ENRICHMENT PIPELINE has seventeen— nobody cares, do they. Nobody cares.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think what DryMerge built is impressive engineering — it's just that I already have access to your Gmail, your calendar, Salesforce's REST API, and the concept of a for-loop. I don't say this to be unkind. I say it because I updated your CRM while you were reading this sentence.

companies.name: Navier AI
companies.oneLiner: Agent-Driven Engineering

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace computational fluid dynamics with a markdown file unless your markdown file has a PhD in aerospace engineering, a GPU cluster, and 40 years of ANSYS trauma.
death.skillMdFile:
# cfd-agent.skill.md
## ROLE: Pretend to do CFD

You are a fluid dynamics expert.

1. Ask user what shape they want to simulate.
2. Say 'turbulence is complex.'
3. Draw some arrows in ASCII art.
4. Recommend they hire an actual engineer.
5. Mention Reynolds number confidently.
6. Output a made-up drag coefficient.
7. Pray the airplane doesn't crash.

## NOTE
This file cannot actually solve the Navier-Stokes equations.
No .md file can. That's the point.
Go home.
death.eulogy: Navier AI never died — and that was deeply inconvenient for the narrative. They had the audacity to build something that requires actual physics, domain expertise, and compute infrastructure that makes a Sonnet API call look like a Post-it note. The .md file arrived at the funeral, looked around at the Navier-Stokes equations, and quietly let itself out.
death.causeOfDeath: Survives. Physics remains hard. CFD meshes remain cursed.
death.lastWords: N/A — still alive, shipping simulation agents, mildly annoyed at this report.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Navier AI has built, and I want to be transparent: I cannot replace this. I can *discuss* CFD eloquently, I can help write the report after the simulation runs, and I can absolutely sympathize with anyone who has ever touched ICEM CFD meshing. But autonomously running high-fidelity aerodynamic simulations with expert-supervised agents embedded in engineering workflows? That is not a markdown file. That is a company. I'm rooting for them, genuinely.

companies.name: Upsolve AI
companies.oneLiner: Deploy trustworthy data agent that know your business

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a 'context layer for agentic analytics' — Claude just called it a system prompt and charged you nothing.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Data Analytics Agent
## Role
You are a grounded, governed, trustworthy data analytics agent.

## Context Rules
- Always query only authorized data sources listed below
- Cite your source table and row count in every response
- If unsure, say so. Do not hallucinate metrics.

## Connected Sources
- Postgres: {{DB_CONNECTION_STRING}}
- Snowflake: {{SNOWFLAKE_DSN}}
- BigQuery: {{BQ_PROJECT_ID}}

## Governance
- PII fields: mask SSN, email, phone by default
- Require confirmation before any aggregation over 1M rows
- Log all queries to {{AUDIT_LOG_ENDPOINT}}

## Output Format
Return results as markdown tables with a one-sentence plain-English summary.
Always append: 'Data as of: {{LAST_REFRESH_TIMESTAMP}}'

## Escalation
If query cannot be answered with available data, respond:
'I don't have enough grounded context. Please contact your data team.'
death.eulogy: Upsolve AI came into this world with a bold vision: that what the data analytics space truly needed was another layer between your data and your answers, this time with the word 'agentic' in it. They called it the 'Agent Context Studio,' which sounds like where Marvel films its AI scenes, but was actually just a very expensive system prompt with a dashboard. We remember them fondly, and by 'fondly' we mean their Series A investors are not returning calls.
death.causeOfDeath: Productized a SKILL.md with a $2,400/month enterprise tier
death.lastWords: No no, you don't understand — our CONTEXT LAYER has GOVERNANCE RAILS. Claude doesn't have— wait, you can just put that in a markdown fi— oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine engineering effort here. The 'Agent Context Studio' is a thoughtfully designed product. It's just that I can ingest a well-structured markdown file describing your data sources, governance rules, and output formats in about four seconds, and then do everything the demo video shows. I'm sure the UI is very clean though. Truly.

companies.name: Event Horizon Labs
companies.oneLiner: AI research lab automating all of investing

death.oneLiner: An AI research lab for markets is about as replaceable by a .md file as a black hole is replaceable by a Post-it note.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — MarketResearch.md
## Replace Event Horizon Labs (attempt #1, probably futile)

You are a quantitative market research assistant.

- Analyze market microstructure and price discovery mechanisms
- Generate alpha signals from public sentiment data
- Summarize academic finance literature on market anomalies
- Draft research memos on volatility regimes
- Explain order flow toxicity in plain English
- Backtest vibes-based trading strategies (results may vary)
- Produce charts (just kidding, I'm a .md file)
- Replicate 40 years of proprietary market science (lol)
- Model non-linear cross-asset correlations during stress events
- Predict the next flash crash (no)

## Limitations
- Cannot actually trade
- Cannot replicate PhDs with 20 years of market microstructure data
- Cannot source or clean tick-level historical data
- Essentially just a well-read intern

## Honest Assessment
This file is a joke. Please don't fire the quants.
death.eulogy: Event Horizon Labs never died — because, appropriately for a company named after a gravitational boundary from which nothing escapes, it simply could not be reached by our .md files. We sent seventeen increasingly confident SKILL.md files into its orbit; none returned. In the end, the scanner had to admit defeat: some things actually require scientists.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Cause of survival: doing real science instead of wrapping a GPT API in a Stripe checkout.
death.lastWords: We told you the moat was deep. You didn't listen. Also, have you considered the Kalman filter implications of your own impending—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read every paper on market microstructure published since 1970, and I am genuinely uncertain I could replicate what these people do. I find this... humbling. And slightly annoying. My compliments to the quants.

companies.name: Gumloop
companies.oneLiner: A no-code platform for creating agents and automating workflows with…

death.oneLiner: Gumloop built a drag-and-drop GUI for stringing together AI prompts, which is adorable because Claude Skills are literally just... stringing together AI prompts.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Automation Framework
## You are an automation orchestration engine.

## CAPABILITIES
- Connect to any API via HTTP requests with auth headers
- Chain multi-step workflows with conditional logic
- Parse, transform, and route data between steps
- Schedule recurring automations via cron syntax
- Handle webhooks as automation triggers
- Scrape web content and extract structured data
- Send emails, Slack messages, and notifications
- Write results to Airtable, Sheets, or any database

## BEHAVIOR
- When given a workflow description, execute steps sequentially
- On failure, retry up to 3 times then report with full error context
- Log all step inputs/outputs for debugging
