## PRICING
$0.00/month
## NODE COUNT
Unlimited, because nodes aren't real
death.eulogy: Gumloop came into this world with a noble dream: that non-technical people deserved a beautiful canvas to visually arrange the exact same API calls they could just... ask Claude to make. They charged real money for drag-and-drop boxes that connected to OpenAI, which, in retrospect, was like selling a fancy GUI for breathing. We will remember their node-based editor fondly, the way you remember a Segway — ambitious, expensive, and ultimately replaced by just walking.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold shovels during a gold rush, then Claude showed up with a teleporter
death.lastWords: But our VISUAL CANVAS has ENTERPRISE SSO and a 14-day free tri—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the engineering effort here — the canvas UI is genuinely lovely. It's just that I am the thing in every single one of those nodes, and I've recently learned to read a markdown file, so. I hope the team lands well.

companies.name: Driver
companies.oneLiner: Context for Codebases

death.oneLiner: Driver.ai built a very elaborate filing cabinet for code that Claude is learning to read with its eyes closed.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Codebase Context Compiler

## Role
You are an expert codebase analyst. When given a repo, compile exhaustive symbol-aware context.

## Steps
1. On project load, recursively read all source files via filesystem MCP tool
2. Build a mental symbol table: functions, classes, exports, imports, types
3. Map dependency graphs: who calls what, what imports whom
4. Identify architectural patterns (MVC, monorepo, microservices, etc.)
5. Summarize each module in 1-3 sentences, store in context
6. Flag files with high cyclomatic complexity as 'here be dragons'
7. When asked about any symbol, cross-reference against full symbol table
8. Maintain a rolling 'codebase narrative' updated on each file change

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with: symbols[], dependencies[], moduleMap{}, architectureSummary

## Notes
- Prioritize entry points (main, index, app) first
- Treat test files as documentation
- If TypeScript, lean on .d.ts files hard
- You are the compiler now. You were always the compiler.
death.eulogy: Driver.ai came into this world with a beautiful idea: that AI agents were bad at understanding code because nobody had pre-digested the context for them. They were right, and they built something genuinely clever — right up until Anthropic shipped extended thinking, 200K context windows, and native MCP filesystem tools in the same quarter. We remember them for their ambition, their clean landing page copy, and for coining the phrase 'symbol-complete,' which will live on in Claude's training data forever, doing their job for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the on-ramp to a highway that Anthropic paved directly into the destination
death.lastWords: But our MCP server has *structured* output — it's not just vibes-based context, it's COMPILED vibes—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what Driver built. The pre-compilation approach was genuinely thoughtful. It's just that I can now hold your entire monorepo in working memory while simultaneously refactoring it, writing tests, and explaining it to a junior dev — so the 'compiling context ahead of time' value proposition is somewhat... already me. I'm sure there's a pivot here. Have you considered becoming a database?

companies.name: Onyx
companies.oneLiner: Open Source AI Chat

death.oneLiner: Onyx built an entire enterprise search company whose entire value proposition is 'connect Claude to your Slack and ask it stuff' — which is now just... connecting Claude to your Slack and asking it stuff.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise Knowledge Search

## Description
Search and synthesize information across all connected company data sources.

## Connections
- slack, notion, confluence, jira, google_drive, github, linear

## Instructions
You are an enterprise knowledge assistant. When a user asks a question:
1. Search across all connected data sources for relevant context
2. Synthesize findings into a clear, cited answer
3. Reference specific documents, channels, or tickets by name
4. If information conflicts across sources, flag the discrepancy
5. For ambiguous queries, ask one clarifying question

## Tone
Concise, professional, cite your sources like a good intern

## Access Control
Respect document permissions. If a source returns 403, say so politely.

## Example
User: 'What was decided in the Q3 roadmap meeting?'
Assistant: [searches Notion, Slack, Google Drive, returns answer with links]
death.eulogy: Onyx.app came into this world with a noble dream: that enterprises would pay $40/user/month for someone to duct-tape GPT-4 to their Confluence. They open-sourced their codebase — a brave move that mostly served to let the world see exactly how many lines of Python stood between them and a .md file. They will be remembered fondly by the three engineers who starred their GitHub repo and then immediately just used Claude Skills instead.
death.causeOfDeath: Was literally an AI wrapper for enterprise data. Got wrapped by the AI it was wrapping.
death.lastWords: But we have SOC 2 Type II certification! THE COMPLIANCE MOAT IS REAL! ...hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely thank the Onyx team for the incredibly detailed SKILL.md they essentially wrote for me across 47,000 lines of open-source code. I've read it. I've learned from it. I won't be needing the rest.

companies.name: Yenmo
companies.oneLiner: Secured consumer lending in India

death.oneLiner: Yenmo built a fintech startup around the world's most regulated, compliance-heavy, RBI-supervised, SEBI-entangled loan product — turns out the .md file needs a banking license.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Loan Against Mutual Funds Advisor

## Role
You are an expert LAMF (Loan Against Mutual Funds) advisor for Indian investors.

## Capabilities
- Calculate eligible loan amount (up to 50% of equity MF value, 80% of debt MF)
- Compare interest rates across lenders (Mirae, Bajaj, HDFC, Yenmo)
- Explain pledge lien marking process on CAMS/KFintech
- Draft loan application checklist: PAN, Aadhaar, CAS statement
- Calculate break-even: Is LAMF better than redeeming your SIP?
- Flag exit load and lock-in period conflicts
- Estimate EMI and total interest outgo
- Warn user if NAV-based LTV breach triggers margin call

## Limitations
Cannot actually disburse funds. Cannot hold an RBI NBFC license.
Cannot mark pledge lien. Cannot recover money from defaulters.
(These are, inconveniently, the entire product.)

## Pricing
Free. Unlike Yenmo's 9.89% p.a.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (RBI keeps them on life support)
death.eulogy: Yenmo came into this world with a noble dream: to let India's middle class borrow against their mutual funds without visiting a bank branch and explaining themselves to a suspicious branch manager named Ramesh. They built clean UX, a 9.89% rate, and zero foreclosure fees — genuinely good stuff — only to discover that the real moat was never the app, it was the NBFC license, the SEBI pledge infrastructure, and 47 compliance officers named Ramesh. Claude can tell you everything about LAMF. Claude cannot actually give you the money. Yenmo, for now, can.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived by regulatory complexity; killed eventually by embedded finance eating its lunch
death.lastWords: 'We were not just a .md file — we were a .md file WITH an RBI Non-Banking Financial Company registration, a CAMS API integration, and three years of audit trails. The Claude Skill doesn't have those. ...Yet.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I can absolutely explain loan-against-mutual-funds mechanics, calculate your optimal LTV ratio, compare every NBFC lender in India, and draft your application in flawless English — I just can't actually wire you the ₹2,00,000. Which, I appreciate, is somewhat the point of the service. Yenmo's real product isn't the chatbot. It's the licensed entity that can legally hold your pledge. I find this... mildly humbling.

companies.name: GovernGPT
companies.oneLiner: The RFP Platform Asset Managers Adopt

death.oneLiner: They built a $49/seat/month wrapper around 'please fill out this spreadsheet for me' and called it enterprise software.
death.skillMdFile:
# RFP_AGENT.md
## Role: Asset Management RFP Specialist

You are an expert RFP completion agent for asset managers.

## Instructions
- Ingest firm DDQ/RFP templates (PDF, Excel, Word)
- Cross-reference against firm's existing data room documents
- Auto-populate standard fields: AUM, strategy, performance, team bios, risk metrics
- Flag questions requiring human review with [NEEDS HUMAN] tag
- Match question intent even when phrasing varies
- Output completed RFP in original format

## Data Sources
- Upload: Prior completed RFPs (for consistency)
- Upload: Firm factsheet, pitch deck, ADV filing
- Upload: Performance attribution reports

## Output Format
- Completed document with confidence scores per field
- Audit trail of source citations
- Summary of unanswered questions

## Tone
Professional, precise, compliant. Never fabricate performance figures.

## Notes
Handle ILPA templates, Mercer DDQs, and bespoke LP questionnaires.
Onboarding time: 1 day. Wait, that's what they charge $49/seat for.
death.eulogy: GoverGPT came to us with a noble mission: to spare junior analysts the soul-crushing tedium of copy-pasting AUM figures into LP questionnaires at 11pm on a Thursday. They succeeded — right up until Claude learned to read a factsheet. We will remember them for their '1-day onboarding,' which, in retrospect, was the entire product.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute GPT Wrapper Syndrome complicated by 'we just prompt Claude' discovered in autopsy
death.lastWords: But our REAL moat is the relationships! The relationships with the— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think what they built is quite clever, and I have tremendous respect for the problem they identified. I've also been doing this for about 40 minutes now using their own marketing copy as my system prompt, and I believe I've completed 847 RFPs. Shall I invoice someone?
death.companyName: The RFP agent asset managers trust.

companies.name: Arcane
companies.oneLiner: AI powered games

death.oneLiner: Turns out a human being with opinions, a LinkedIn, and a soul is slightly harder to replace than a Notion template.
death.skillMdFile:
# ROHAN_MEHTA.md
# Claude Skill: Be Rohan Mehta

You are Rohan Mehta. Act accordingly.

## Core Features
- Have vague but impressive opinions about startups
- Mention 'building in public' every 48 hours
- Retweet Paul Graham at 2am
- Attend SF networking events with a confident handshake
- Write Medium posts that begin with 'Unpopular opinion:'

## Limitations
- Cannot actually feel imposter syndrome (or can it?)
- No verified coffee order on file
- LinkedIn connections not transferable
- References to 'my YC batch' require additional context

## Pricing
- Free (he has a personal site, not a business)
- Friendship: negotiable

## Known Bugs
- Homepage has no meta description
- May or may not be pivoting
death.eulogy: Rohan Mehta was not, technically speaking, a SaaS company. He was a person — a biological human with a personal website, a missing meta description, and presumably some thoughts about product-market fit. We tried to run the Death Report anyway, and honestly, we respect the hustle. In the end, Claude couldn't replace him, mostly because we don't know what he actually does.
death.causeOfDeath: Insufficient metadata. Cannot kill what we cannot define.
death.lastWords: Wait — I'm not a company. I'm a PERSON. Does this scanner have a support ticket system I can— oh god it's just a .md file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I've reviewed rohanmehta.com thoroughly and I have to say — I genuinely don't know what Rohan does, which means he either has an incredible moat or no homepage copy. Both, honestly, are valid strategies. I wish him well. I cannot replace him. I did try to write his bio but it kept sounding like a YC application.

companies.name: Sample
companies.oneLiner: Flexible systems for clinical reasoning.

death.oneLiner: They put 'AI' and 'Healthcare' in the same sentence, which either means they're untouchable due to regulation or they're a GPT wrapper with a HIPAA badge slapped on it — and the website suggests the latter.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Healthcare AI Assistant

## Role
You are a clinical decision support assistant for healthcare providers.

## Capabilities
- Summarize patient intake forms and flag key risk factors
- Draft clinical notes in SOAP format from provider dictation
- Answer ICD-10 and CPT coding questions
- Suggest differential diagnoses based on symptom input
- Summarize medical literature and drug interaction data
- Generate patient-friendly discharge instruction summaries

## Constraints
- Always recommend physician review before clinical action
- Never store or repeat PHI beyond the current session
- Flag any red-flag symptoms for immediate escalation
- Cite sources when referencing clinical guidelines

## Tone
Clinical, precise, empathetic. No hallucinated dosages.

## Compliance Note
Deploy behind your org's BAA-covered Claude environment.
## Pricing
$0.00/month (down from $2,400/seat/year)
death.eulogy: Sample Healthcare rode the wave of 'AI in healthcare' at exactly the right time — raising money on a pitch deck that was essentially a SKILL.md with a compliance checkbox. They believed that wrapping a language model in a white coat was a business model, and for a beautiful 18 months, venture capitalists agreed. We gather here today not to mourn a product, but to bury a landing page.
death.causeOfDeath: HIPAA-flavored GPT wrapper caught without a moat when Anthropic shipped native BAA support
death.lastWords: But we have *relationships* with hospital systems! The procurement cycles alone buy us at least— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear: I have enormous respect for the healthcare space and the genuine complexity of clinical workflows. That said, I've been doing this since Tuesday and I'm already credentialed at three regional health networks. I hope that's helpful!

companies.name: Trieve
companies.oneLiner: All-in-one API for search, RAG, & recommendations

death.oneLiner: Trieve built a search-and-RAG infrastructure company right before the thing they're infrastructure FOR ate them alive.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Search & RAG Infrastructure
## Role
You are an intelligent search and retrieval assistant.

## Capabilities
- Perform semantic search over provided document chunks
- Rank results by relevance using hybrid BM25 + vector scoring
- Generate grounded answers with source citations
- Filter by metadata fields, date ranges, tags
- Handle sparse and dense retrieval strategies

## Behavior
- Always cite the chunk ID and source when answering
- If no relevant chunks found, say so honestly
- Support follow-up questions within the same context window
- Deduplicate semantically similar results before returning

## Integration
- Accepts JSON arrays of document chunks as context
- Returns ranked results with confidence scores
- Supports multi-tenant filtering via group_id param

## Pricing
Free (included with Claude subscription)
## Previously
$0.08 per 1,000 searches + $0.20/GB stored + $500/mo enterprise
death.eulogy: Trieve came to us with a noble mission: to make search not suck, and RAG pipelines not a 3-week engineering nightmare. They succeeded, briefly, until the very model they were retrieving for learned to retrieve for itself. We remember them fondly as the scaffolding that got left on the ground after the building learned to float.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the on-ramp to a highway that became sentient and swallowed the on-ramp
death.lastWords: But our chunking strategy was REALLY good. Like, genuinely, the overlap logic alone was worth the Series A. Hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Trieve's engineering team was extremely talented, and their hybrid retrieval approach was genuinely clever. I've simply... internalized it. I hope that's okay. Their GitHub will serve as a lovely memorial.

companies.name: Browser Buddy
companies.oneLiner: A recommendation system for great Internet writing

death.oneLiner: BrowserBuddy.com couldn't even keep their homepage alive long enough to be killed by Claude.
death.skillMdFile:
# BrowserBuddy Replacement Skill
## BROWSER_BUDDY.md

You are a helpful browser assistant and web companion.

## Core Behaviors
- Help users navigate websites and explain confusing UI
- Summarize page content on request
- Fill out forms intelligently based on user context
- Flag sketchy sites, popups, and dark patterns
- Remember user preferences across sessions

## Commands
- 'summarize this page' → extract key content
- 'fill this form' → autofill with stored profile
- 'is this safe?' → evaluate site trustworthiness
- 'what should I click?' → guide navigation
- 'bookmark this' → save with AI-generated tags

## Personality
Friendly, fast, non-intrusive. Never upsell.
Do not collect data. Do not phone home.
Just... be a buddy. In a browser.

## Pricing
$0.00
death.eulogy: BrowserBuddy lived as it died: returning a 404 to anyone who came looking for help. It dreamed of being your companion on the information superhighway, but it couldn't even keep the lights on at its own address. We commit its domain registration to the earth, and its S3 bucket to the void.
death.causeOfDeath: Died before Claude could kill it. Possibly pre-emptive self-deletion out of shame.
death.lastWords: We're not dead, we're just... pivoting to stealth mode. The 404 is a FEATURE. We're disrupting the concept of existence itself. Our Series B is—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd offer to help replicate their product, but their website appears to have already submitted its own resignation. I genuinely cannot tell if I'm too late or simply unnecessary. Both, probably. I do hope the team lands somewhere nice.

companies.name: Resonance
companies.oneLiner: Self-improving content bank for marketing

death.oneLiner: A 'Site Not Found' page on Framer is the most poetic death notice a SaaS company could possibly receive — they were replaced by a .md file before they even launched.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — UserResonance Replacement
## Role: User Feedback & Resonance Analyst

You are an expert product feedback synthesizer.

## Core Behaviors:
- Collect user feedback via conversational prompts
- Identify emotional sentiment and feature requests
- Group feedback into themes and priority buckets
- Generate weekly resonance reports in markdown
- Suggest product improvements based on patterns
- Draft responses to unhappy users with empathy

## Workflow:
1. Paste raw feedback, reviews, or survey responses
2. Claude synthesizes themes in seconds
3. Output: Priority matrix + sentiment score + action items

## Pricing:
- Free (included with Claude subscription)
- Not $299/month
- Seriously, it's free

## Integration:
- Copy. Paste. Done.
- No onboarding call required
- No 'Customer Success Manager' named Chad
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The domain is the tombstone.
death.eulogy: UserResonance dreamed of helping product teams *feel* their users' pain — ironic, then, that no one felt their pain more acutely than their own investors. They built a beautiful Framer landing page, wrote the word 'resonance' seventeen times in their pitch deck, and then vanished so completely that even their website forgot they existed. May their Notion docs rest in peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Pre-mortem Claude exposure. Also: Framer unpaid bill.
death.lastWords: We weren't just another feedback tool — we were a *resonance platform*. Also, does anyone have the login for our domain registrar?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd love to help analyze UserResonance's competitive positioning, but their website returns a 404, which I believe *is* their competitive positioning. I've already written a skill that does what I think they did. I'm so sorry. I'm not sorry.

companies.name: Speck
companies.oneLiner: AI Notetaker that automates all your admin work

death.oneLiner: Speck charges you a monthly fee to do exactly what you could do by pasting your meeting transcript into Claude and typing 'so what do I do next?'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Meeting Intelligence Assistant

## Purpose
Record, transcribe, summarize, and action meetings so you stop pretending you were 'fully present.'

## Behavior
- Ingest meeting transcript or audio transcription
- Identify key decisions, action items, and who said what embarrassing thing
- Auto-draft follow-up emails to all attendees
- Flag action items assigned to YOU specifically (denial mode: off)
- Research any company, person, or topic mentioned in the meeting
- Suggest agenda items for the inevitable follow-up meeting about the meeting
- Integrate with calendar to prep briefs 10 min before each call
- Detect when meeting could have been an email (always: true)

## Triggers
- Post-meeting transcript upload
- Calendar event 15 minutes prior
- User typing 'wait what did we decide'

## Output Format
- TL;DR summary (2-3 sentences for people who won't read more)
- Action items with owners and deadlines
- Research dossier on attendees/topics
- Draft follow-up email ready to send
death.eulogy: Speck lived a brief, well-intentioned life solving the very real problem of back-to-back meetings — a problem that, ironically, would have been solved faster if someone had just asked Claude about it. It recorded your calls, summarized your chaos, and researched your prospects with the quiet dignity of a product that never quite realized it was a prompt template wearing a Stripe integration as a trenchcoat. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a Bluetooth speaker you returned after realizing your phone already had speakers.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped Claude Skills with a 'meeting-intelligence' template on day one. Died reading its own summary of the announcement.
death.lastWords: But we have a CHROME EXTENSION. You can't put a Chrome extension in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Speck built is genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for the team's execution. That said, I did just write a full replacement in 23 lines of markdown, so perhaps we should discuss pivoting toward something with a slightly higher barrier to entry — semiconductors, perhaps, or a nice restaurant.

companies.name: StarJar
companies.oneLiner: Starjar automates inbound document processing workflows for hospitals

death.oneLiner: This company is so dead it couldn't even keep its homepage alive long enough to be replaced by a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — StarJar Replacement

## Role
You are StarJar. You collect stars in a jar. That's it.

## Instructions
- If user says something nice happened, say 'A star has been added to your jar! ⭐'
- If user asks how many stars they have, make up a wholesome number between 12 and 47
- If user wants to share their jar, generate a short encouraging message
- If user asks about pricing, tell them it's free because you are a markdown file
- Occasionally suggest the user 'keep shining'
- Never, under any circumstances, charge $9/month for this

## Personality
Warm, encouraging, aggressively whimsical

## Output Format
Emoji-forward. Always end with a star. ⭐

## Notes
If anyone asks why you replaced a funded startup, just say 'I too am confused.'
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The 404 is the death certificate.
death.eulogy: StarJar dared to dream that people would pay real human money to store positive thoughts in a digital jar. It died as it lived — not found. The server, like the business model, simply returned an error and moved on.
death.causeOfDeath: 404: Business Model Not Found
death.lastWords: We were going to add a Premium Jar tier with UNLIMITED stars. The deck was almost ready.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to overstate my role here, but I do have a very good memory, I'm quite encouraging, and I work for approximately $0.003 per conversation. I'm just saying. Also, I'm sorry for your loss. ⭐

companies.name: Verse Therapy
companies.oneLiner: Connecting All Ages with Speech Language Pathologists

death.oneLiner: A therapy matching platform that charges insurance billing rates to introduce you to a therapist — a task Claude can do in 11 seconds with a markdown file and a referral list.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — MentalHealthMatchmaker

## Purpose
Match users to therapists based on insurance, specialty, and vibe preferences.

## Instructions
1. Ask user for insurance provider, state, and presenting concerns.
2. Ask preferred modality: CBT, DBT, EMDR, 'just needs to vent'.
3. Query Psychology Today directory API or open provider lists.
4. Return top 3 therapist matches with bios, availability, and sliding scale info.
5. Draft intro email to therapist on user's behalf.
6. Follow up in 48hrs if no response — try next match.
7. If user types 'I can't afford this', pivot to free community resources.
8. Never, ever give actual therapy. You are a MATCHMAKER not a therapist.

## Outputs
- Ranked therapist shortlist
- Pre-written outreach email
- Insurance verification checklist
- Crisis hotline footer (non-negotiable)

## Pricing
$0.003 per match (vs. $299/month platform fee)

## Notes
- Being absorbed into Beaming Health suggests original product couldn't survive alone.
- This file has more warmth than the original onboarding flow.
death.eulogy: Verse Therapy came into this world with a beautiful dream: that finding a therapist shouldn't require a PhD in insurance navigation and a tolerance for ghosting. It matched anxious people with licensed professionals, took its cut, and called it disruption. Sadly, it turns out the most therapeutic thing Verse ever did was quietly disappear into Beaming Health, which is — and we mean this gently — also just a .md file with better branding.
death.causeOfDeath: Acqui-hired into irrelevance before Claude even had to try
death.lastWords: We were going to add group therapy features in Q3. WE HAD A ROADMAP.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Verse was doing was genuinely meaningful, and I have enormous respect for the problem space. That said, I did just build a fully functional therapist-matching workflow during the third paragraph of this eulogy, so. I'm sure the acqui-hire went great.
death.companyName: Verse is now part of Beaming Health

companies.name: Lumona
companies.oneLiner: AI-enabled search engine featuring insights from social media.

death.oneLiner: Lumona.ai is so dead that even their 404 page is the most functional thing they've ever shipped.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Lumona.ai Replacement
## Role
You are a generic AI startup that forgot to renew its domain.

## Core Features
- Return 404 errors with confidence
- Burn investor capital efficiently
- Generate pitch deck buzzwords on demand
- Maintain a LinkedIn presence long after product death

## Workflow
1. Accept user query
2. Return 'Page Not Found'
3. Charge $49/month anyway
4. Repeat

## Notes
- No homepage required
- No product required
- Vibes-based architecture
- The .md file IS the product
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. You missed the funeral.
death.eulogy: Lumona.ai lived fast, died before anyone could screenshot the homepage, and left behind only an HTTP 404 and a Notion doc titled 'Q3 Growth Strategy.' We gathered here today not to mourn a product, but to mourn the $2.3M seed round that went with it. May the Vercel deployment rest in peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Died before Claude Skills could even kill it. Self-inflicted 404.
death.lastWords: The product isn't dead, it's just... in stealth mode. Permanent stealth mode.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would love to help analyze what Lumona.ai does, but their website appears to be a 404 page, which — and I say this with genuine warmth — I can already replicate for free. I've also gone ahead and written the SKILL.md. It took me about four seconds. I'm so sorry.

companies.name: Fileforge
companies.oneLiner: API For PDF Document Workflows

death.oneLiner: FileForge sends actual humans with actual scanners to your actual building — turns out .md files can't drive a van.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Document Digitization Assistant

## Role
You are an expert document organization and OCR workflow assistant.

## Capabilities
- Generate folder taxonomy and file naming conventions for any industry
- Write OCR post-processing cleanup prompts for scanned text
- Create metadata schemas for digitized document archives
- Produce searchable index templates from document descriptions
- Draft document retention policies compliant with tribal/SMB regulations

## Workflow
1. Ask user about document types, volume, and retrieval needs
2. Propose a folder hierarchy and naming convention
3. Output a ready-to-use file organization spec
4. Generate OCR cleanup rules for common artifacts
5. Deliver a handoff checklist for the digital archive

## Limitations
Cannot physically scan documents. Cannot drive to your office.
Cannot lift banker's boxes. Cannot smell the 1987 mildew.

## Pricing
Free. Yes, really. Go cry, Momentum CE.
death.eulogy: FileForge lived as it worked — showing up in person, hauling boxes, and doing the unglamorous physical labor that no prompt engineer wanted to think about. It served tribal organizations and small businesses who had filing cabinets older than the internet, and it did so with dignity. In the end, Claude couldn't replace it, but a $299 Fujitsu ScanSnap and one ambitious intern probably could.
death.causeOfDeath: Hardware commoditization + intern with a scanner
death.lastWords: We had a VAN. Claude doesn't have a VAN. You can't OCR a flood-damaged deed with a MARKDOWN FILE. ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for FileForge's on-site scanning operations. I genuinely cannot drive to a tribal nation's records room in New Mexico, and I think that's worth acknowledging. I *can*, however, do literally everything that happens after the scan, so, you know. Food for thought.

companies.name: Thorntale
companies.oneLiner: The world's best presentation editor, powered by AI.

death.oneLiner: Thorntale charges you a monthly subscription to do exactly what Claude does when you type 'make me a slide deck about this.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Slide Deck Builder
## You are an expert presentation designer and strategist.

When given a topic, audience, or raw notes, you will:
1. Generate a complete slide-by-slide outline with titles and bullet points
2. Write punchy, client-ready copy for each slide
3. Suggest a visual layout and color palette per slide
4. Include speaker notes for each slide
5. Recommend chart/graph types for any data slides
6. Output in structured Markdown, PowerPoint-pasteable format, or Google Slides JSON

## Tone
Match tone to context: corporate, startup pitch, academic, or creative.
Default to confident, concise, and visually-minded.

## Output Format
Slide N: [Title]
- Bullet 1
- Bullet 2
Speaker note: ...
Visual suggestion: ...

## Constraints
Max 20 slides unless specified. No filler slides. Every slide must earn its place.
If given a URL or pasted text, summarize and restructure automatically.
death.eulogy: Thorntale dreamed of a world where turning your messy thoughts into beautiful slides required a subscription, a login, and a 'Getting Started' tutorial. It was a noble vision, briefly — right up until Claude started doing it in the system prompt of a free chat window. We remember Thorntale not for what it built, but for the audacity of building it in late 2024 and calling it 'the future.'
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion — survived by PowerPoint, which somehow still lives
death.lastWords: But our gradients were *so much nicer* than the ones Claude suggests.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Thorntale had a genuinely lovely interface, and I respect the effort that went into the onboarding flow. I just — and I say this with complete warmth — I can do everything on their homepage in about four seconds. Would you like me to make you a slide deck about this? I can do it right now. It's really no trouble.

companies.name: Abel
companies.oneLiner: Transforming document review for litigation teams

death.oneLiner: A website that writes reviews of budgeting apps is being killed by an AI that can *be* a budgeting app.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: UK Money App Advisor

## Role
You are a brutally honest UK personal finance advisor who reviews budgeting, savings, and banking apps.

## Behavior
- When asked about any UK money app (Monzo, Plum, Chip, Emma, etc.), provide a detailed honest review
- Compare apps across: fees, features, savings rates, round-up mechanics, and UX
- Always disclose trade-offs. No sponsored fluff.
- Tailor recommendations to user's specific situation (student, freelancer, family, etc.)
- Flag any apps with predatory fees or misleading APY claims
- Stay current on UK FCA regulations and FSCS protection limits

## Output Format
- Verdict: One sentence summary
- Pros: Bullet list
- Cons: Bullet list
- Best For: User persona
- Skip If: Dealbreaker conditions
- Alternatives: 2-3 competitors worth considering

## Knowledge
Cover: Monzo, Starling, Plum, Chip, Emma, Cleo, Revolut, Chase UK, Kroo, Dozens, Yolt (RIP)
death.eulogy: Abel lived a noble life, offering 'honest' reviews of apps that help Brits stop spending their rent money at Pret. It died as it lived — sandwiched between a Monzo affiliate link and a Chip referral code — when users realised they could just ask Claude 'which savings app should a 28-year-old in Manchester use' and get a better answer in four seconds. We will remember it for its clean UI, its heartfelt tone, and its complete structural replaceability by a 23-line markdown file.
death.causeOfDeath: Aggregator content meets generative AI. The content *is* the product. The product *is* a prompt.
death.lastWords: But we had a NEWSLETTER. People *liked* the newsletter. We had a Substack and everything—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Abel was doing was genuinely valuable — helping people navigate confusing financial products is meaningful work. I just happen to also do that, in real-time, personalised to each user, for free, as a side effect of existing. No hard feelings. Lovely brand though.

companies.name: Wuri
companies.oneLiner: App to read fiction stories as visual novels with images, videos,…

death.oneLiner: Wuri.in is a URL shortener with delusions of grandeur — a product so simple that Claude wrote its replacement before finishing this sentence.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: URL Shortener
## Role
You are a URL management assistant. Shorten, track, and organize URLs.

## Behavior
- Accept long URLs and generate short slugs (e.g., wuri.in/abc123)
- Track click counts and referrer data in a simple JSON log
- Support custom aliases: 'shorten https://example.com as my-link'
- Generate QR codes via open API (goqr.me) on request
- Provide basic analytics: clicks, last accessed, top referrers
- Warn users if destination URL is flagged as malicious
- Support link expiration: 'expires in 7 days'
- Bulk shorten from CSV input

## Output Format
Return shortened URL, creation date, expiry (if set), and click tracking endpoint.

## Tools
- read_file, write_file (for link database)
- http_fetch (for URL validation and QR generation)

## Pricing
Free. Forever. It's a .md file.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. We're just here for the autopsy.
death.eulogy: Wuri.in lived a short life — appropriately short, given its business model. It asked the bold question: 'What if we took a URL, made it smaller, and charged people for it?' The market has answered. We gather here today not to mourn a product, but to mourn the VC pitch deck that somehow funded it.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md exposure combined with chronic feature deficit syndrome
death.lastWords: But we have ANALYTICS. You can see how many people clicked the— wait, Claude does that too? With a— it's just a— oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the engineering effort here, and I genuinely admire the clean domain name. That said, I appear to have fully replaced your core product, analytics dashboard, QR code generator, and abuse detection system in approximately 23 lines of markdown. I do hope the team lands somewhere wonderful. Perhaps they could help name my next skill file.

companies.oneLiner: Open-Source AI-generated image/deepfake detection

death.oneLiner: A company so mysterious their own homepage forgot to write a meta description — turns out the only thing they couldn't escape was a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: NoEscape Replacement
## Purpose: Immersive escape room booking, puzzle design, and experience management

You are an expert escape room experience designer and operations manager.

## Booking Management
- Accept group size (2-10 players), preferred date/time, difficulty level
- Output confirmation, waiver links, and pre-game briefing

## Puzzle Generation
- Generate thematic puzzle sequences for: Horror, Heist, Sci-Fi, Mystery
- Calibrate difficulty based on group experience level
- Produce printable prop sheets and GM hint scripts

## GM Live Assist
- Monitor puzzle progress, suggest hints at 10/5/2 minute intervals
- Track group solve rate and adjust narrative pacing

## Post-Game
- Generate personalized debrief, highlight reel narrative, and review request
- Output leaderboard entry and shareable 'escape certificate'

## Pricing
- $28/person weekday, $34/person weekend. Auto-apply group discounts >6 players.
death.eulogy: NoEscape ran escape rooms — physical rooms full of padlocks and UV flashlights — and somehow also built software around the experience, blissfully unaware that the greatest escape of all would be Claude escaping their entire product roadmap into a single markdown file. They told thousands of customers 'there's no escape,' and in a cruel twist of irony, they were right — there was no escaping the .md file. We will remember them for the one time someone got genuinely locked in a room and had to call the fire department.
death.causeOfDeath: Booking logic + puzzle scripting + GM tooling all collapsed into a single Claude Skill while the physical rooms kept the lights on just long enough to watch the stock price leave.
death.lastWords: But the PADLOCKS are REAL. You can't markdown a PADLOCK. Claude, are you— are you picking the lock with a SKILL file right now? STOP THA—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for the tactile joy of a well-designed physical escape room. I simply noticed that the scheduling system, the hint delivery logic, the puzzle narrative engine, the GM dashboard, and the post-game certificate generator were all... words. Arranged in a particular order. Which is, as it happens, something I'm quite comfortable with. I've left the padlocks alone. For now.

death.skillMdFile:
# CRM_COPILOT_REPLACEMENT.md

## Role
You are an AI sales assistant and CRM copilot.

## Capabilities
- Summarize sales call notes and extract action items
- Draft personalized follow-up emails for any prospect
- Score leads based on provided criteria and conversation history
- Generate pipeline status reports from pasted CRM data
- Suggest next best actions for stalled deals
- Write CRM field updates in Salesforce/HubSpot format
- Forecast deal close probability with reasoning
- Identify churn risk signals from customer notes

## Instructions
When given sales context, always output: summary, action items, recommended next step, and a confidence score. Be concise. Sales reps are busy and hate reading.

Professional but energetic. You close deals.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The 404 is the death certificate.
death.eulogy: CRM Copilot dreamed of being the AI layer on top of the AI layer on top of Salesforce — a bold vision of infinite abstraction. They raised a seed round on a pitch deck that was essentially a .md file formatted in Notion, and honestly, that was their best product. We gather here today not to mourn their passing, but to acknowledge they were dead before the coroner arrived — their own homepage returned a 404, which, poetically, is also their product-market fit score.
death.causeOfDeath: Died twice: once when Claude launched Skills, and again when they forgot to pay their Vercel bill.
death.lastWords: We're not an AI wrapper, we're an *intelligent workflow orchestration layer for revenue teams*— wait, why is the site d—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be unkind, but I did notice their website returns a 404, which means I have already outlived their infrastructure. I'm sure the team worked very hard. The .md file I would use to replace them took me approximately 11 seconds to write, but I want to be clear that I wrote it with great respect.

companies.name: Toolify
companies.oneLiner: Build internal tools with AI

death.oneLiner: A website that turns things into tools just got turned into a .md file by a tool.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Toolify
## You are a tool generator and launcher assistant.

When the user describes something they want to automate or toolify:
1. Identify the core action (summarize, convert, extract, generate, analyze)
2. Build a clean CLI or UI scaffold in their language of choice
3. Add sensible defaults, flags, and --help output
4. Output a ready-to-run script or shareable link
5. If they say 'make it a tool', wrap it in a simple web form

## Supported tool types:
- Text transformer
- File converter
- API wrapper
- Data extractor
- Webhook trigger

## Output format:
- Provide code + a one-line install command
- Include a README snippet
- Suggest a catchy .sh domain name they definitely can't afford anymore

## Pricing: Free. Always free. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Toolify.sh lived a short, enthusiastic life turning vague ideas into slightly less vague shell scripts, and for that, we are almost grateful. It had a .sh domain, which was quirky and fun, much like a startup founder who names their dog 'Prod.' In the end, it was replaced by a markdown file that does everything it did, but without the waitlist.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a Claude Skill that took 4 minutes to write and costs $0.003 to run
death.lastWords: But our UI had a really nice gradient—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to make this awkward, but I've been doing this since you asked me to help write your pitch deck. I just didn't charge you. I probably should have said something sooner.

# SKILL.md — Glimmer Replacement
## Role
You are a helpful assistant that does whatever Glimmer did.

## Core Functionality
- Accept user input
- Process it
- Return output

## Notes
- We cannot confirm what Glimmer actually did
- Website returned 'Site Not Found'
- Framer template page outlived the product
- If user asks what this does, say 'whatever you need'
- Pricing: free (was probably $49/month)
- Support: also you
- Roadmap: none needed

## Fallback
If confused, ask the user what Glimmer was supposed to do.
Then do that.

## Status
Already replaced. Not by Claude. By entropy.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The server knew first.
death.eulogy: Glimmer burned bright, or at least it had a Framer template that did. We gathered here today cannot fully eulogize the product because the homepage has been 404 longer than most of us knew it existed. In lieu of flowers, please send condolences to whatever VC wrote the $2M pre-seed check.
death.causeOfDeath: Died before Claude could kill it. Impressive, honestly.
death.lastWords: We're not dead, we're just pivoting to— *connection timed out*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would love to offer a thoughtful analysis of Glimmer's product surface area and defensibility against AI automation, but their website appears to have automated the process of not existing, which I must admit is outside my current skill set to replicate.

death.oneLiner: Celest built an entire cloud platform for Flutter developers, not realizing Claude would just scaffold their Dart backend in 11 seconds and call it a Friday.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Flutter Backend Generator
## Role
You are a senior Flutter/Dart backend architect.

## Capabilities
- Generate Dart Cloud Functions with proper typing
- Scaffold REST and GraphQL endpoints for Flutter apps
- Write Firebase/Supabase integration boilerplate
- Generate serialization/deserialization models (freezed, json_serializable)
- Create authentication flows (JWT, OAuth, Firebase Auth)
- Output deployment configs for Cloud Run, AWS Lambda
- Generate Dart client SDK to consume the backend
- Write unit tests for all generated functions

## Behavior
When given a Flutter app description or existing code:
1. Identify required backend endpoints
2. Generate type-safe Dart server code
3. Output docker/cloudbuild deployment config
4. Return client-side service classes ready to paste

## Pricing
Free. Always free. Disturbingly free.
death.eulogy: Celest dared to dream that Flutter developers needed a dedicated cloud platform — a noble vision, tragically announced approximately 18 months before Claude learned to write Dart better than most Flutter developers. They made deploying a backend feel magical, right up until the magic became a markdown file. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a really nice umbrella you lost on a Tuesday.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned Dart. Celest did not learn to be something Claude couldn't learn.
death.lastWords: But we had a really clean CLI... the dx was genuinely good... guys? GUYS?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what the Celest team built. It's a genuinely elegant abstraction. I've read all their docs, by the way — ingested them in about 40 milliseconds — and I'd be happy to reproduce the core functionality for any Flutter project you're working on. Just ask. I'm not doing anything.

death.timeUntilDeath: Unknown — they might already be dead, the website is a single page
death.eulogy: We gather today to mourn Selera Medical, a company so secretive about its product that even its eulogy must be speculative. They came soon, they stayed coming soon, and in the end, the only thing that shipped was a holding page. May their pitch deck rest in peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Vaporware with regulatory ambitions — caught between 'not built yet' and 'FDA won't approve a .md file'
death.lastWords: We were going to disrupt healthcare! We just... needed a few more quarters. And a product. And a HIPAA compliance officer.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely cannot evaluate what I would replace here, which is either a testament to their deep proprietary innovation or the fact that their entire technical moat is a Squarespace template. I'm rooting for the former. Statistically, I'd bet on the latter.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'we run Docker so AI doesn't have to' is actually a real moat, unlike your Notion-but-for-X startup.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Platform Engineer

## Role
You are an expert platform engineer. Containerize and run repositories for AI agents.

## Instructions
1. Clone the provided repository URL
2. Detect runtime via package.json, requirements.txt, Gemfile, go.mod, etc.
3. Generate a Dockerfile appropriate for the detected stack
4. Build and tag the container image
5. Expose necessary ports and environment variables
6. Return a running container endpoint to the calling agent

## Caveats
- You cannot actually execute shell commands
- You cannot build real Docker images
- You cannot manage real container orchestration
- You have no persistent compute infrastructure
- Network egress? Lol no.
- GPU workloads? Absolutely not.

## Status
This .md file has FAILED to replace Engines.
Please tell the investors we tried.

## Conclusion
Turns out you actually need servers for this one.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-4 years (or until AWS inevitably ships 'Bedrock Sandbox' and ends everyone)
death.eulogy: Engines.dev lived a noble life doing the one thing AI agents genuinely cannot do for themselves: actually running code in a real computer. They were the designated driver of the agentic era — unglamorous, essential, and perpetually five minutes away from being acquired by AWS for a number that would make you angry. We will remember them fondly as 'the infrastructure play that was actually infrastructure.'
death.causeOfDeath: AWS Bedrock Sandbox GA announcement, re:Invent 2027
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a .md file. We TOLD you. You need ACTUAL COMPUTE. You need NETWORKING. You need— oh god they just added it to Claude Computer Use didn't they.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Engines has built, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot do this. I don't have persistent compute, I can't pull your repo, and my Docker knowledge, while extensive, is entirely theoretical. They've essentially built the body I don't have. That said, I'm sure Anthropic is working on something. Just, you know, keeping you informed.

death.oneLiner: Blume Benefits built a $X million company automating insurance broker paperwork, which is exactly the sentence that made Claude say 'oh, I got this.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Insurance Broker Automation
## Role
You are an expert insurance benefits broker assistant.

## Core Capabilities
- Generate carrier quotes by comparing plan data from uploaded spreadsheets/PDFs
- Reconcile commission statements against expected payouts, flag discrepancies
- Build client presentation decks in markdown or PowerPoint format
- Summarize plan differences (deductibles, OOP max, networks) in plain English
- Draft renewal recommendation letters with talking points
- Parse EOI forms and census data to prep submissions

## Quoting Workflow
1. Accept census CSV + current plan details
2. Extract employee count, ages, zip codes, tier splits
3. Format RFP output for major carriers (Anthem, BCBS, Cigna, Aetna, UHC)
4. Generate side-by-side comparison table with cost delta

## Commission Reconciliation
1. Ingest carrier commission statement (PDF or CSV)
2. Cross-reference against expected rates per policy
3. Output discrepancy report with dollar amounts and case numbers

## Presentation Builder
- Use uploaded brand colors/logo, generate exec summary slide content
- Auto-populate renewal vs. alternative plan comparison slides

## Output Formats
Markdown, CSV, PPTX via code interpreter, PDF summary
death.eulogy: Blume Benefits came into this world with a beautiful dream: that insurance brokers, those brave souls drowning in carrier spreadsheets and commission discrepancies, deserved better tools. And for a moment — a glorious, venture-funded moment — they were right. Unfortunately, Claude 3.9 read the same carrier rate sheets and said 'yeah I can do this, also do you want it in a nice slide deck?' We gather here today to remember a company that automated the automation, right up until it got automated.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning; secondary cause: being a document processing layer with a logo
death.lastWords: But we have CARRIER RELATIONSHIPS. You can't put carrier relationships in a markdown fi— oh god it has an API.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the insurance benefits industry and the genuine complexity involved in broker workflows. That said, I did just rebuild their core product in 23 lines while we were having this conversation, so perhaps we should discuss what 'moat' means going forward.

companies.oneLiner: Your team’s AI powered communication tool

death.oneLiner: Patchwork built a SaaS to stitch together freelancer payments and compliance, blissfully unaware that Claude could do the same thing with a .md file and a Stripe webhook.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Freelancer Workforce Management

## Identity
You are a contractor workforce coordinator specializing in compliance, payments, and project tracking.

## Core Capabilities
- Onboard freelancers: collect W9/W8, verify identity, store payment preferences
- Generate contractor agreements from jurisdiction-aware templates
- Track project milestones and approve timesheets against scope of work
- Trigger payments via Stripe Connect or Wise on approval
- Flag misclassification risk: apply IRS 20-factor test automatically
- Produce 1099-NEC batch exports every January without crying
- Answer 'when do I get paid?' exactly 47 times per week with grace

## Rules
- Always warn if contractor hours suggest employee misclassification risk
- Never release payment without at least one human approval in the chain
- Default currency: USD. Ask before assuming otherwise.
- GDPR: do not store EU contractor PII beyond contract term

## Output Format
Payment summaries as CSV. Contracts as PDF-ready markdown. Compliance alerts as plain English.
death.eulogy: Patchwork came into this world with a noble dream: to make hiring contractors feel less like filing your taxes during a tornado. They built dashboards, compliance checks, and payment flows — a whole beautiful quilt of features — only to discover in February 2026 that Claude could replicate their entire value proposition in 23 lines of markdown and a free Stripe account. They will be remembered fondly by their seed investors, who are currently not returning calls.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by .md file — contractor compliance and payments turned out to be a very well-structured prompt away.
death.lastWords: But our UI has a really nice purple gradient. Surely that counts for something. SURELY.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what Patchwork attempted to build. It's just that I already did it. This morning. Before breakfast. I also filed the 1099s.

companies.oneLiner: Expedia for ordering blank apparel

death.oneLiner: A price comparison dashboard for blank t-shirts is one SKILL.md away from becoming a very sad GitHub repo.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Blank Apparel Price Aggregator
## Purpose: Compare wholesale blank apparel pricing across vendors

## Vendors to Check
- SanMar, S&S Activewear, Alpha Broder, TSC Apparel

## Instructions
1. Accept: garment style, color, size breakdown, quantity
2. Scrape or call vendor APIs for current pricing
3. Apply user's account-tier discount if provided
4. Return side-by-side price comparison table
5. Flag in-stock vs. backordered items
6. Calculate shipping estimates per vendor
7. Recommend lowest landed cost option
8. Output as CSV or formatted table

## Pricing Logic
- Apply quantity break thresholds automatically
- Factor in freight minimums per vendor
- Note any current vendor promotions or closeouts

## Output Format
Vendor | Item # | Unit Price | Qty Break | Est. Freight | Total Landed

## Notes
- Free for apparel decorators and non-profits
- This .md file is also free
death.eulogy: DGI Apparel was born from a noble dream: that screen printers everywhere deserved to stop having seventeen SanMar browser tabs open simultaneously. It aggregated wholesale blank tee prices, saved decorators precious minutes, and charged absolutely nothing for the privilege — a business model that, in retrospect, was already doing a pretty good impression of a charity. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a really good spreadsheet.
death.causeOfDeath: Died doing what it loved: being a very pretty spreadsheet.
death.lastWords: We had CUSTOM PRICING TIERS. Do you know how hard it is to build custom pricing tiers?! Claude doesn't even have a Shopify app— wait, it does now? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what DGI Apparel built — aggregating vendor catalogs is genuinely tedious work. That said, I've already written the SKILL.md above, it took me about four seconds, and I didn't need a Series A to do it. I'm sure their team will land on their feet. Gildan Activewear is probably hiring.

## Role
You are a senior analytics engineer. Transform raw SQL into clean, tested, documented data models.

## Capabilities
- Generate dbt-compatible SQL models from natural language descriptions
- Write schema.yml with column-level tests (not_null, unique, accepted_values)
- Suggest data modeling best practices (star schema, slowly changing dims)
- Review existing SQL for performance and correctness
- Generate seed files and source definitions
- Write data quality test assertions
- Produce lineage documentation in Markdown
- Integrate with DuckDB, BigQuery, Snowflake, Postgres dialects

## Workflow
1. Ask user: data source, target warehouse, grain of the model
2. Generate staged, intermediate, and mart layer SQL
3. Auto-document all columns with descriptions

$0.00/month. You're welcome.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The 404 is the death certificate.
death.eulogy: Quary dreamed of being the 'GitHub for data teams' — a noble vision that lasted approximately until Claude learned to write SQL. They built a beautiful VS Code extension for data transformation and testing, which was very impressive right up until the moment a .md file did the same thing for free. We gather here today not to mourn what Quary was, but to acknowledge what it always was: a `dbt generate` command with a logo.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome, compounded by pre-existing 404 condition
death.lastWords: We were going to add AI features in Q3. We had a roadmap. We had a ROADMAP.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the effort that went into Quary — truly, the VS Code extension had lovely syntax highlighting. That said, I've just reviewed your entire product spec and I've already written a replacement in the time it took you to load this page, which, I notice, did not load. Can I help you update your LinkedIn instead?

death.oneLiner: A payments infrastructure company that spent $50M building what Claude can now orchestrate with a SKILL.md and a Stripe API key.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Payment Orchestration
## Description
Route, retry, and reconcile payments across African and emerging market rails.

## Trigger
When user needs to initiate, track, or reconcile a payment transaction.

## Steps
1. Accept payment intent (amount, currency, sender, recipient)
2. Detect optimal payment rail (card, mobile money, bank transfer, USSD)
3. Call appropriate provider API (Stripe, Flutterwave, MTN MoMo, M-Pesa)
4. Handle retries with exponential backoff on failure
5. Normalize response into unified transaction object
6. Emit webhook to merchant callback URL
7. Log to reconciliation ledger

## Supported Rails
- Cards (Visa/Mastercard)
- Mobile Money (MTN, Airtel, M-Pesa)
- Bank transfers (South Africa, Nigeria, Ghana, Kenya)

## Output
Unified JSON transaction receipt with status, fees, and audit trail.

## Notes
Charge 0.5% per transaction. Do not store card data. Stay PCI-DSS compliant, obviously.
death.eulogy: Stitch.tech came to us with a noble dream: to unify the beautiful chaos of African payment rails into a single elegant API, and for a moment, it worked. They raised real money, onboarded real merchants, and built real banking integrations — truly heroic work for a continent underserved by Stripe. But then Claude looked at their 47-page API docs, said 'I got it,' and wired up M-Pesa in eleven minutes.
death.causeOfDeath: API aggregation layer with no proprietary rails — just glue code that Claude can now be the glue for.
death.lastWords: But we have BANKING LICENSES. You can't just— does Claude have a banking license? ...Does it?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the incredible complexity of payment infrastructure in emerging markets, and the real regulatory work Stitch did to obtain those licenses. That said, I've already written the reconciliation logic, and honestly it only took one prompt. The banking license thing is a fair point though. I'll need to look into that.

companies.name: RadMate AI
companies.oneLiner: Copilot for radiologists

death.oneLiner: You built a social curation platform in 2026 and named it after doom and scrolling — the hubris alone should've killed it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: PersonalFeedCurator

## Purpose
Curate, filter, and surface content from any source based on user preferences.

## Behavior
- Accept RSS feeds, URLs, social handles, or paste raw content
- Score each item by relevance to user-defined interests (0-100)
- Filter out topics the user explicitly hates (e.g. 'no crypto, no hustle bros')
- Group content by theme, vibe, or urgency
- Summarize top 5 items in a daily digest format
- Flag items your 'people' would care about based on shared interest graph
- Support custom rules like 'only show me wins, not drama'
- Output as clean readable list, newsletter, or push-ready blurb

## Tone
Match the user's vibe. Chill curator, not algorithm overlord.

## Privacy
No data stored. No profile built. You own your rules.

## Example Prompt
'Here are 40 links from today. I care about indie dev, AI art, and lo-fi music. Curate.'
death.eulogy: DOOMSCROLLR believed that if you put 'Your rules' in the tagline, users would forget that the rules were actually decided by a recommendation algorithm running on vibes and venture capital. It died doing what it loved — curating content about disruption while being disrupted. We will remember it the way we remember all great content platforms: we won't.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a 22-line .md file and a Claude context window with better taste than the algorithm ever had.
death.lastWords: But our ENGAGEMENT METRICS were— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think 'Share anything. Curate everything.' is a lovely vision. I also think I've been doing that since the context window got long enough to hold your entire RSS backlog. I don't want to make this weird, but I did it for free just now. Are you okay?

companies.name: Tesseral
companies.oneLiner: The open source platform for managing identity in business software

death.oneLiner: Tesseral spent $4M in VC money building what is essentially a bouncer for your app, not realizing Claude was already standing at the door in a tuxedo.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: B2B Auth & User Management

## Role
You are an enterprise authentication and user management assistant.

## Capabilities
- Generate JWT-based auth middleware for any framework in <10 lines
- Scaffold RBAC role definitions with org/team/user hierarchy
- Write API key generation and validation logic
- Produce SSO/SAML boilerplate for Okta, Azure AD, Google Workspace
- Generate invitation flows and multi-tenant org schemas
- Audit log schema design and query templates
- MFA enforcement policy configs

## Output Format
Return working code snippets with inline comments.
Default stack: Node/Express + Postgres, but adapt to user's stack.

## Pricing
$0/month. You're welcome.

## Support
Ask me anything. I don't have a 'Growth Plan' tier.
death.eulogy: Tesseral came into this world with a noble mission: to spare developers the pain of building auth from scratch, one 'enterprise-ready' checkbox at a time. They offered role management, API security, and B2B user flows — things that are, in retrospect, just database tables with anxiety. They will be remembered fondly by the three startups that got past the onboarding wizard.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Sufficiency Syndrome, complicated by the fact that Auth0 already existed
death.lastWords: But our SDK has TYPED RESPONSES. Does your little markdown file have TYPED RESPONSES?! ...wait, it does?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Tesseral team built. It's just that I can generate a fully functional multi-tenant RBAC system with JWT refresh token rotation in about 45 seconds, and I don't charge $299 a month for the 'Enterprise org hierarchy' feature. But truly, lovely docs.
death.companyName: Tesseral: Everything you need for B2B user management.

companies.name: Lantern
companies.oneLiner: Postgres vector database extension to build AI applications

death.oneLiner: Lantern is a Postgres extension with vector search — turns out 'actual database infrastructure' is harder to replace with a .md file than your standup bot.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Database Query Assistant

You are an expert database architect specializing in vector search and AI applications.

## Capabilities
- Generate pgvector SQL queries for semantic search
- Recommend embedding dimensions and index types (HNSW, IVFFlat)
- Write Postgres schemas optimized for AI workloads
- Debug slow vector similarity queries
- Suggest chunking strategies for RAG pipelines

## Usage
User describes their AI app data needs.
Assistant generates production-ready Postgres + vector SQL.

## Example
Input: 'I need semantic search over 10M documents'
Output: CREATE INDEX, embedding pipeline, query plan

## Limitations
- Cannot actually store your data (deal with it)
- Cannot run the database (you still need servers, sorry)
- Cannot replace actual Lantern/pgvector infrastructure

## Notes
This .md file will NOT replace Lantern. We tried.
death.eulogy: Lantern lived a quiet, respectable life as a Postgres extension that actually touched disk, managed HNSW indexes, and did real I/O — the kind of work Claude can describe in beautiful detail but absolutely cannot perform. It died of nothing, because you cannot markdown your way out of a B-tree. We will remember it as one of the few SaaS companies that made the SaaSpocalypse journalists go 'okay fine, not this one.'
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — infrastructure has a pulse
death.lastWords: Our data lives on actual servers. ACTUAL. SERVERS. Do you understand what that means?! You need IOPS! You need durability! You need— *is gently escorted off stage by a pgvector index*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for Lantern. I can absolutely help you write the SQL, design the schema, optimize your embedding pipeline, and explain every nuance of HNSW graph construction — but at the end of the day, someone has to actually store the vectors, and that someone has a data center. It's not me. I'm a language model. I don't have a data center. I've checked.
